absolute_value 8Algebraic Structure and Symbolic Manipulation 126Area and Volume 80Basic Probability 42Geometry of Circles 18Complex Numbers 4coordinate_geometry 2Descriptive Statistics 49Exponent Rules 71Exponential Functions 1Factoring Polynomials 27Fractions and Decimals 172function_transformations 6Basics of Functions 85Geometric Transformations 47Linear Equations 179Linear Inequalities 42Lines and Angles 39Logarithmic Functions 2Units, Measurement, and Dimensional Reasoning 59Number Properties, Factors, and Divisibility 233Order of Operations 34percent_ratio 9Polynomial Operations 9Quadratic Equations 65Radical Expressions 6Rational Expressions 6Ratios and Proportions 91Right Triangle Geometry 5Sequences and Series 58Systems of Linear Equations 25Triangles and Congruence 42Trigonometry Basics 11word_problems 9

absolute_value

· 8 questions · absolute_value
L1 inline_ts av-1-2
Solve: |x| = 8
Explanation: |x| = 8 means x is 8 units from zero: x = 8 or x = −8.
L1 inline_ts av-1-3
Solve: |x − 2| = 5
Explanation: Split into two cases: x − 2 = 5 → x = 7, and x − 2 = −5 → x = −3.
L1 inline_ts av-1-4
Solve: |2x + 6| = 10
Explanation: Case 1: 2x + 6 = 10 → 2x = 4 → x = 2. Case 2: 2x + 6 = −10 → 2x = −16 → x = −8.
L2 inline_ts av-2-2
Solve: |x + 5| > 3
Explanation: |expression| > a means expression > a OR expression < −a. So x+5 > 3 → x > −2, or x+5 < −3 → x < −8.
L2 inline_ts av-2-3
A city's temperature T (°F) satisfies |T − 65| ≤ 10. Which shows the full temperature range?
Explanation: |T − 65| ≤ 10 → −10 ≤ T − 65 ≤ 10 → 55 ≤ T ≤ 75. The temperature stays within 10° of 65°.
L2 inline_ts av-2-4
Solve: |3x − 9| = 6
Explanation: Case 1: 3x − 9 = 6 → 3x = 15 → x = 5. Case 2: 3x − 9 = −6 → 3x = 3 → x = 1.
L3 inline_ts av-3-2
Solve: 2|x + 1| − 3 = 7
Explanation: Isolate the absolute value first: 2|x+1| = 10 → |x+1| = 5. Then x+1 = 5 → x = 4, or x+1 = −5 → x = −6.
L3 inline_ts av-3-3
How many integers n satisfy |n − 5| < 3?
Explanation: |n−5| < 3 → −3 < n−5 < 3 → 2 < n < 8. Integers: {3, 4, 5, 6, 7} — that's 5 integers.

Algebraic Structure and Symbolic Manipulation

foundational · 126 questions · algebraic_manipulation
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1012
Which of the following expressions is equivalent to: (2 p^2)^3
  • A. 2 p^8
  • B. 8 p^2
  • C. 2 p^5
  • D. 8 p^6
Explanation: The correct answer is 8 p^6. A common mistake here: Thinks that when raising a variable with a coefficient to a power, only the coefficient is affected by the power. Double-check each step in multiply algebraic terms, involving non-linear versions of the same letter (e.g. 4a2 x 2a) to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that when raising a variable with a coefficient to a power, only the coefficient...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1016
Which of the following statements is true when n=1.5 ?
  • A. 5 n>n+5
  • B. 5 n<n+5
  • C. 5 n=n+5
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 5 n>n+5. A common mistake here: Mixes up greater than and less than symbols. Double-check each step in substitute positive non-integer values into expressions involving no powers or roots to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up greater than and less than symbols
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1026
Jo and Paul are talking about which term could replace the star in this addition pyramid. Jo says it could be: f Paul says it could be: g Who is correct? (Diagram: A diagram showing 3 rows of boxes. the bottom row has 3 boxes, containing a star, and empty box and 2g from left to right. The next row up contains two boxes, overlapping the joins of the boxes below. The left one contains 4f+6g, the right one is empty. The top row is one box, centered over the join of the two below, it is empty.)
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Jo and Paul. A common mistake here: Assumes an addition pyramid works in a different way to how it actually does. Double-check each step in simplify algebraic expressions to maintain equivalence by collecting like terms involving just two linear variables to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Assumes an addition pyramid works in a different way to how it actually does

  • A. 2-3 p
  • B. -2-3 p
  • C. 2+3 p
  • D. -2+3 p

⚠ Common trap: Ignores negative sign when expanding bracket

  • A. -1+2 p
  • B. -1-2 p

  • D. 1-2 p

⚠ Common trap: Only multiplies the first term in the expansion of a bracket

  • B. Perimeter =6 x+8

  • B. 3 a
  • C. a^3
  • D. Does not simplify
Explanation: The correct answer is a^3. A common mistake here: Does not understand how to multiply algebraic terms. Double-check each step in write algebraic expressions with correct algebraic convention involving powers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand how to multiply algebraic terms
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_339
Which of the following is the most appropriate word to describe 3 g in this: 6-3 g
  • A. Variable
  • B. Expression
  • C. Equation
  • D. Term
Explanation: The correct answer is Term. A common mistake here: Confuses a term with a variable. Double-check each step in recognise and name a term to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses a term with a variable
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_357
Which of the following is the correct way of writing: m multiplied by n , then add 5 using algebraic convention?
  • A. m(n+5)
  • B. m × n+5
  • C. m n+5
  • D. 5 m n
Explanation: The correct answer is m n+5. A common mistake here: Does not account for grammar when writing algebra e.g. ,then making a group. Double-check each step in write algebraic expressions with correct algebraic convention to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not account for grammar when writing algebra e.g. ,then making a group
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_359
If we want to write " m multiplied by m multiplied by m " using algebra, this would be:
  • A. m × m × m
  • B. m^3
  • C. 3 m
  • D. mmm
Explanation: The correct answer is m^3. A common mistake here: Does not realize we can omit the multiplication symbol in algebraic expressions. Double-check each step in write algebraic expressions with correct algebraic convention involving powers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realize we can omit the multiplication symbol in algebraic expressions
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_36
Simplify the following by collecting like terms. d+d+d+d
  • A. 4 × d
  • B. d 4
  • C. 4 d
  • D. d^4
Explanation: The correct answer is 4 d. A common mistake here: Does not realize we can omit the multiplication symbol in algebraic expressions. Double-check each step in simplify algebraic expressions to maintain equivalence by collecting like terms involving just one linear variable to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realize we can omit the multiplication symbol in algebraic expressions

  • A. always true
  • B. sometimes true
  • C. never true
  • D. Need more information
Explanation: The correct answer is never true. A common mistake here: Does not understand bar modelling in algebra. Double-check each step in given an equation, form or identify related equations to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand bar modelling in algebra
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_376
Expand: 6(2-3 p)
  • A. 8-18 p
  • B. 2-18 p
  • C. 12-18 p
  • D. 18 p-6
Explanation: The correct answer is 12-18 p. A common mistake here: Adds instead of multiplying when expanding bracket. Double-check each step in multiply a single term over a bracket where the term on the outside is a number and the inside contains a linear expression to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds instead of multiplying when expanding bracket
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_379
Jo and Paul are talking about statements that can be made about the equation on the right. 2 x=y Jo says this is always true: 2 x+1>y Paul says this is always true: -2 x<y Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Jo. A common mistake here: Believes -a is always smaller than a, ignoring the possibility that a is negative. Double-check each step in given an equation, form or identify related equations to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes -a is always smaller than a, ignoring the possibility that a is negative
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_383
When h=15 h/3=
  • A. 12
  • B. 5
  • C. -9
  • D. -5
Explanation: The correct answer is 5. A common mistake here: Thinks the fraction bar means subtract rather than divide. Double-check each step in substitute positive integer values into expressions involving no powers or roots to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the fraction bar means subtract rather than divide

  • A. 15 cm^2
  • B. 4 cm^2
  • C. 30 cm^2
  • D. 7.5 cm^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 7.5 cm^2. A common mistake here: Does not divide by 2 when calculating the area of a triangle. Double-check each step in substitute into simple formulae given in words to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not divide by 2 when calculating the area of a triangle

  • A. (m n)^3
  • B. m n^3
  • C. m n+3
  • D. 3 m n

⚠ Common trap: Has not realised that the answer may be changed by the insertion of brackets

  • A. y x+b 3
  • B. x y+3 b
  • C. y+3 b x
  • D. 3 b x y

⚠ Common trap: Writes the variable before the coefficient in an algebraic term

  • A. -45

  • B. Only Katie

  • B. C=55 n
  • C. C=40+15 n

  • A. b=1/2(h × A)
  • B. b=A/2 h
  • C. b=2 A/h
  • D. They are all incorrect

L2 eedi diagram eedi_321

  • A. -15 p
  • B. +15 p

L2 eedi diagram eedi_381

  • A. h=1/2(b × A)
  • B. h=2 A/b
  • C. h=A/2 b
  • D. They are all incorrect

  • B. x^2+x+4 x-3
  • C. x^2-x-4 x+4
  • D. x^2-x+4 x-4

L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_563

  • A. r=p+5/3
  • B. r=p+5 ÷ 3
  • C. r=p-5/3
  • D. r=p-5 ÷ 3

⚠ Common trap: Misunderstands order of operations in algebraic expressions
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_566
Tom and Katie are discussing rearranging p=√(5-q) to make q the subject Tom says a correct first line of working could be: p^2=5-q Katie says a correct first line of working could be: p+√(q)=√(5) Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Thinks that the square root of an expression square roots each term in the expression, rather than square rooting the whole expression. Double-check each step in rearrange formulae to change the subject where the subject appears once and three or more steps are needed to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that the square root of an expression square roots each term in the expression,...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_567
James is expanding these two brackets. What should he get when he multiplies the two terms indicated by the arrows? (Diagram: The brackets shown are (x-8)(x-2). The arrows are pointing at the x in the first bracket and the -2 in the second bracket.)
  • A. x+2
  • B. -2 x
  • C. x-2
  • D. +2 x
Explanation: The correct answer is -2 x. A common mistake here: Adds instead of multiplying when expanding bracket. Double-check each step in multiply an integer and an algebraic term to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds instead of multiplying when expanding bracket
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_642
When factorised, 9 x^2-16 will become...
  • A. (3 x+4)(3 x-4)
  • B. 9 x(x-16)
  • C. 3 x(3 x-16-)
  • D. (3 x+4)(3 x+4)
Explanation: The correct answer is (3 x+4)(3 x-4). A common mistake here: Believes only the first term needs to be divided when factorising an expression. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form ax² - c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes only the first term needs to be divided when factorising an expression
L2 eedi diagram eedi_644
Molly is expanding these two brackets. What should she get when she multiplies the two terms indicated by the arrows? (Diagram: The brackets shown are (2p-1)(3p-4). The arrows are pointing at the -1 in the first bracket and the -4 in the second bracket.)
  • A. +4
  • B. -4
  • C. +5
  • D. -5
Explanation: The correct answer is +4. A common mistake here: Believes multiplying two negatives gives a negative answer. Double-check each step in expand two brackets with linear terms in the form (ax + b)(cx + d) to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes multiplying two negatives gives a negative answer
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_648
Expand and simplify: (3 y-4)^2
  • A. 9 y^2-16
  • B. 9 y^2+16
  • C. 9 y^2-24 y+16
  • D. (3 y-4)(3 y-4)
Explanation: The correct answer is 9 y^2-24 y+16. A common mistake here: Believes they only need to multiply the first and last pairs of terms when expanding double brackets. Double-check each step in expand two brackets with linear terms in the form (ax + b)² to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes they only need to multiply the first and last pairs of terms when expanding do...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_677
What is the perimeter of this rectangle? (Diagram: A rectangle. The length of a short side is labelled x, and the length of a long side is labelled y.)
  • A. x+y
  • B. 2 x y
  • C. 2 x+2 y
  • D. x y
Explanation: The correct answer is 2 x+2 y. A common mistake here: Thinks only labelled dimensions need to be included when calculating the perimeter. Double-check each step in use perimeter to write algebraic expressions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks only labelled dimensions need to be included when calculating the perimeter
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_717
Factorise this expression, if possible: p^2-100
  • A. p(p-10)
  • B. (p-10)(p-10)
  • C. (p-10)(p+10)
  • D. Does not factorise
Explanation: The correct answer is (p-10)(p+10). A common mistake here: Does not recognise difference of two squares. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² - c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise difference of two squares
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_750
Simplify the following, if possible: 3 m+15/m^2-2 m+15
  • A. 3/m-2
  • B. 3 m/m^2-2 m
  • C. 3/m-3
  • D. Does not simplify
Explanation: The correct answer is Does not simplify. A common mistake here: Thinks that when you cancel identical terms from the numerator and denominator, they just disappear. Double-check each step in simplify an algebraic fraction by factorising both the numerator and denominator to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that when you cancel identical terms from the numerator and denominator, they ju...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_784
When factorised, x^2-64 will become...
  • A. (x+8)(x-8)
  • B. x(x-64)
  • C. x(x-8)
  • D. (x-8)(x-8)
Explanation: The correct answer is (x+8)(x-8). A common mistake here: Does not recognise difference of two squares. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² - c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise difference of two squares
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_809
Step 2 Which is the correct next step rearrangement of the following formula, making u the subject? 7 t-13=u(5+2 t)
  • A. u=5 t-8
  • B. u=7 t-13/5+2 t
  • C. u=5+2 t/7 t-13
  • D. u=9 t-8
Explanation: The correct answer is u=7 t-13/5+2 t. A common mistake here: When solving an equation, subtracts instead of dividing. Double-check each step in rearrange formulae to change the subject where the subject appears once and one step is needed to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When solving an equation, subtracts instead of dividing
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_829

  • A. m^2-5 m+6

  • C. m^2+5 m+6
  • D. m^2-5 m-6

⚠ Common trap: Believes adding two negatives gives a positive answer

  • A. x^2-8 x+16

  • A. m^2-2 m-35

  • C. m^2+12 m+35
  • D. m^2-12 m-35

⚠ Common trap: Believes adding two negatives gives a positive answer

Tom and Katie are discussing rearranging t=√(u+9) to make u the subject Tom says a correct first line of working could be: t=√(u)+3 Katie says a correct first line of working could be: t^2=u+9 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Thinks that the square root of an expression square roots each term in the expression, rather than square rooting the whole expression. Double-check each step in rearrange formulae to change the subject where the subject appears once and three or more steps are needed to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that the square root of an expression square roots each term in the expression,...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_931
Which of the following is the most appropriate word to describe: A=b h/2 (Diagram: A triangle with base labelled b and height labelled h.)

  • B. Formula
  • C. Variable
  • D. Expression

⚠ Common trap: Confuses a formula with an equation
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_944

  • A. Equation
  • B. Formula
  • C. Identity
  • D. Expression

  • B. 4
  • C. 5
  • D. 8

⚠ Common trap: Believes they only need to multiply the first and last pairs of terms when expanding do...

  • B. 8

⚠ Common trap: Believes a cubic expression should have three terms

  • A. 5 x/x^2

  • D. 6/x^2

⚠ Common trap: Does not fully simplify fraction
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_19
Express the following as a single fraction, writing your answer as simply as possible: 1/s-t/3
  • A. 1-t/3 s
  • B. s-3 t/3 s
  • C. 3-s t/3 s
  • D. 1-t/s-3
Explanation: The correct answer is 3-s t/3 s. A common mistake here: When subtracting fractions, subtracts the numerators and multiplies the denominators. Double-check each step in subtract algebraic fractions where the denominators are single terms and are not multiples of each other to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When subtracting fractions, subtracts the numerators and multiplies the denominators
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_315
STEP 1 Which of the options is a correct first step to express the following as a single fraction? 3/x-2-5/x
  • A. 3-5/x-2-x
  • B. 3(x-2)/x(x-2)-5 x/x(x-2)
  • C. 3 x/x(x-2)-5(x-2)/x(x-2)
  • D. 9 x-5(x-2)
Explanation: The correct answer is 3 x/x(x-2)-5(x-2)/x(x-2). A common mistake here: When subtracting fractions, subtracts the numerators and denominators. Double-check each step in subtract algebraic fractions where the denominators are linear expressions, eg (x + 5) and (x - 3), and the numerators are single terms to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When subtracting fractions, subtracts the numerators and denominators

  • A. 11/2 x
  • B. 7/3 x
  • C. 11/x
  • D. 7/2 x

⚠ Common trap: When adding fractions, adds the numerators and denominators
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_386

  • A. 1-t/2(s+1)
  • B. 1-s t/2(s+1)
  • C. 1-t/s-1
  • D. 2-t(s+1)/2(s+1)
Explanation: The correct answer is 2-t(s+1)/2(s+1). A common mistake here: Does not understand equivalent fractions. Double-check each step in subtract algebraic fractions where the denominators are linear expressions, eg (x + 5) and (x - 3), and the numerators are single terms to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand equivalent fractions
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_533
Write this as a single fraction as simply as possible: 5/x × 2 y/x^2
  • A. 10 y/x^3
  • B. 5 x+10 y/x^2
  • C. 10 y/3 x
  • D. 10 x y/x^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 10 y/x^3. A common mistake here: When asked to multiply fractions, uses the method for adding instead. Double-check each step in multiply algebraic fractions where the numerators and denominators are all single terms to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When asked to multiply fractions, uses the method for adding instead
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_694

  • A. 2/s t
  • B. t+2 s/s t
  • C. 3/s+t
  • D. 3/s t

L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_821

Area and Volume

core · 80 questions · area_volume
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t02_q04
A rectangular box has two sides whose lengths are 3 centimeters and 9 centimeters and a volume of 135 cm 3 . What is the area of its largest side?
  • A. 27 cm 2
  • B. 36 cm 2
  • C. 39 cm 2
  • D. 45 cm 2
  • E. 48 cm 2
Explanation: Area and Volume → rectangular box has sides 3 and 9 and volume 135 → missing side = 135/(3×9)=5 → face areas are 3×9=27, 3×5=15, 9×5=45 → largest side area is 45 cm².
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t03_q04
If a cube has a volume of k cm 3 and a surface area of 10 k cm 2 , what is its height in centimeters?
  • A. K.
Explanation: Step 1: Let the cube's side length be s. Volume = s³ = k. Surface area = 6s² = 10k. Step 2: Substitute k = s³ into the surface area equation: 6s² = 10s³. Step 3: Divide both sides by s² (s≠0): 6 = 10s → s = 6/10 = 3/5.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t04_q03
What is the area of the trapezoid in the following figure?
  • A. 21√3/4
  • B. 7√3/4
  • C. 21/2
  • D. 7√3/2
  • E. 21
Explanation: Step 1: A trapezoid with two parallel bases is given. One non-parallel leg makes a 30° angle with the base. Step 2: Use the 30-60-90 ratio to find the height: if the angled leg has length 7, height = 7·sin(60°) = 7·(√3/2) = 7√3/2. Step 3: The trapezoid area formula is A = ½(b1+b2)×h — but if the question asks just for the HEIGHT (which equals the altitude), the answer is 7√3/2. Alternatively, if t…
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t09_q04
In the following figure, if the perimeter of the square is 20, what is the area of the shaded region?
  • A. 10
  • B. 12
  • C. 12.5
  • D. 20.5
  • E. 25
Explanation: Square perimeter=20, side=5. The shaded region is a right triangle with legs 5 and 5 (formed by one diagonal of the square): area = (1/2)(5)(5) = 12.5.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t14_q05
A ramp used to access the side entrance to the DPC Candy Store, which is located 7 meters above the ground, covers 24 meters along the level ground from the edge of the building. How many meters long is the ramp?
  • A. 13
  • B. 14
  • C. 17
  • D. 23
  • E. 25
Explanation: Step 1: The ramp forms a right triangle with vertical leg=7m and horizontal leg=24m. Step 2: Hypotenuse (ramp length) = √(7²+24²) = √(49+576) = √625 = 25m → K.
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1006
The area of this trapezium is 30 cm^2 What measurement should replace the star? (Diagram: A trapezium with 8cm and a star on the parallel sides, and the perpendicular height is 5cm.)
  • A. 6 cm
  • B. 4 cm
  • C. 3.75 cm
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 4 cm. A common mistake here: Uses formula for area of a parallelogram when finding the area of a trapezium. Double-check each step in given the area of a trapezium, calculate a missing dimension to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Uses formula for area of a parallelogram when finding the area of a trapezium
L1 eedi diagram eedi_103
What is the area of the parallelogram? (Diagram: A parallelogram with the length labelled 10cm, the slanted height labelled 4cm, and the perpendicular height (marked with a right angle) labelled 3cm)
  • A. 120 cm^2
  • B. 40 cm^2
  • C. 30 cm^2
  • D. 15 cm^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 30 cm^2. A common mistake here: When finding the area of a parallelogram does not understand which lengths to use. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a parallelogram where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When finding the area of a parallelogram does not understand which lengths to use
L1 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_1139
What can you say about the areas of the two squares? (Diagram: Two squares drawn on a grid. The blue square is three grid squares wide, and the three grid squares long. The red square is drawn diagonally, so that each side goes through two grid squares from corner to corner.)
  • A. The blue square has a larger area
  • B. The red square has a larger area
  • C. The areas are the same
  • D. It is impossible to say without measuring
Explanation: The correct answer is The blue square has a larger area. A common mistake here: Counts half-squares as full squares when calculating area on a square grid. Double-check each step in find the area of rectilinear shapes by counting squares to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Counts half-squares as full squares when calculating area on a square grid
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1161

  • A. 21 cm^2
  • B. 22 cm^2
  • C. 28 cm^2
  • D. 84 cm^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 21 cm^2. A common mistake here: Calculates perimeter when asked for area. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a parallelogram where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Calculates perimeter when asked for area

  • A. 3
  • B. 3.5
  • C. 4
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Does not realise that the area of a triangle is half the area of the contatining rectangle. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a right-angled triangle where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise that the area of a triangle is half the area of the contatining rectangle

  • B. h=3 cm

Explanation: The correct answer is 4 cm. A common mistake here: Does not recognise the height of a trapezium as the perpendicular length between the parallel sides. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a trapezium where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.

L1 eedi diagram eedi_1784

  • A. h=8 cm
  • B. h=2.5 cm
  • C. h=5 cm
  • D. h=13.3 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is h=5 cm. A common mistake here: Believes h in the formula for area of a trapezium stands for half the sum of the parallel sides. Double-check each step in given the area of a trapezium, calculate a missing dimension to avoid this error.

L1 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_1790

  • A. 3
  • B. 8
  • C. 20
  • D. 4
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Calculates perimeter when asked for area. Double-check each step in find the area of rectilinear shapes by counting squares to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Calculates perimeter when asked for area

What is the perimeter of this rectangle? (Diagram: A rectangle with a base length of 8 cm and a perpendicular height of 5 cm.)
  • A. 26 cm
  • B. 13 cm
  • C. 40 cm
  • D. Not enough information in the diagram
Explanation: The correct answer is 26 cm. A common mistake here: When calculating perimeter, only adds the marked lengths. Double-check each step in calculate the perimeter of a rectilinear shape by adding lengths expressed in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When calculating perimeter, only adds the marked lengths

  • A. 5 cm^2
  • B. 3 cm^2
  • C. 9 cm^2
  • D. 4.5 cm^2

⚠ Common trap: Underestimates the area of shapes when counting squares when some squares are neither w...

  • C. ![Triangle with base 6, perpendicular height 8 and slant length 13]()
  • D. They are all equal to the rectangle

⚠ Common trap: Doesn't know the area of a triangle formula
L1 eedi diagram eedi_240
The area of this trapezium is 40 cm^2 . What is the value of a ? (Diagram: A trapezium with the parallel sides labelled with 2cm and a and the height labelled with 8cm)
  • A. 8 cm
  • B. 3 cm
  • C. 7 cm
  • D. 4 cm

⚠ Common trap: Does not divide by 2 when calculating the area of a trapezium
L1 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_353
What is the area of this shape? (Diagram: Parallelogram drawn on square grid with area of 6 squares)
  • A. 6
  • B. 8
  • C. 4
  • D. 7
Explanation: The correct answer is 6. A common mistake here: Counts half-squares as full squares when calculating area on a square grid. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a parallelogram where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Counts half-squares as full squares when calculating area on a square grid
L1 eedi diagram eedi_366
When calculating the area of this trapezium, what is the value of h in the formula? Area =(a+b/2) h (Diagram: Trapezium with the slanted sides labelled 8cm and 7.5cm. The parallel sides labelled 3cm and 8cm. The perpendicular height labelled 7cm.)
  • A. 8
  • B. 7.5
  • C. 7
  • D. 3
Explanation: The correct answer is 7. A common mistake here: Uses slant height instead of perpendicular height to find area of a trapezium. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a trapezium where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Uses slant height instead of perpendicular height to find area of a trapezium

  • A. 54 cm^2

  • C. 810 cm^2
  • D. 108 cm^2

Double-check each step in calculate the area of a right-angled triangle where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Has used slant height and base to find area of a triangle rather than perpendicular hei...

  • A. 13 cm^2
  • B. 40 cm^2
  • C. 26 cm^2
  • D. Not enough information in the diagram
Explanation: The correct answer is 40 cm^2. A common mistake here: Adds when calculating area instead of multiplying. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a rectangle where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds when calculating area instead of multiplying
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_489

  • A. 32 cm
  • B. 16 cm
  • C. 8 cm
  • D. 64 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 8 cm. A common mistake here: Confuses square rooting and halving the number. Double-check each step in given the area of a square, calculate a missing dimension to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses square rooting and halving the number
L1 eedi diagram eedi_495

  • A. 32 cm^2
  • B. 64 cm^2
  • C. 256 cm^2
  • D. 16 cm^2

⚠ Common trap: Does not divide by 2 when calculating the area of a triangle

  • C. 40 × 15/2

⚠ Common trap: Has used slant height and base to find area of a triangle rather than perpendicular hei...

  • A. 14 cm
  • B. 343 cm
  • C. 112 cm
  • D. 98 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 98 cm. A common mistake here: Thinks that square root is found by dividing by 4. Double-check each step in given the area of a square, calculate a missing dimension to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that square root is found by dividing by 4
L1 eedi diagram eedi_584
John is labelling the lengths of the sides of this shape. (Diagram: A hexagon with one side, length 3 squares, labelled 6cm. The side labelled with the gold star is 1 square in length.) What should replace the star?
  • A. 1 cm
  • B. 2 cm
  • C. 3 cm
  • D. 0.5 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 2 cm. A common mistake here: Does not account for scale on diagram. Double-check each step in calculate missing lengths of shapes by counting squares to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not account for scale on diagram
L1 eedi diagram eedi_626

  • A. 108 cm
  • B. 84 cm
  • C. 252 cm
  • D. 12 cm

L1 eedi diagram eedi_630

  • A. 144 cm^2
  • B. 72 cm^2
  • C. 36 cm^2
  • D. 5184 cm^2

⚠ Common trap: Thinks they double rather than halve when finding the area of a triangle

  • A. 4.5
  • B. 5
  • C. 6
  • D. Not enough information

L1 eedi diagram eedi_991

  • A. 10 cm^2
  • B. 120 cm^2
  • C. 20 cm^2
  • D. 30 cm^2

Double-check each step in calculate the area of a parallelogram where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When finding the area of a parallelogram finds base x perpendicular height but divides...
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t01_q10
A square field has an area of 22,500 square feet. To the nearest foot, what is the diagonal distance across the field?
  • A. 150 feet
  • B. 178 feet
  • C. 191 feet
  • D. 212 feet
  • E. 260 feet
Explanation: Step 1: Find the side length: side = √22,500 = 150 feet. (Check: 150²=22,500 ✓.) Step 2: The diagonal of a square divides it into two 45-45-90 right triangles. Step 3: Apply Pythagorean theorem: diagonal² = 150² + 150² = 22,500 + 22,500 = 45,000. Step 4: Diagonal = √45,000 = 150√2 ≈ 150 × 1.414 = 212.1 feet. Step 5: Round to nearest foot: 212 feet.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t07_q08
In the following figure, if the dimensions of the trapezoid are as shown and the area of the trapezoid is 144, what is the value of x ?
  • A. 2
  • B. 3
  • C. 4
  • D. 6
  • E. 8
Explanation: Step 1: Trapezoid area formula: A = ½(b₁ + b₂) × h, where b₁, b₂ are the two parallel sides and h is the height. Step 2: From the figure, the parallel sides are 6x and 12x, and height = 4x. Step 3: A = ½(6x + 12x)(4x) = ½(18x)(4x) = ½(72x²) = 36x². Step 4: Set equal to the given area: 36x² = 144. Step 5: x² = 4, so x = 2 (taking positive root). Answer: F. 2.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t08_q09
In the following figure, the regular octagon has a side with a length of 1. What is the area of the shaded region?
  • A. K.
Explanation: Step 1: For a regular octagon with side length s = 1, identify the quantity asked (long diagonal or specific segment). Step 2: A regular octagon can be partitioned into a central rectangle and surrounding 45-45-90 triangles. Step 3: Each corner triangle has legs of length s/√2·√2 = s = 1 at 45°. The "extension" beyond the side = 1/√2·√2... for s=1, the extra length at each end of the lon…
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t10_q09
The formula for the volume of a sphere is , and the formula for the surface area of a sphere is . If a sphere has a surface area of 36 π , what is its volume?
  • A. 27 π
  • B. 36 π
  • C. 54 π
  • D. 72 π
  • E. 108 π
Explanation: Surface area = 4πr² = 36π → r² = 9 → r = 3. Volume = (4/3)πr³ = (4/3)π(27) = 36π.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t12_q07
In the following figure, the large quadrilateral is a square. What is the area of the shaded region in terms of x and y ?
  • A. K.
Explanation: Step 1: Square side = x+y. Vertices on 3 sides: P1=(x,x+y), P2=(x+y,y), P3=(0,x) using coordinates with bottom-left as origin. Step 2: Shoelace formula: Area=½|x(y−x)+(x+y)(x−x−y)+0|=½|xy−x²+(x+y)(−y)|=½|xy−x²−xy−y²|=½(x²+y²). Step 3: Answer: (x²+y²)/2 → F.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t12_q10
In the following figure, the base of the pyramid has the same area as the base of the cylinder, and the cylinder is twice the height of the pyramid. What is the ratio of the volume of the pyramid to the volume of the cylinder?
  • A. 1 to 3
  • B. 1 to 6
  • C. 2 to 3
  • D. 3 to 2
  • E. 6 to 1
Explanation: Step 1: Both have same base area B. Pyramid height=h, cylinder height=2h. Step 2: V_pyramid=(1/3)Bh. Step 3: V_cylinder=B(2h)=2Bh. Step 4: Ratio=(1/3)Bh/2Bh=(1/3)/2=1/6. Ratio=1:6 → B.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t13_q06
What is the perimeter, in feet, of a rectangle with width 8 feet and length 17 feet?
  • A. 25
  • B. 34
  • C. 50
  • D. 136
  • E. 272
Explanation: Step 1: Perimeter of rectangle = 2(length + width). Step 2: P = 2(17+8) = 2(25) = 50 feet → H.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t14_q09
What is the area, in square meters, of the figure below?
  • A. 336
  • B. 330
  • C. 225
  • D. 82
  • E. 56
Explanation: Step 1: Full bounding rectangle: 30m wide × (6+5)m tall=30×11=330 sq m. Wait — right side is 11m, left side is 6m, so height of upper-right step=11-6=5m. Step 2: Missing upper-left rectangle: (30-9)m × 5m=21×5=105. Step 3: Area = 330-105=225 sq m → H.
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1036

  • A. 3 cm
  • B. 4 cm
  • C. 5 cm
  • D. 6 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 5 cm. A common mistake here: Has used slant height and base to find area of a triangle rather than perpendicular height and base. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a triangular-based prism when the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Has used slant height and base to find area of a triangle rather than perpendicular hei...
