Each of these measurements matches one of these objects. (Diagram: An image of 4 objects and 4 measurements. The objects are an egg cup, a cereal box, a chest of drawers and a piggy bank. And, the measurements are 87 cm^3, 1013 cm^3, 4172 cm^3 and 197,177 cm^3.) Which measurement most likely matches the egg cup?
  • A. 87 cm^3
  • B. 1013 cm^3
  • C. 4172 cm^3
  • D. 197177 cm^3
Explanation: The correct answer is 87 cm^3. A common mistake here: Cannot estimate the relative volume order, for different objects. Double-check each step in estimate the volume or capacity of an object to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Cannot estimate the relative volume order, for different objects
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_150
Which of the following is not a measure of volume?
  • A. Cubic meters
  • B. Centimetres cubed
  • C. Litres
  • D. Metres
Explanation: The correct answer is Metres. A common mistake here: Does not know that cubic means the same as cubed in units. Double-check each step in identify a unit of volume or capacity to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know that cubic means the same as cubed in units
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1543
What is the perimeter of this composite shape made from rectangles? (Diagram: A composite shape made from three rectangles that form upside down step structure. The base of the shape is horizontal and is 12m long. The vertical height of the whole shape is 27m. The horizontal width of the top part of the shape is 32m. The horizontal width of the top step is 15m. The vertical height of the top rectangle is 9m. The vertical height of the bottom rectangle is 10m.)
  • A. 118 m
  • B. 121 m
  • C. 130 m
  • D. 105 m
Explanation: The correct answer is 118 m. A common mistake here: Does not know how to find missing lengths in a composite shape. Double-check each step in calculate the perimeter of a rectilinear shape by adding lengths expressed in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to find missing lengths in a composite shape
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1581
What is the volume of this cube? (Diagram: A cube with one side labelled 5 cm.)
  • A. 120 cm^3
  • B. 100 cm^3
  • C. 125 cm^3
  • D. 150 cm^3
Explanation: The correct answer is 125 cm^3. A common mistake here: Calculates perimeter when asked for area. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a cube to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Calculates perimeter when asked for area
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1626
What is the area of the face marked F (Diagram: A cylinder with height 9cm and radius 5cm. An arrow points to the circular face at the bottom of the cylinder.)
  • A. 10 π\ cm^2
  • B. 25 π\ cm^2
  • C. 45 π\ cm^2
  • D. 100 π\ cm^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 25 π\ cm^2. A common mistake here: Calculates circumference when asked for area. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a cylinder when the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Calculates circumference when asked for area
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1670
Amy wants to calculate the volume of this shape. Which face should she work out the area of to help her? (Diagram: The diagram shows a right angled triangular prism with arrows pointing to one of the right angled triangle faces and the three rectangular faces.)
  • A. ![A points to 'the slope' which is the rectangular face coming off the hypotenuse of the right angled triangle]()
  • B. ![B points to 'the front' which is the right angled triangle face]()
  • C. ![C points to 'the back' which is the rectangular face coming off the short vertical side of the right angled triangle]()
  • D. ![D points to 'the base' which is the rectangular face coming off the long horizontal side of the right angled triangle]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![B points to 'the front' which is the right angled triangle face](). A common mistake here: Think cross section of a prism is the biggest face. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a triangular-based prism when the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Think cross section of a prism is the biggest face
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1862
Paige, Kaci and David want to find the area of the school playground. They each do it a different way. Who is correct? (Diagram: A compound shape made up of two rectangles. Dimensions are 6 meters by 12 meters and also 4 meters by 8 meters. Two sides do not have measurements so need to be deduced)
  • A. Paige: 12 × 6+8 × 4
  • B. Kaci: 10 × 6+4 × 6
  • C. David: 12 × 10-6 × 4
  • D. They are all correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Paige: 12 × 6+8 × 4. A common mistake here: Cannot identify the correct side lengths to use when asked to find the area of a face. Double-check each step in calculate compound area involving just rectangles and squares, where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Cannot identify the correct side lengths to use when asked to find the area of a face
L2 eedi diagram eedi_20
The volume of this cuboid is 30 cm^3 What length should replace the star? (Diagram: A cuboid, depth 2cm and height 3cm. The width is labelled with a star.)
  • A. 6 cm
  • B. 5 cm
  • C. 4 cm
  • D. 25 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 5 cm. A common mistake here: Adds when calculating volume instead of multiplying. Double-check each step in given the volume of a cuboid, work out missing information to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds when calculating volume instead of multiplying
L2 eedi diagram eedi_236
Which of the following calculations would give the volume of this cuboid? (Diagram: A cuboid with dimensions 60mm, 7cm and 5cm)
  • A. 7 × 5 × 60
  • B. 7 × 5 × 6
  • C. 7+5+60
  • D. 7+5+6
Explanation: The correct answer is 7 × 5 × 6. A common mistake here: Does not convert measurements to have the same units before calculating area or volume. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a cuboid when the dimensions are given in different units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not convert measurements to have the same units before calculating area or volume
L2 eedi diagram eedi_271
Tom and Katie are arguing about prisms. Tom says this shape is a prism. Katie says this shape is a prism. (Diagram: A square based pyramid for Tom. A cone for Katie.) Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Confuses triangular prism with pyramid. Double-check each step in identify a prism from a diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses triangular prism with pyramid
L2 eedi diagram eedi_303
Which of the following calculations would give you the total surface area of this cuboid in cm^2 ? (Diagram: A cuboid. The height is 4cm, the depth is 3cm and the width is 6cm.)
  • A. (6 × 4)+(6 × 3)+(4 × 3)
  • B. 6 × 4 × 3
  • C. (6 × 4) ×(6 × 3) ×(4 × 3)
  • D. [(6 × 4)+(6 × 3)+(4 × 3)] × 2
Explanation: The correct answer is [(6 × 4)+(6 × 3)+(4 × 3)] × 2. A common mistake here: When calculating surface area, only includes visible faces. Double-check each step in calculate the surface area of a cuboid where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When calculating surface area, only includes visible faces
L2 eedi diagram eedi_320
What is the area of this compound shape made with rectangles? (Diagram: Compound shape made of two rectangles with the sides labelled 10 cm, 7 cm, 3 cm and 4 cm. Two sides are unlabelled)
  • A. 840 cm^2
  • B. 52 cm^2
  • C. 42 cm^2
  • D. 51 cm^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 42 cm^2. A common mistake here: Multiplies all given dimensions when calculating an area. Double-check each step in calculate compound area involving just rectangles and squares, where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Multiplies all given dimensions when calculating an area
L2 eedi diagram eedi_394
What is the volume of this cuboid? (Diagram: A cuboid with dimensions labelled 10cm, 4cm and 5cm)
  • A. 19 cm^3
  • B. 50 cm^3
  • C. 200 cm^3
  • D. 220 cm^3
Explanation: The correct answer is 200 cm^3. A common mistake here: Adds when calculating volume instead of multiplying. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a cuboid when the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds when calculating volume instead of multiplying
L2 eedi diagram eedi_441
Which of the following calculations would give you the total surface area of this cuboid in cm^2 ? (Diagram: A cuboid. The top measures 4cm by 2cm, and the height is 7cm.)
  • A. (2 × 7)+(4 × 2)+(4 × 7)
  • B. [(2 × 7)+(4 × 2)+(4 × 7)] × 2
  • C. (2 × 7) ×(4 × 2) ×(4 × 7)
  • D. 2 × 4 × 7
Explanation: The correct answer is [(2 × 7)+(4 × 2)+(4 × 7)] × 2. A common mistake here: When calculating surface area, only includes visible faces. Double-check each step in calculate the surface area of a cuboid where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When calculating surface area, only includes visible faces
L2 eedi diagram eedi_591
Which calculation would give you the area of the face of this cuboid labelled Q ('the top')? (Diagram: A cuboid with dimensions 6cm by 3cm by 4 cm. The top face Q is marked. It is a rectangle with dimensions 6cm by 3cm.)
  • A. 6 cm × 3 cm
  • B. 4 cm × 3 cm
  • C. 6 cm × 4 cm
  • D. 6 cm × 4 cm × 3 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 6 cm × 3 cm. A common mistake here: Cannot identify the correct side lengths to use when asked to find the area of a face. Double-check each step in calculate the surface area of a cuboid where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Cannot identify the correct side lengths to use when asked to find the area of a face
L2 eedi diagram eedi_726
What is the area of this compound shape made with rectangles? (Diagram: Compound shape made of two rectangles with the sides labelled 15 cm, 12 cm, 7 cm and 7 cm. Two sides are unlabelled)
  • A. 124 cm^2
  • B. 154 cm^2
  • C. 180 cm^2
  • D. 189 cm^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 124 cm^2. A common mistake here: Makes an assumption about line segments being equal within a shape. Double-check each step in calculate compound area involving just rectangles and squares, where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Makes an assumption about line segments being equal within a shape
L2 eedi diagram eedi_832
What is the volume of this cube? (Diagram: A cube with one edge labelled 5cm)
  • A. 125 cm^3
  • B. 15 cm^3
  • C. 25 cm^3
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 125 cm^3. A common mistake here: Adds when calculating volume instead of multiplying. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a cube to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds when calculating volume instead of multiplying
L2 eedi diagram eedi_871
Paige, Kaci and David want to find the area of the school playground. They each do it a different way. Who is correct? (Diagram: A compound shape making an L shape. The full width across the bottom is 9m, the partial width on the top is 5m. The full height on the left is 10m, the partial height on the right is 6m.)
  • A. Paige: 10 × 6+5 × 4
  • B. Kaci: 10 × 5+9 × 6
  • C. David: 9 × 10-6 × 4
  • D. They are all correct
Explanation: The correct answer is David: 9 × 10-6 × 4. A common mistake here: Cannot identify the correct side lengths to use when asked to find the area of a face. Double-check each step in calculate compound area involving just rectangles and squares, where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Cannot identify the correct side lengths to use when asked to find the area of a face
L2 eedi diagram eedi_989
What is the perimeter of this composite shape made from rectangles? (Diagram: A composite shape made from three rectangles that form a step structure. The base of the shape is horizontal and is 23m long. The vertical height of the whole shape is 22m. The horizontal width of the top part of the shape is 6m. The horizontal width of the first step is 7m. The vertical height of the bottom rectangle is 5m. The vertical height of the top rectangle is 8m.)
  • A. 86 m
  • B. 88 m
  • C. 90 m
  • D. 71 m
Explanation: The correct answer is 90 m. A common mistake here: Does not know how to find missing lengths in a composite shape. Double-check each step in calculate the perimeter of a rectilinear shape by adding lengths expressed in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to find missing lengths in a composite shape
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t01_q12
Andrea wants to fill in two sections of her backyard with sod that must be purchased in 2-by-2-foot squares. If the two sections measure 30 × 40 feet and 60 × 80 feet, how many squares of sod does she need to buy?
  • A. 1,000
  • B. 1,250
  • C. 1,500
  • D. 1,600
  • E. 2,000
Explanation: Step 1: Area of first section = 30 × 40 = 1,200 sq ft. Step 2: Area of second section = 60 × 80 = 4,800 sq ft. Step 3: Total area = 1,200 + 4,800 = 6,000 sq ft. Step 4: Area of each sod square = 2 × 2 = 4 sq ft. Step 5: Number of sod squares = 6,000 ÷ 4 = 1,500.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t05_q14
If the height of an equilateral triangle is 9, what is its area?
  • A. 27
  • B. 54
  • C. 81
  • D. 27√3
  • E. 54√3
Explanation: Equilateral triangle with height h. For equilateral triangle with side s: height = (s√3)/2 = 9, so s = 18/√3 = 6√3. Area = (1/2)·base·height = (1/2)(6√3)(9) = 27√3.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t06_q13
Jane ran around the perimeter of a rectangular park at a constant rate of 10 feet per second. The park has an area of 67,500 square feet, and its length is exactly three times its width. For how many seconds did Jane run?
  • A. 60
  • B. 120
  • C. 240
  • D. 360
  • E. 480
Explanation: Let width=w, length=3w. Area=3w²=67,500 → w²=22,500 → w=150 ft. Length=450 ft. Perimeter=2(150+450)=1,200 ft. Time=1,200÷10=120 seconds.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t07_q14
In the following figure, the area of the large square is 81. What is the area of the shaded square?
  • A. 45
  • B. 40.5
  • C. 40
  • D. 36
  • E. 32.4
Explanation: Large square area=81, side=9. The shaded inner square has vertices at distance 3 from each corner of the outer square along the sides. The inner square's side = sqrt(3²+6²) = sqrt(9+36) = sqrt(45) = 3√5. Area = (3√5)² = 45.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t11_q14
What is the area of the shaded region in the following figure?
  • A. 10.5
  • B. 12.5
  • C. 18
  • D. 21
  • E. 24
Explanation: Step 1: Identify the vertices: A=(−5,−3), B=(−2,−3), C=(1,4). Step 2: AB is a horizontal segment (both y=−3): base = |−2−(−5)| = 3. Step 3: Height = perpendicular distance from C=(1,4) to the line y=−3: h = 4−(−3) = 7. Step 4: Area = ½ × base × height = ½ × 3 × 7 = 10.5. Answer: F. 10.5.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t13_q14
The 8-sided figure below is divided into 12 congruent isosceles right triangles. The total area of the 12 triangles is 96 square centimeters. What is the perimeter, in centimeters, of the figure?
  • A. 8
  • B. 20 + 4
  • C. 48
  • D. 40 + 8
  • E. 56
Explanation: Step 1: Area per triangle = 96/12=8 sq cm. Step 2: Isosceles right triangle area = a²/2=8 → a=4. Hypotenuse=4√2. Step 3: 8-sided staircase perimeter: 2 diagonal edges (4√2 each) + 6 straight edges summing to 40. Step 4: Total = 40+8√2 → J.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q13
The volume of the right circular cylinder shown below is 64Ï_x0080_ cubic inches. If its height is 4 in., what is its radius in., what is its radius in inches?
  • A. 2
  • B. 4
  • C. 8
  • D. 10
  • E. 16
Explanation: Step 1: V=πr²h. Step 2: 64π=πr²(4). Step 3: 64=4r² → r²=16 → r=4 → B.
L3 eedi diagram eedi_265
STEP 2 Jessica is trying to work out the volume of this cone. (Diagram: A cone with the slant height labelled 9cm, the perpendicular height labelled h and half the cone's base (forming a right angled triangle with the slant and perpendicular heights) is labelled 6cm.) First she needs the perpendicular height. Which of the following equations is true?
  • A. h^2=9^2+6^2
  • B. h^2=9^2-6^2
  • C. h^2=12^2+9^2
  • D. h^2=12^2-9^2
Explanation: The correct answer is h^2=9^2-6^2. A common mistake here: Does not rearrange the Pythagoras' Theorem formula accurately. Double-check each step in calculate the volume of a cone where the dimensions are all given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not rearrange the Pythagoras' Theorem formula accurately

Basic Probability

core · 42 questions · basic_probability
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t08_q02
A password for a computer system requires exactly 6 characters. Each character can be either one of the 26 letters from A to Z or one of the ten digits from 0 to 9. The first character must be a letter and the last character must be a digit. How many different possible passwords are there?
  • A. less than 10 7
  • B. between 10 7 and 10 8
  • C. between 10 8 and 10 9
  • D. between 10 9 and 10 10
  • E. more than 10 10
Explanation: 6 characters. First = letter (26). Last = digit (10). Middle 4 positions = letter or digit (36 each). Total = 26 × 36⁴ × 10 = 26 × 1,679,616 × 10 ≈ 4.37 × 10⁸. 4.37×10⁸ is between 10⁸ and 10⁹.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t13_q05
A 16-piece orchestra wants to choose one of its members to speak at performances. They decide that this member CANNOT be one of the 4 soloists in the group. What is the probability that Itzhak, who is NOT a soloist, will be chosen as the speaker?
  • A. 0
  • B. E.
Explanation: Step 1: 16 total orchestra members. Step 2: 4 soloists cannot be chosen. Step 3: 12 non-soloists are eligible. Step 4: Itzhak is one non-soloist. Step 5: P(Itzhak chosen) = 1/12 → C.
L1 inline_ts bp-1-2
A fair die is rolled. What is the probability of rolling an even number?
Explanation: Even numbers on a die: {2, 4, 6} — 3 out of 6. P = 3/6 = 1/2.
L1 inline_ts bp-1-3
P(rain) = 0.3. What is P(no rain)?
Explanation: Complement rule: P(not A) = 1 − P(A). So P(no rain) = 1 − 0.3 = 0.7.
L1 inline_ts bp-1-4
A spinner has 8 equal sections numbered 1–8. What is the probability of landing on a number greater than 5?
Explanation: Numbers greater than 5: {6, 7, 8} — that's 3 out of 8. P = 3/8.
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1054
This four-sided spinner is fair. What is the probability of spinning a 2 ? Give your answer as a percentage (Diagram: A four sided spinner labelled with 1, 2, 3 and 4)
  • A. 25 %
  • B. 40 %
  • C. 20 %
  • D. 33.333 ... %
Explanation: The correct answer is 25 %. A common mistake here: Converts fraction to percentage by multiplying the denominator by 10. Double-check each step in assign probabilities to single events using percentages to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Converts fraction to percentage by multiplying the denominator by 10
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1067
This is a fair spinner. How many possible outcomes are there if it is spun once? (Diagram: A hexagonal shaped spinner with 6 equal sections labelled 1, 3, 3, 5, 5 and 5.)
  • A. 6
  • B. 3
  • C. 1
  • D. 1,3 or 5
Explanation: The correct answer is 6. A common mistake here: Confuses all possible outcomes with options available. Double-check each step in identify the outcomes of a single event to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses all possible outcomes with options available
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1076
Tom and Katie are discussing the probability spinner Tom says the sample space can be written as: S=\5,6\ Katie says the sample space can be written as: S=\5,5,6,6,6\ Who do you agree with? (Diagram: A circle divided into 5 equal sections. 3 of the sections are labelled with a purple number 6. 2 of the sections are labelled with a blue number 5.)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Believes a sample space should show repeated outcomes, not just unique possible outcomes. Double-check each step in identify and represent sets using set notation to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes a sample space should show repeated outcomes, not just unique possible outcomes
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1205
Percy is playing a game where all 26 letters of the alphabet at placed into a bag and Percy has to pick one out at random. Picking a P and a vowel are known as...
  • A. Independent events
  • B. Mutually exclusive events
  • C. Probability events
  • D. Dependant events
Explanation: The correct answer is Mutually exclusive events. A common mistake here: Cannot identify mutually exclusive events. Double-check each step in understand the term mutually exclusive to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Cannot identify mutually exclusive events
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1344
The probability of picking a green ball is 1/5 Where is this value on the probability scale? (Diagram: A diagram showing a probability scale with 10 segments. The scale starts at 0.0, has 0.5 marked after 5 segments, and 1.0 marked at the end, after 10 segments. A is marked after 2 segments, B after 4, C after 5 and D after 7.)
  • A. A
  • B. B
  • C. C
  • D. D
Explanation: The correct answer is A. A common mistake here: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using the denominator after the decimal point. Double-check each step in represent positive unit fractions on a number line to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using the denominator after the decimal point
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1369
There are 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls in a cup. (Diagram: The image shows the cup with the 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls.) What is the probability of picking a red ball?
  • A. 4/3
  • B. 7/4
  • C. 3/7
  • D. 4/7
Explanation: The correct answer is 4/7. A common mistake here: Gives the probability out of the number of non-successes rather than the total. Double-check each step in assign probabilities to single events using fractions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Gives the probability out of the number of non-successes rather than the total
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1492
The probability of an event occurring is 0 . Which of the following most accurately describes the likelihood of the event occurring?
  • A. Certain
  • B. Unlikely
  • C. Even chance
  • D. Impossible
Explanation: The correct answer is Impossible. A common mistake here: Does not understand that a probability of 0 represents something that is impossible. Double-check each step in know that probabilities must lie between 0, impossible, and 1, certain to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that a probability of 0 represents something that is impossible
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1673
This five-sided spinner is fair. What is the probability of spinning a 3 ? Give your answer as a percentage (Diagram: A five sided spinner labelled with 1, 1, 2, 3 and 4)
  • A. 30 %
  • B. 25 %
  • C. 1/5
  • D. 20 %
Explanation: The correct answer is 20 %. A common mistake here: Believes all possible outcomes must have equal probability. Double-check each step in assign probabilities to single events using percentages to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes all possible outcomes must have equal probability
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_235
A box contains 10 colored counters. 3 of the counters are pink 3 of the counters are blue The rest of the counters are green What is the probability of choosing a green counter?
  • A. 4
  • B. 6/10
  • C. 1/3
  • D. 4/10
Explanation: The correct answer is 4/10. A common mistake here: Forgets the denominator in probability. Double-check each step in assign probabilities to single events using fractions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Forgets the denominator in probability
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_350
The probability of an event occurring is 2/7 . What is the probability of the event not occurring?
  • A. 2/7
  • B. 5/7
  • C. 5
  • D. 2/5
Explanation: The correct answer is 5/7. A common mistake here: Thinks that probabilities of an event occurring or not occurring must be equal. Double-check each step in know that the probabilities of exhaustive events must sum to 1 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that probabilities of an event occurring or not occurring must be equal
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_412
The probability of an event occurring is 0.9 . Which of the following most accurately describes the likelihood of the event occurring?
  • A. Certain
  • B. Likely
  • C. Unlikely
  • D. Impossible
Explanation: The correct answer is Likely. A common mistake here: Does not understand that a probability of 1 represents something that is certain. Double-check each step in know that probabilities must lie between 0, impossible, and 1, certain to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that a probability of 1 represents something that is certain
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_794
A bag contains some strawberry sweets, some orange sweets and some blackcurrant sweets. The probability of choosing a strawberry sweet is 0.8 . What is the probability of choosing an orange sweet?
  • A. 1/3
  • B. 0.1
  • C. 0.2
  • D. Impossible to know
Explanation: The correct answer is Impossible to know. A common mistake here: Thinks that probabilities of an event occurring or not occurring must be equal. Double-check each step in know that the probabilities of exhaustive events must sum to 1 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that probabilities of an event occurring or not occurring must be equal
L1 eedi diagram eedi_930
Tom and Katie are discussing the probability spinner Tom says the sample space can be written as: S=\1,2\ Katie says the sample space can be written as: S=\1,1,2,2,2\ Who do you agree with? (Diagram: Circle divided into 5 equal sections. 2 sections labelled with a blue number 1. 3 sections labelled with a red number 2.)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Believes a sample space should show repeated outcomes, not just unique possible outcomes. Double-check each step in identify and represent sets using set notation to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes a sample space should show repeated outcomes, not just unique possible outcomes
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t06_q10
A bag contains 7 black socks, 12 white socks, and 17 red socks. If you pick one sock at random from the bag, what is the probability that it will NOT be white?
  • A. 12/36
  • B. 17/36
  • C. 12/13
  • D. 24/36
  • E. 7/12
Explanation: Total socks = 7+12+17 = 36. P(NOT white) = (36–12)/36 = 24/36 = 2/3.
L2 inline_ts bp-2-2
You flip a fair coin twice. What is the probability of exactly one head?
Explanation: Sample space: {HH, HT, TH, TT}. Exactly one head: {HT, TH} = 2/4 = 1/2.
L2 inline_ts bp-2-3
Events A and B are independent. P(A) = 0.4 and P(B) = 0.5. What is P(A and B)?
Explanation: For independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.4 × 0.5 = 0.2.
L2 inline_ts bp-2-4
In a class of 30, 18 play soccer and 12 play basketball. 6 play both. What is P(a student plays neither)?
Explanation: By inclusion-exclusion: n(soccer or basketball) = 18+12−6 = 24. Neither = 30−24 = 6. P = 6/30 = 1/5.
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1022
60 people completed an online survey about the car they own. The results are shown in the Venn Diagram. How many people own a 4 × 4 ? (Diagram: A Venn diagram with two circles labelled Owns a 4x4 and Owns a Black Car. The number 27 is in the Owns a 4x4 only region, 15 is in the intersection, and 8 is in the Owns a Black Car only region)
  • A. 27
  • B. 15
  • C. 8
  • D. 42
Explanation: The correct answer is 42. A common mistake here: Forgets to include the intersection value when asked for total value of a set in a Venn diagram. Double-check each step in identify the regions on a double venn diagram given in words to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Forgets to include the intersection value when asked for total value of a set in a Venn...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1733
All the students in a class were asked if they have a brother, sister, both or none. The Venn Diagram shows the results. (Diagram: A Venn diagram with two sets, one labelled Brother and one labelled Sister. The number 11 is written in Brother without the intersection , number 7 is in Sister without the intersection, the number 4 is in the intersection of Brother and Sister. The number 6 is outside the circles.) How many students are in the class?
  • A. 4
  • B. 24
  • C. 28
  • D. 22
Explanation: The correct answer is 28. A common mistake here: Confuses the number of regions in a Venn diagram as the total number of entries in the diagram. Double-check each step in interpret what the numbers in each region of a double venn diagram mean to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses the number of regions in a Venn diagram as the total number of entries in the...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1776
All the students in a class were asked if they have a brother, sister, both or none. The Venn Diagram shows the results. (Diagram: A Venn diagram with two sets, one labelled Brother and one labelled Sister. The number 10 is written in Brother without the intersection , number 5 is in Sister without the intersection, the number 7 is in the intersection of Brother and Sister. The number 9 is outside the circles.) How many students are in the class?
  • A. 24
  • B. 31
  • C. 4
  • D. 21
Explanation: The correct answer is 31. A common mistake here: Confuses the number of regions in a Venn diagram as the total number of entries in the diagram. Double-check each step in interpret what the numbers in each region of a double venn diagram mean to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses the number of regions in a Venn diagram as the total number of entries in the...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_443
In which region would 3 6 belong? (Diagram: A Venn diagram with two sets, one labelled Square number and one labelled Odd number. Square Number without the intersection is labelled A, Odd Number without the intersection is labelled C, the intersection of the two sets is labelled B, and outside the circles is labelled D.)
  • A. A
  • B. B
  • C. C
  • D. D
Explanation: The correct answer is A. A common mistake here: Mixes up odd and even numbers. Double-check each step in fill in regions of a double venn diagram with numbers from given information to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up odd and even numbers
L2 eedi diagram eedi_698
(Diagram: Two spinners shown. The first spinner has the numbers 1-4 and the second spinner has the number 1-5.) You spin the above fair spinners What is the probability of getting a 1 on both spinners?
  • A. 1/9
  • B. 1/20
  • C. 2/9
  • D. 2/20
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/20. A common mistake here: When multiplying fractions, multiplies the numerator and adds the denominator. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of two independent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When multiplying fractions, multiplies the numerator and adds the denominator
L2 eedi diagram eedi_761
These are fair spinners How many possible outcomes are there if they are spun together and both scores are recorded? (Diagram: Two spinners. One is a four-sided spinner with 4 equal sections labelled 1, 2, 3 and 4. One is a five-sided spinner with 5 equal sections labelled 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5.)
  • A. 20
  • B. 9
  • C. 5
  • D. 2
Explanation: The correct answer is 20. A common mistake here: Adds rather than multiplies when using the product rule for counting. Double-check each step in identify the outcomes of more than one event to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds rather than multiplies when using the product rule for counting
L2 eedi diagram eedi_80
In which region would 1 5 belong? (Diagram: A Venn diagram with two sets, one labelled Square number and one labelled Odd number. Square Number without the intersection is labelled A, Odd Number without the intersection is labelled C, the intersection of the two sets is labelled B, and outside the circles is labelled D.)
  • A. A
  • B. B
  • C. C
  • D. D
Explanation: The correct answer is C. A common mistake here: Does not know what a square number is. Double-check each step in fill in regions of a double venn diagram with numbers from given information to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know what a square number is
L2 eedi diagram eedi_813
There are 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls in a cup. (Diagram: The image shows the cup with the 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls.) Kate picks a yellow ball out and then replaces it. She then has a second pick. What is the probability the second pick is also a yellow ball?
  • A. 3/7
  • B. 3/6
  • C. 2/7
  • D. 2/6
Explanation: The correct answer is 3/7. A common mistake here: Assumes the item has not been replaced in a probability question where it has actually been replaced. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of two independent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Assumes the item has not been replaced in a probability question where it has actually...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_823
60 people completed an online survey about the car they own. The results are shown in the Venn Diagram. How many people own a black car? (Diagram: A Venn diagram with two circles labelled Owns a 4x4 and Owns a Black Car. The number 27 is in the Owns a 4x4 only region, 15 is in the intersection, and 8 is in the Owns a Black Car only region.)
  • A. 27
  • B. 15
  • C. 8
  • D. 23
Explanation: The correct answer is 23. A common mistake here: Believes the intersection on a Venn diagram represents the total of one set. Double-check each step in identify the regions on a double venn diagram given in words to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the intersection on a Venn diagram represents the total of one set
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t04_q13
When she chooses a password, Eloise always uses exactly ten different characters: five letters (A, B, C, D, and E) and five numbers (2, 3, 4, 5, and 6). Additionally, she always ensures that no pair of letters is consecutive and that no pair of numbers is consecutive. How many different passwords conform to these rules?
  • A. fewer than 1,000
  • B. between 1,000 and 10,000
  • C. between 10,000 and 100,000
  • D. between 100,000 and 1,000,000
  • E. more than 1,000,000
Explanation: Step 1: Count 4-digit codes (range 1000–9999) where digits alternate between even and odd (or equivalently: first digit odd/even, next even/odd, etc.). Step 2: For an even-first pattern: even digits 0,2,4,6,8 (5 choices each even slot); odd digits 1,3,5,7,9 (5 choices each odd slot). But position 1 (thousands) can't be 0 if we need a 4-digit number (≥1000). Step 3: If first digit must be odd (5 ch…
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t11_q13
A two-digit number from 10 to 99, inclusive, is chosen at random. What is the probability that this number is divisible by 5?
  • A. E.
Explanation: Step 1: Total two-digit numbers from 10 to 99 inclusive: 99−10+1 = 90. Step 2: Two-digit multiples of 5 from 10 to 99: 10,15,20,...,95. Count = (95−10)/5 + 1 = 85/5 + 1 = 17+1 = 18. Step 3: Probability = 18/90 = 1/5. Answer: A. 1/5.
L3 inline_ts bp-3-2
A shooter makes 60% of free throws (independently). What is the probability of making all 3 in a row?
Explanation: P(all 3) = 0.6 × 0.6 × 0.6 = 0.6³ = 0.216.
L3 inline_ts bp-3-3
A and B are mutually exclusive. P(A) = 0.3 and P(A or B) = 0.7. What is P(B)?
Explanation: Mutually exclusive means P(A and B) = 0. P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) → 0.7 = 0.3 + P(B) → P(B) = 0.4.
L3 eedi word_problem eedi_1024
Jumbo Restaurant has 34 choices of starters, 92 choices of main course, and 27 choices of dessert. Sally wants a starter, main course and dessert. Which of the following calculations would correctly work out the total possible combinations of available to Sally?
  • A. 34 × 92 × 27
  • B. 3 × 34 × 92 × 27
  • C. 34+92+27
  • D. 3 ×(34+92+27)
Explanation: The correct answer is 34 × 92 × 27. A common mistake here: Adds rather than multiplies when using the product rule for counting. Double-check each step in identify the total number of combinations in a given scenario by using the product rule for counting where duplicates are considered different to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds rather than multiplies when using the product rule for counting
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1160
There are 5 red balls and 4 yellow balls in a cup (Diagram: A cup with 5 red and 4 yellow balls in it. All of the balls are the same size.) Kate picks out two balls at the same time. What is the probability at least one ball is red?
  • A. 67/72
  • B. 20/72
  • C. 60/72
  • D. 40/72
Explanation: The correct answer is 60/72. A common mistake here: Does not work out all possibilites when given an 'at least' question in probability. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of two dependent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not work out all possibilites when given an 'at least' question in probability
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1200
There are 5 red balls and 4 yellow balls in a cup (Diagram: A cup with 5 red and 4 yellow balls in it. All of the balls are the same size.) Kate picks out three balls at the same time. She wants to work out the probability all the balls are red. What is the denominator of the final unsimplified answer?
  • A. 24
  • B. 9
  • C. 504
  • D. 729
Explanation: The correct answer is 504. A common mistake here: Adds rather than multiplies for combined probabilities. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of three or more dependent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds rather than multiplies for combined probabilities
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1312
There are 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls in a cup (Diagram: A cup with 4 red and 3 yellow balls in it. All of the balls are the same size.) Kate picks out three balls at the same time. She wants to work out the probability all the balls are red. What is the denominator of the final unsimplified answer?
  • A. 7
  • B. 210
  • C. 343
  • D. 18
Explanation: The correct answer is 210. A common mistake here: Adds rather than multiplies for combined probabilities. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of three or more dependent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds rather than multiplies for combined probabilities
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1326
There are 5 red balls and 4 yellow balls in a cup (Diagram: A cup with 5 red and 4 yellow balls in it. All of the balls are the same size.) Kate picks a yellow ball out and does not replace it. She then has a second pick. What is the probability the second pick is a red ball?
  • A. 5/9
  • B. 5/8
  • C. 4/9
  • D. 4/8
Explanation: The correct answer is 5/8. A common mistake here: Does not reduce the denominator when an item is not replaced in a probability question. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of two dependent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not reduce the denominator when an item is not replaced in a probability question
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1836
There are 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls in a cup (Diagram: A cup with 4 red and 3 yellow balls in it. The balls are all the same size.) Kate picks out two balls at the same time. What is the probability at least one ball is red?
  • A. 12/42
  • B. 36/42
  • C. 24/42
  • D. 37/42
Explanation: The correct answer is 36/42. A common mistake here: Does not work out all possibilites when given an 'at least' question in probability. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of two dependent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not work out all possibilites when given an 'at least' question in probability
L3 eedi diagram eedi_461
There are 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls in a cup. (Diagram: The image shows the cup with the 4 red balls and 3 yellow balls.) Kate picks a yellow ball out and does not replace it. She then has a second pick. What is the probability the second pick is a red ball?
  • A. 3/7
  • B. 3/6
  • C. 4/7
  • D. 4/6
Explanation: The correct answer is 4/6. A common mistake here: Does not reduce the denominator when an item is not replaced in a probability question. Double-check each step in calculate the probability of two dependent events occurring without drawing a tree diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not reduce the denominator when an item is not replaced in a probability question

Geometry of Circles

core · 18 questions · circles_geometry
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t05_q05
In the following figure, the circle centered at n has a radius of 4. What is the area of the shaded region?
  • A. 3 π
  • B. 6 π
  • C. 9 π
  • D. 12 π
  • E. 16 π
Explanation: Circle with radius 4, area = π(4²) = 16π. The shaded region is 3/4 of the circle (a three-quarter sector). Shaded area = (3/4)(16π) = 12π.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t08_q03
On the xy- plane, what is the area of a circle with this equation: ( x + 3) 2 + ( y – 4) 2 = 49?
  • A. 5 π
  • B. 7 π
  • C. 25 π
  • D. 49 π
  • E. 125 π
Explanation: (x+3)²+(y-4)²=49 is in standard form (x-h)²+(y-k)²=r². r²=49, r=7. Area=π(7²)=49π.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t08_q05
The following figure shows a cylindrical tank whose diameter is 3 times the length of its height. The tank holds approximately 231.5 cubic meters of fluid. Which of the following answer choices most closely approximates the height of the tank?
  • A. 2 meters
  • B. 3 meters
  • C. 4 meters
  • D. 5 meters
  • E. 6 meters
Explanation: Diameter = 3h (given), so radius r = 3h/2. Volume = πr²h = π(3h/2)²h = 9πh³/4 = 231.5. h³ = 4×231.5/(9π) = 926/(9π) ≈ 32.75. h = ∛32.75 ≈ 3.2 meters. Closest choice: G. 3 meters.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t12_q02
In the following figure, is a chord of the circle centered at O, with . If KL = 12 and MO = 6, what is the area of the circle?
  • A. 36 π
  • B. 48 π
  • C. 72 π
  • D. 96 π
  • E. 144 π
Explanation: Step 1: Chord KL=12, M is midpoint, so MK=6. Step 2: OM⊥KL (radius to midpoint of chord is perpendicular). Step 3: Right triangle OMK: OM²+MK²=r². Step 4: From figure OM=6. Step 5: r²=6²+6²=72. Step 6: Area=πr²=72π → C.
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1237
If the diameter of a circle is 5.4 cm , the radius is...
  • A. 2.2 cm
  • B. 10.8 cm
  • C. 2.7 cm
  • D. 5.4 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 2.7 cm. A common mistake here: Doubles the diameter when finding the radius. Double-check each step in recognise that the diameter is twice the radius to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Doubles the diameter when finding the radius
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1247
What is the correct name for the line marked on the circle? (Diagram: A circle with a line from the centre of the circle to the circumference.)
  • A. Radius
  • B. Chord
  • C. Tangent
  • D. Diameter
Explanation: The correct answer is Radius. A common mistake here: Confuses chord and radius. Double-check each step in identify a radius to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses chord and radius
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1359
Which of the following diagrams shows the circumference of a circle?
  • A. ![Circle with a line from the centre to the edge]()
  • B. ![Circle with its whole edge highlighted]()
  • C. ![Circle with a line below it, touching the circle in one place]()
  • D. ![Circle with a line across from edge to edge, not passing through the centre]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![Circle with its whole edge highlighted](). A common mistake here: Confuses circumference and radius. Double-check each step in identify the circumference to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses circumference and radius
L1 eedi diagram eedi_601
The straight line across this circle is called a ....? (Diagram: A circle with a line across from edge to edge, passing through the centre)
  • A. Diagram
  • B. Diameter
  • C. Diagonal
  • D. Diamond
Explanation: The correct answer is Diameter. A common mistake here: Does not know the term 'diameter'. Double-check each step in identify a diameter to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the term 'diameter'
L1 eedi diagram eedi_914
The straight line shown in this diagram is a ....? (Diagram: A circle with a line across from edge to edge, passing through the centre)
  • A. Radius
  • B. Arc
  • C. Diameter
  • D. Circumference
Explanation: The correct answer is Diameter. A common mistake here: Confuses diameter and radius. Double-check each step in identify a diameter to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses diameter and radius
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t02_q07
In the following figure, ABCD is a square and is a diameter of the circle centered at O. If the area of the square is 100, what is the area of the shaded region?
  • A. K.
Explanation: Geometry of Circles → square area 100 means side length 10 → CD is the circle diameter so diameter=10 → radius=5 → shaded region is half the circle → area = πr²/2 = 25π/2.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t03_q08
In the following figure, the area of the shaded region is 10% of the area of the circle. If the radius of the circle is 10, what is the arc length from P to Q ?
  • A. π
  • B. 2 π
  • C. 4 π
  • D. 5 π
  • E. 10 π
Explanation: Step 1: The shaded sector's area is 10% of the whole circle's area, so its central angle is also 10% of 360° → 36°. Step 2: Arc length = (angle/360°) × circumference = (36/360) × 2π(10) = 0.1 × 20π = 2π.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t04_q06
If you plot the equation x 2 + ( y – 2) 2 = 4 as a circle on a standard xy- graph, what is the area of the circle’s region that will lie in Quadrant 1, as shown in the following figure?
  • A. 0
  • B. π
  • C. 2 π
  • D. 4 π
  • E. 16 π
Explanation: Step 1: A circle with radius 4 has full circumference = 2π(4) = 8π. Step 2: A quarter of that circle's arc = 8π/4 = 2π.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t11_q08
In the following figure, O is the center of the circle, is tangent to the circle at D, and is tangent to the circle at
  • A. Which of the following must be true?
  • B. is isosceles
  • C. is a square
Explanation: Step 1: AB is tangent to the circle at D → OD ⊥ AB → angle ODB = 90°. Step 2: BC is tangent to the circle at E → OE ⊥ BC → angle OEB = 90°. Step 3: The figure shows angle B = 90° (right angle symbol at B). Step 4: Sum of angles in quadrilateral DBEO = 360°. Angles D + E + B + O = 90° + 90° + 90° + angle O = 360° → angle O = 90°. Step 5: All four angles = 90°. Since tangent lengths from external po…
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t03_q15
In the following figure, each circle is tangent to the other two circles, and the two shaded circles are identical to each other. What is the ratio of the shaded region to the nonshaded region?
  • A. 1:01
  • B. 4:05
  • C. 5:04
  • D. 4: π
  • E. π :4
Explanation: Step 1: The two identical shaded circles sit side by side inside the large circle, each tangent to the other and internally tangent to the large circle — so the two small circles' combined width (2 diameters) spans the large circle's diameter. Step 2: If the large circle has radius R, each small circle has diameter R, i.e. radius R/2. Step 3: Each small circle's area = π(R/2)² = πR²/4; two of them…
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t07_q13
In the following figure, the area of the shaded region is 20% of the area of the whole circle centered at P. The angle shown measures d degrees. What is its measurement in radians?
  • A. π/18
  • B. π/9
  • C. 2π/9
  • D. 2π/5
  • E. 4π/5
Explanation: 20% of the circle means the central angle is 20% of 360° = 72°. Convert to radians: 72° x (π/180) = 72π/180 = 2π/5.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t12_q15
The following figure shows a regular octagon inscribed in a circle. The arc length from A to B is 6 π . What is the area of the shaded region of the circle?
  • A. 8 π
  • B. 16 π
  • C. 24 π
  • D. 36 π
  • E. 64 π
Explanation: Step 1: Regular octagon has 8 equal arcs, each = 2πr/8 = πr/4. Step 2: Arc AB spans 3 of the 8 arcs: 3(πr/4)=6π → r=8. Step 3: Full circle area=π(8²)=64π. Step 4: Shaded sector=(3/8)(64π)=24π → H.
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1706
Which of the following points lies on the circle with equation: x^2+y^2=10
  • A. (-9,1)
  • B. (0,5)
  • C. (3,-1)
  • D. (6,4)
Explanation: The correct answer is (3,-1). A common mistake here: Believes squaring a negative number just changes the sign. Double-check each step in calculate points that lie on the equation of a circle in the form x² + y² = r² to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes squaring a negative number just changes the sign
L3 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_26
What is the equation of this circle? (Diagram: A set of axes with a circle drawn. The circle goes through the points (4,0), (0, -4), (-4,0) and (0,4).)
  • A. x^2+y^2=16
  • B. x^2+y^2=√(4)
  • C. x^2+y^2=8
  • D. x^2+y^2=4
Explanation: The correct answer is x^2+y^2=16. A common mistake here: Confuses the square root of the radius with the radius squared in the equation of a circle. Double-check each step in given a sketch of a circle with centre at the origin, write its equation in the form x² + y² = r² to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses the square root of the radius with the radius squared in the equation of a circle

Complex Numbers

advanced · 4 questions · complex_numbers
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t12_q01
In the complex numbers, where i 2 = –1, what is the value of 5 + 6 i multiplied by 3 – 2 i ?
  • A. 27
  • B. 27 i
  • C. 27 + 8 i
  • D. 15 + 8 i
  • E. 15 – 18 i
Explanation: Step 1: Expand (5+6i)(3-2i) using FOIL: 5×3=15, 5×(-2i)=-10i, 6i×3=18i, 6i×(-2i)=-12i². Step 2: i²=-1 so -12i²=+12. Step 3: Real parts: 15+12=27. Imaginary parts: -10i+18i=8i. Step 4: Result: 27+8i → H.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t03_q09
Given that i 2 = –1, what is the value of (4 + 2 i )(4 – 2 i )?
  • A. 12
  • B. 20
  • C. 16 – 4 i
  • D. 4 + 16 i
  • E. 12 – 16 i
Explanation: Step 1: (4+2i)(4–2i) is a product of conjugates, matching the pattern (a+b)(a–b)=a²–b². Step 2: Here a=4, b=2i, so the product = 4² – (2i)² = 16 – 4i². Step 3: Since i²=–1, 4i² = –4, so 16 – (–4) = 16+4 = 20.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t08_q14
In the complex numbers, where , the conjugate of any value a + bi is a – bi. What is the result when you multiply 2 + 7 i by its conjugate?
  • A. 45
  • B. –45
  • C. 45 i
  • D. 53
  • E. 53 i
Explanation: (2+7i)(2-7i) = 2²+(7)² = 4+49 = 53. (Using conjugate pair: (a+bi)(a-bi)=a²+b².)
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t15_q11
For the imaginary number i , which of the following is a possible value of i n if n is an integer less than 5?
  • A. 0
  • B. -1
  • C. -2
  • D. -3
  • E. -4
Explanation: Step 1: Powers of i cycle: i⁰=1, i¹=i, i²=-1, i³=-i, i⁴=1, ... Step 2: For n<5, possible real values: 1 (n=0,4) and -1 (n=2). Step 3: Among choices A.0,B.-1,C.-2,D.-3,E.-4: only B.-1 is a valid power of i → B.

coordinate_geometry

· 2 questions · coordinate_geometry
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q08
The endpoints of the diameter of a circle O are A and C . In the standard ( x , y ) coordinate plane, A is at (4,3) and C is at (-9,-2). What is the y -coordinate of the center of the circle?
  • A. -5
  • B. -
  • C. 1
  • D. 2
Explanation: Step 1: Endpoints of diameter: A(4,3) and C(-9,-2). Step 2: Center = midpoint = ((4+(-9))/2, (3+(-2))/2) = (-5/2, 1/2). Step 3: y-coordinate = 1/2 → H.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q09
On a sonar map in the standard ( x , y ) coordinate plane, the Yellow Submarine and the Sandwich Submarine are located at the points (-7,4) and (-2,6), respectively. Each unit on the map represents an actual distance of 5 nautical miles. Which of the following is closest to the distance, in nautical miles, between the two submarines?
  • A. 5
  • B. 19
  • C. 27
  • D. 30
  • E. 67
Explanation: Step 1: Distance in units = √((-2-(-7))²+(6-4)²) = √(5²+2²) = √29 ≈ 5.385 units. Step 2: Each unit = 5 nautical miles. Step 3: Distance = 5√29 ≈ 5×5.385 ≈ 26.93 ≈ 27 nautical miles → C.

Descriptive Statistics

core · 49 questions · descriptive_statistics
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t01_q05
William earned $3,200 per month as a teacher for the ten months from September to June. Then he took a job as a barista at a local café, where he earned $2,000 per month during July and August. What was his average monthly pay for the 12 months?
  • A. $2,400
  • B. $2,500
  • C. $2,600
  • D. $2,800
  • E. $3,000
Explanation: Step 1: Total earnings in 10 months (Sep–Jun) = 10 × $3,200 = $32,000. Step 2: Total earnings in 2 months (Jul–Aug) = 2 × $2,000 = $4,000. Step 3: Grand total over 12 months = $32,000 + $4,000 = $36,000. Step 4: Monthly average = $36,000 ÷ 12 = $3,000.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t04_q04
The following graph shows the number of new bank accounts that eight account executives have opened so far this week. Which of the following answer choices is the median number of accounts opened among these eight people?
  • A. 9.5
  • B. 10
  • C. 10.5
  • D. 11
  • E. 12
Explanation: Step 1: Read off the frequency counts from the bar chart for each value/category. Step 2: Find the total count n, then the median is the value at position (n+1)/2 when data is sorted. Step 3: Accumulate frequencies from the lowest value until the middle position is reached — the median falls at 10.5 (midpoint between the two middle values in an even-n dataset).
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t11_q04
This semester, Gerry scored an average of 93 on his five history exams. He got the same score on his first two exams, and then he got a 94, an 85, and a 90 on the remaining exams. What score did he receive on his first two exams?
  • A. 95
  • B. 96
  • C. 97
  • D. 98
  • E. 99
Explanation: Step 1: Total score = average × number of exams = 93 × 5 = 465. Step 2: Last three exam scores: 94 + 85 + 90 = 269. Step 3: First two exams (each score x): 2x = 465 − 269 = 196. Step 4: x = 98. Answer: J. 98.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t12_q03
Eldridge opened a savings account with an initial balance of $1,000. After that, every month for 8 months, she made one deposit to the account, always for the same amount. During that time, she made just one withdrawal. Which of the following graphs accurately describes these transactions over an 8-month period?
  • A. K.
Explanation: Step 1: Eldridge makes equal monthly deposits except one month of withdrawal. Step 2: Correct graph shows uniform positive bars for deposit months, one bar at a different (negative or lower) level for the withdrawal month. Step 3: Answer: H (graph matching this pattern).
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q03
A recent survey of book critics asked 30 critics how many stars out of a possible 5 they gave to a recent novel from a popular author. The 30 critics' responses are summarized by the histogram below. What fraction of the critics gave the book a one-star review?
  • A. E.
Explanation: Step 1: Read histogram: 1-star bar height = 4 critics. Step 2: Total critics = 30. Step 3: Fraction = 4/30 = 2/15 → E.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q04
A recent survey of book critics asked 30 critics how many stars out of a possible 5 they gave to a recent novel from a popular author. The 30 critics' responses are summarized by the histogram below. The group that took the survey wants to show the data in a circle graph (pie chart). What should be the measure of the central angle of the portion for one-star reviews?
  • A. 15Â°
  • B. 24Â°
  • C. 30Â°
  • D. 48Â°
  • E. 60Â°
Explanation: Step 1: 1-star reviews = 4 out of 30. Step 2: Central angle = (fraction)×360° = (4/30)×360° = 48° → J.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q05
A recent survey of book critics asked 30 critics how many stars out of a possible 5 they gave to a recent novel from a popular author. The 30 critics' responses are summarized by the histogram below. To the nearest hundredth, what is the average star review for the 30 reviews?
  • A. 2
  • B. 2.33
  • C. 2.5
  • D. 2.63
  • E. 3
Explanation: Step 1: From histogram: 0-star×3, 1-star×4, 2-star×6, 3-star×8, 4-star×6, 5-star×3. Step 2: Weighted sum=0+4+12+24+24+15=79. Step 3: Average=79/30≈2.633≈2.63 → D.
L1 inline_ts ds-1-2
What is the median of 3, 9, 4, 12, and 7?
Explanation: Sort the data: 3, 4, 7, 9, 12. The middle value is 7.
L1 inline_ts ds-1-3
What is the range of 11, 6, 18, 9, and 20?
Explanation: Range = maximum − minimum. The maximum is 20 and the minimum is 6, so range = 20 − 6 = 14.
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1092
What is the median of the following numbers? 2, 0.2, 0.02, 0.002
  • A. 0.22
  • B. 0.11
  • C. 0.44
  • D. 0.011
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.11. A common mistake here: When finding the median from a even dataset does not understand we must find the midpoint of the two values in the middle. Double-check each step in calculate the median as an average from an even numbered list of data to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When finding the median from a even dataset does not understand we must find the midpoi...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1197
When calculating the mean of the following numbers which calculation will give the correct answer? 0,0,2,6,9
  • A. 0+0+2+6+9 ÷ 5
  • B. 2+6+9/5
  • C. 2+6+9/3
  • D. None of them
Explanation: The correct answer is 2+6+9/5. A common mistake here: Does not include brackets when required. Double-check each step in calculate the mean as an average from a list of data to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not include brackets when required
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1262
What is the mean of the following numbers? 1, 0.1, 0.01
  • A. 0.333
  • B. 0.33
  • C. 0.1
  • D. 0.37
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.37. A common mistake here: Believes you divide 1 by the number of values, rather than the total, when finding the mean. Double-check each step in divide a decimal by an integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes you divide 1 by the number of values, rather than the total, when finding the...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1328
Without using a calculator, what is the mean of the following numbers? 3,4,3,4,4
  • A. 3
  • B. A little more than 4
  • C. 4
  • D. A little less than 4
Explanation: The correct answer is A little less than 4. A common mistake here: Believes the mean is the middle number. Double-check each step in calculate the mean as an average from a list of data to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the mean is the middle number
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1330
What is the mode of the following numbers? 1,1,4,6,7,7
  • A. 1 and 7
  • B. 7
  • C. 4
  • D. There is no mode
Explanation: The correct answer is 1 and 7. A common mistake here: Believes you can only have one mode. Double-check each step in calculate the mode as an average from a list of data when there are two or more modes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes you can only have one mode
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1437
What is the range of this set of data? 4 cm, 22 cm, 5 cm, 7 cm
  • A. 18 cm
  • B. 22 cm
  • C. 3 cm
  • D. 26 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 18 cm. A common mistake here: Identifies the biggest value for the range. Double-check each step in calculate the range from a list of data to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Identifies the biggest value for the range

  • A. 0.055
  • B. 0.22
  • C. 0.55

  • A. 20
  • B. 4
  • C. 3
  • D. 2

  • A. Subtract the smallest number from the largest number.
  • B. Write the numbers in ascending order.
  • C. Add all the numbers together.
  • D. Write the numbers in ascending or descending order.

⚠ Common trap: Finds the median instead of the range

⚠ Common trap: Believes you don't need to numerically order the data before calculating the median

  • D. 120

  • B. Check the units of your answer
  • C. Add all the numbers together.

⚠ Common trap: Believes range does not need units
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_254
When calculating the mean of the following numbers which calculation will give the correct answer? 0,1,3,4,7
  • A. 1+3+4+7/5
  • B. 0+1+3+4+7 ÷ 5
  • C. 1+3+4+7/4
  • D. None of them
Explanation: The correct answer is 1+3+4+7/5. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in calculate the mean as an average from a list of data to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order

  • B. 2/5

L1 eedi word_problem eedi_433
Leanne has written a questionnaire on the topic of holidays. One of her questions is, "Describe how you feel when you are about to go on holiday." What type of data is she collecting by asking this question?
  • A. Primary Quantitative data
  • B. Secondary Quantitative data
  • C. Attribute data
  • D. Primary Qualitative data
Explanation: The correct answer is Primary Qualitative data. A common mistake here: Mixes up quantitative and qualitative data types. Double-check each step in identify data which is qualitative or quantitative to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up quantitative and qualitative data types
L1 eedi diagram eedi_494
(Diagram: Pictogram showing smiley faces, where each smiley face is worth 10 people. Game 1 has 5 smiley faces. Game 2 has 2 smiley faces. Game 3 has 3 smiley faces. Game 4 has 2 smiley faces.) The pictogram shows how many people came to watch a netball match. What is the mean number of people at each game?
  • A. 3
  • B. 30
  • C. 10
  • D. 120
Explanation: The correct answer is 30. A common mistake here: When interpreting a pictogram, thinks each symbol stands for 1. Double-check each step in interpret a pictogram involving full symbols to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When interpreting a pictogram, thinks each symbol stands for 1

  • A. 0 and 2
  • B. 2

  • D. There is no mode

L2 act_master diagram act_math_t02_q10
This question refers to the following figure, which shows a graph of the percentage of votes tallied by an online book club regarding the next author that the club should focus on. Which of the following pairs of authors received exactly 30 percent of the votes altogether?
  • A. Cormac McCarthy and Toni Morrison
  • B. Gabriel García Márquez and Toni?Morrison
  • C. Toni Morrison and Annie Proulx
  • D. Toni Morrison and John Steinbeck
  • E. Annie Proulx and John Steinbeck
Explanation: Data Display → read each author percentage from the pie chart → add candidate pairs → choose the pair totaling exactly 30%.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t05_q06
Tara’s three bowling scores in a tournament were 167, 178, and 186. What was her average score for the tournament?
  • A. 176
  • B. 177
  • C. 178
  • D. 179
  • E. 180
Explanation: Sum = 167+178+186 = 531. Average = 531÷3 = 177.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t09_q10
Damien played golf on each of the four days of his vacation. His scores on the first three days were 93, 92, and 89, and his average for the four days was 90. What was his score on the fourth day?
  • A. 84
  • B. 85
  • C. 86
  • D. 87
  • E. 88
Explanation: Average of 4 scores = 90. Total = 4×90 = 360. Sum of first 3: 93+92+89 = 274. 4th score = 360–274 = 86.
L2 inline_ts ds-2-2
For the data set 2, 5, 5, 7, 11, 12, what is the median?
Explanation: There are 6 values, so the median is the average of the 3rd and 4th values. Those are 5 and 7, so median = (5 + 7)/2 = 6.
L2 inline_ts ds-2-3
If every value in a data set is increased by 4, what happens to the standard deviation?
Explanation: Adding the same constant to every value shifts the entire data set but does not change how spread out the values are. Standard deviation stays the same.
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_2
Tom and Katie are discussing the 5 plants with these heights: 24 cm, 17 cm, 42 cm, 26 cm, 13 cm Tom says if all the plants were cut in half, the range wouldn't change. Katie says if all the plants grew by 3 cm each, the range wouldn't change. Who do you agree with?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Believes if you changed all values by the same proportion the range would not change. Double-check each step in calculate the range from a list of data to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes if you changed all values by the same proportion the range would not change
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_699
Tom and Katie are discussing the 5 plants with these heights: 23 cm, 17 cm, 40 cm, 25 cm, 31 cm Tom says if all the plants doubled in height, the range wouldn't change. Katie says if all the plants grew by 12 cm each, the range wouldn't change. Who do you agree with?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Believes if you changed all values by the same proportion the range would not change. Double-check each step in calculate the range from a list of data to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes if you changed all values by the same proportion the range would not change
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t02_q11
This question refers to the following figure, which shows a graph of the percentage of votes tallied by an online book club regarding the next author that the club should focus on. If exactly 260 people voted, how many votes did Cormac McCarthy receive?
  • A. 15
  • B. 30
  • C. 33
  • D. 39
  • E. 45
Explanation: Data Display → Cormac McCarthy has 15% of votes → total votes = 260 → 0.15×260 = 39 votes.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t14_q14
In early November in Winnipeg, Manitoba, the temperatures for each of nine consecutive days were -9Â°C, 3Â°C, -7Â°C, 2Â°C, 5Â°C, 1Â°C, 0Â°C, -8Â°C, and -7Â°
  • A. -7Â°C
  • B. 0Â°C
  • C. 1.5Â°C
  • D. 3Â°C
  • E. 5Â°C
Explanation: Step 1: Temperatures: -9,3,-7,2,5,1,0,-8,-7. Step 2: Sort ascending: -9,-8,-7,-7,0,1,2,3,5. Step 3: 9 values → median = 5th value = 0°C → B.
L3 inline_ts ds-3-2
A data set has mean 20. One value, 8, is removed, and the mean of the remaining values is 22. How many values were in the original data set?
Explanation: Let n be the original count. Original sum = 20n. After removing 8, the remaining count is n−1 and sum is 22(n−1). Equation: 20n − 8 = 22(n−1). Then 20n − 8 = 22n − 22, so 14 = 2n and n = 7.
L3 inline_ts ds-3-3
Two data sets have the same mean. Set A has values clustered within 2 units of the mean, while Set B has values spread 15 units from the mean. Which statement is true?
Explanation: Standard deviation measures spread around the mean. Since Set B values are farther from the mean, Set B has the larger standard deviation.

Exponent Rules

core · 71 questions · exponent_rules
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1079
What is the square root of sixteen?
  • A. 8
  • B. 32
  • C. 256
  • D. 4
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Halves when asked to find the square root. Double-check each step in calculate the square root of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Halves when asked to find the square root
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1110
Tom and Katie are arguing about powers. m and n are integers Tom says there is no value of m that means 2^m ends in 0 . Katie says there is no value of n that means 5^n ends in 0 . Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Assumes a fact without considering enough examples. Double-check each step in raise a positive integer to a positive integer power to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Assumes a fact without considering enough examples

  • B. 36

  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Jo and Paul. A common mistake here: Assumes a fact without considering enough examples. Double-check each step in raise a positive integer to a positive integer power to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Assumes a fact without considering enough examples
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_656
What is the first cube number greater than 40 ?
  • A. 43
  • B. 49
  • C. 64
  • D. 125
Explanation: The correct answer is 64. A common mistake here: Has confused cube numbers with prime numbers. Double-check each step in recognise cube numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Has confused cube numbers with prime numbers

What is the square root of nine?
  • A. 3
  • B. 4.5
  • C. 81
  • D. 18
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Halves when asked to find the square root. Double-check each step in calculate the square root of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Halves when asked to find the square root
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_683
Which of the following numbers is a cube number?
  • A. 3
  • B. 6
  • C. 9
  • D. None of these numbers are cube numbers
Explanation: The correct answer is None of these numbers are cube numbers. A common mistake here: Does not know what a cube number is. Double-check each step in recognise cube numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know what a cube number is
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_731
Which of the following numbers is a cube number?
  • A. 1
  • B. 3
  • C. 9
  • D. None of these numbers are cube numbers
Explanation: The correct answer is 1. A common mistake here: Mixes up cubing and multiplying by 3. Double-check each step in recognise cube numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up cubing and multiplying by 3
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_854
What is the first cube number greater than 100 ?
  • A. 103
  • B. 125
  • C. 121
  • D. 102
Explanation: The correct answer is 125. A common mistake here: Does not know what a cube number is. Double-check each step in recognise cube numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know what a cube number is
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_887
Tom and Katie are arguing about powers. Tom says 2^6=4^3 Katie says 2^6=8^2 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Multiplies by the index. Double-check each step in change an integer base number to another integer base number with a positive integer power to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Multiplies by the index
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_958
Jo and Paul are arguing about powers. Jo says 9^4=3^8 Paul says 9^4=3^2 × 3^2 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Jo. A common mistake here: Believes you multiply matching bases when carrying out a multiplication. Double-check each step in change an integer base number to another integer base number with a positive integer power to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes you multiply matching bases when carrying out a multiplication
L1 inline_ts ex-1-10
What is 2⁻³?
Explanation: Negative exponent = reciprocal: 2⁻³ = 1/2³ = 1/8.
L1 inline_ts ex-1-2
What is the value of (3²)³?
Explanation: Power of a power: multiply exponents. (3²)³ = 3^(2×3) = 3⁶ = 729.
L1 inline_ts ex-1-3
Simplify: x⁶ ÷ x²
Explanation: Dividing same base: subtract exponents. x⁶ ÷ x² = x^(6−2) = x⁴.
L1 inline_ts ex-1-4
What is 5⁰?
Explanation: Any nonzero number raised to the power 0 equals 1. So 5⁰ = 1.
L1 inline_ts ex-1-6
What is (3²)³?
Explanation: Power of a power: multiply exponents. 3^(2×3) = 3⁶.
L1 inline_ts ex-1-7
Simplify: x⁵ ÷ x²
Explanation: Same base, subtract exponents: x^(5−2) = x³.
L1 inline_ts ex-1-8
What is 5⁰?
Explanation: Any non-zero number raised to the power 0 equals 1.
L1 inline_ts ex-1-9
Simplify: (2x²)(3x³)
Explanation: Multiply coefficients: 2×3=6. Multiply variables: x²×x³=x⁵. Result: 6x⁵.
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1095
300200 Write this number using standard form
  • A. 3.002 × 10^5
  • B. 3.2 × 10^5
  • C. 3.002 × 10^6
  • D. 3.2 × 10^6
Explanation: The correct answer is 3.002 × 10^5. A common mistake here: When changing a number to standard form omits zero's of significance . Double-check each step in write numbers greater than 1 in standard form to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When changing a number to standard form omits zero's of significance
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1353
Simplify (if possible): 6^9 ÷ 6^3
  • A. 6^3
  • B. 6^6
  • C. 1^3
  • D. 1^6

⚠ Common trap: Believes when dividing numbers with the same base you divide the powers, rather than su...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1387
What is the reciprocal of 7 ?
  • A. -1/7
  • B. 1/7
  • C. -7
  • D. 0.7
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/7. A common mistake here: Correctly finds the reciprocal of a number, but believes it should be opposite in sign to the original number. Double-check each step in find the reciprocal of an integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Correctly finds the reciprocal of a number, but believes it should be opposite in sign...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1393

  • A. 1.7 × 10^-5
  • B. 1 × 10^-1
  • C. -1.7 × 10^5
  • D. -1.7 × 10^4

⚠ Common trap: Believes a negative power results in a negative answer
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1423

  • A. 3.001 × 10^4
  • B. 3.1 × 10^-4
  • C. 3.001 × 10^-4
  • D. 3.1 × 10^-7

⚠ Common trap: Does not think we can have negative powers in standard form

  • A. 44
  • B. 64
  • C. 400
  • D. 8

L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1552
Tom and Katie are discussing standard form. Tom says 9.3 × 10^5>2.3 × 10^6 Katie says 9.3 × 10^-5>2.3 × 10^-6 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct

  • A. 5.06 × 10^3
  • B. 5.6 × 10^-3
  • C. 5.06 × 10^-5
  • D. 5.06 × 10^-3

Which of the following is a power of 5 ?
  • A. 55
  • B. 100
  • C. 500
  • D. 125

L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1661
(4^6)^2=
  • A. 4^8
  • B. 16^12
  • C. 4^12
  • D. None of these options
Explanation: The correct answer is 4^12. A common mistake here: Has added the powers rather than multiplying them. Double-check each step in calculate questions involving the power law of indices with positive integer powers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Has added the powers rather than multiplying them
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1815
(16)^0=?
  • A. 16
  • B. 0
  • C. 1
  • D. You cannot have a power of 0

⚠ Common trap: Forgotten that anything to the power of 0 equals 1
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_193
(27)^4/3=?
  • A. 36
  • B. 20.25
  • C. 12
  • D. 81

L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_213
What is the reciprocal of -1/3 ?
  • A. -3
  • B. -0.333 ...
  • C. 3
  • D. 1/3

L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_267
(5^4)^3=
  • A. 5^12
  • B. 5^7
  • C. 125^12
  • D. None of these options

⚠ Common trap: Has added the powers rather than multiplying them
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_565
(16)^5/2=?
  • A. 1024
  • B. 80/32
  • C. 20
  • D. 40

⚠ Common trap: Assumes they multiply by the numerator of fractional index
L2 inline_ts ex-2-10
Which expression equals √(x³)?
Explanation: √(x³) = (x³)^(1/2) = x^(3×1/2) = x^(3/2).
L2 inline_ts ex-2-2
Simplify: (2x²y³)³
Explanation: Raise each factor to power 3: 2³ × x^(2×3) × y^(3×3) = 8x⁶y⁹.
L2 inline_ts ex-2-3
What is 5⁰ + 5⁻¹?
Explanation: 5⁰ = 1 and 5⁻¹ = 1/5 (negative exponent = reciprocal). Sum: 1 + 1/5 = 6/5.
L2 inline_ts ex-2-4
If 2^x = 32 and 3^y = 81, what is x + y?
Explanation: 2⁵ = 32 so x = 5. 3⁴ = 81 so y = 4. Therefore x + y = 9.
L2 inline_ts ex-2-6
Evaluate: 27^(1/3)
Explanation: 1/3 exponent means cube root: ∛27 = 3 since 3³ = 27.
L2 inline_ts ex-2-7
Simplify: (2x⁻²)(4x³)
Explanation: 2×4=8; x⁻²×x³ = x^(−2+3) = x. Result: 8x.
L2 inline_ts ex-2-8
Simplify: (x⁴)^(1/2) (assume x ≥ 0)
Explanation: Multiply exponents: x^(4×1/2) = x².
L2 inline_ts ex-2-9
Simplify: (a²b³) / (a⁵b)
Explanation: a²/a⁵ = a⁻³; b³/b = b². Result: b²/a³.
L3 inline_ts ex-3-2
If 4^(x+1) = 8^x, what is x?
Explanation: Rewrite as powers of 2: 2^(2x+2) = 2^(3x). Set exponents equal: 2x+2 = 3x → x = 2.
L3 inline_ts ex-3-3
Simplify: (x³ · x^(−1/2)) / x^(1/2)
Explanation: Numerator: x^(3−1/2) = x^(5/2). Divide by x^(1/2): x^(5/2−1/2) = x² .
L3 inline_ts ex-3-5
Simplify: (x^(1/3))⁶
Explanation: Multiply exponents: (1/3)×6 = 2. Result: x².
L3 inline_ts ex-3-6
If 2^n = 64, what is 2^(n − 2)?
Explanation: 64 = 2⁶ so n = 6. 2^(6−2) = 2⁴ = 16.
L3 inline_ts ex-3-7
Solve for x: 3^(x + 1) = 27^(x − 2)
Explanation: 27 = 3³, so 3^(x+1) = 3^(3x−6) → x+1 = 3x−6 → 7 = 2x → x = 7/2.
L3 inline_ts ex-3-8
Simplify: 4^(3/2)
Explanation: 4^(3/2) = (√4)³ = 2³ = 8.
L3 inline_ts ex-3-9
Simplify: (8x⁶)^(2/3)
Explanation: 8^(2/3) = (∛8)² = 4; (x⁶)^(2/3) = x⁴. Result: 4x⁴.

Exponential Functions

core · 1 questions · exponential_functions
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_822
Which of the following is most likely to be the graph of a exponential function?
  • A. ![A curved graph that starts just above the x-axis on the left and travels right and very gradually up. Just before it reaches the y-axis it travels more steeply up and right, going through a point on the y-axis above the origin and continually steeply rising up and right.]()
  • B. ![A graph with two curves, in the top right and bottom left quadrants. The top right curves starts up and right of the origin and travels steeply down and right, it then levels off to travel alongside the x-axis without touching it. The bottom left curve starts down and left of the origin, travels steeply up and left, it then levels off just below the x-axis without touching it.]()
  • C. ![A curved graph that starts in the top left, travels steeply down and right, turns to travel steeply up and right, to the origin, where it turns around to travel down and right again.]()
  • D. ![A curved graph, an upside down symmetrical U shape. It starts in the bottom left, travels steeply up and right, turns at a point on the y-axis above the origin and travels steeply right and down.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A curved graph that starts just above the x-axis on the left and travels right and very gradually up. Just before it reaches the y-axis it travels more steeply up and right, going through a point on the y-axis above the origin and continually steeply rising up and right.](). A common mistake here: Confuses reciprocal and exponential graphs. Double-check each step in recogn…
⚠ Common trap: Confuses reciprocal and exponential graphs

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1050

  • A. 2(9 a-6)
  • B. 18(a-6)
  • C. 6(3 a-2)
  • D. 9(2 a-1)
Explanation: The correct answer is 6(3 a-2). A common mistake here: Believes that they can fully factorise an expression by taking out any common factor, not just the HCF. Double-check each step in factorise a single bracket containing a linear expression by taking out a single numeric common factor (e.g. 6) to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that they can fully factorise an expression by taking out any common factor, n...

  • D. Does not factorise
Explanation: The correct answer is p(p+100). A common mistake here: When factorising a quadratic without a non variable term, tries to double bracket factorise.
