Which of the following equals 12 x 2 y 3 z 4 – 30 xy 2 + 24 x 2 y 5 z ?
  • A. 6 xy 2 (2 yz 3 – 5 + 4 xy 3 )
  • B. 6 xy 2 (2 xyz 4 – 5 + 4 xy 3 z )
  • C. 6 xy 2 z (2 xyz 3 – 5 + 4 xy 3 )
  • D. 12 x 2 y 3 ( yz 4 – 5 + 2 y 2 z )
  • E. 12 x 2 y 3 z ( xy 2 z 3 – 5 z + 2 y 2 )
Explanation: Factoring Polynomials → find common coefficient and variables across all terms → GCF is 6xy² → divide each term by 6xy² → remaining expression is 2xyz⁴ - 5 + 4xy³z.

  • A. (x+4)(x+9) (x+4)(x+9)
  • B. (x+3)(x+12) (x+3)(x+12)
  • C. (x+1)(x+36) (x+1)(x+36)
  • D. (x+7)(x+18) (x+7)(x+18)
Explanation: The correct answer is (x+3)(x+12) (x+3)(x+12). A common mistake here: Believes that when factorising a quadratic, they can choose any two numbers that multiply to give the constant. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² + bx + c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that when factorising a quadratic, they can choose any two numbers that multip...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_474
Factorise the following expression x^2+6 x+5
  • A. (x+2)(x+3)
  • B. Cannot be factorised
  • C. (x+1)(x+5)
  • D. x(x+6)+5
Explanation: The correct answer is (x+1)(x+5). A common mistake here: Does not know that to factorise a quadratic expression, to find two numbers that add to give the coefficient of the x term, and multiply to give the non variable term . Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² + bx + c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know that to factorise a quadratic expression, to find two numbers that add to...

  • A. 1 × 32

  • C. 4 × 8
  • D. none of these
Explanation: The correct answer is 1 × 32. A common mistake here: Believes that when factorising a quadratic, they can choose any two numbers that multiply to give the constant. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² + bx + c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that when factorising a quadratic, they can choose any two numbers that multip...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_528
Which multiplication will help factorise x^2+13 x+30 ?
  • A. 1 × 30
  • B. 2 × 15
  • C. 3 × 10
  • D. 5 × 6
Explanation: The correct answer is 3 × 10. A common mistake here: Believes that when factorising a quadratic, they can choose any two numbers that multiply to give the constant. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² + bx + c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that when factorising a quadratic, they can choose any two numbers that multip...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_578
Factorise this expression, if possible: p^2+2 p+3
  • A. (p+2)(p+1)
  • B. (p+3)(p+1)
  • C. (p+3)(p-1)
  • D. Does not factorise
Explanation: The correct answer is Does not factorise. A common mistake here: Believes the constant in an expanded quadratic comes from adding the two numbers in the brackets. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² + bx + c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the constant in an expanded quadratic comes from adding the two numbers in the...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_929
When factorising x^2+6 x+8 we need two numbers that:
  • A. Add to give 6 and multiply to give 8
  • B. Add to give 6 x and multiply to give 8
  • C. Add to give 8 and multiply to give 6
  • D. Add to give 8 x and multiply to give 6
Explanation: The correct answer is Add to give 6 and multiply to give 8. A common mistake here: When factorising into double brackets, believes the sum of the two values in the brackets equals bx rather than b. Double-check each step in factorise a quadratic expression in the form x² + bx + c to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When factorising into double brackets, believes the sum of the two values in the bracke...
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1185
y= x^2+8x+2 Write in the form (x+a)^2+b
  • A. (x+4)^2-14
  • B. (x+4)^2+2
  • C. (x+8)^2+2
  • D. (x+4)^2-16
Explanation: The correct answer is (x+4)^2-14. A common mistake here: When completing the square in the form (x - a)^2 + b, believes b = the original constant. Double-check each step in complete the square for expressions that end up in the form (x + a)² + b to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When completing the square in the form (x - a)^2 + b, believes b = the original constant
L3 eedi word_problem eedi_502
Jo says when she is factorising x^2-4 x she tries to think of two numbers that multiply together to give -4 and add together to give 0 Paul says when he is factorising x^2-4/25 he tries to think of two numbers that multiply together to give -4/25 and add together to give 2/25 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: When factorising into double brackets, believes the sum of the two values in the brackets equals bx rather than b. Double-check each step in recognise when it is possible to factorise an expression into a double bracket to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When factorising into double brackets, believes the sum of the two values in the bracke...

  • A. (1,4)
  • B. (1,-4)
  • C. (-1,4)
  • D. (-1,-4)
Explanation: The correct answer is (1,4). A common mistake here: Believes that in completed square form, they need to change the signs of both values to get the turning point. Fractions and Decimals

foundational · 172 questions · fractions_decimals
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1002

  • A. 0.15
  • B. 0.3
  • C. 3.20
  • D. 0.12
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.15. A common mistake here: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using only the numerator after the decimal point. Double-check each step in convert fractions less than 1 to terminating decimals of 2 decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using only the numerator after the decimal point

  • A. 0.5
  • B. 0.3
  • C. 0.35
  • D. None of these.

⚠ Common trap: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using the denominator after the decimal point

  • A. 0.24

  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct

⚠ Common trap: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using only the numerator after the decimal point

  • B. 4/20
  • C. 7/20
  • D. 17/20

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1537
Tom and Katie are discussing how to order decimals Tom says 0.06 is greater than 0.006 Katie says 0.5 is greater than 0.6087 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Thinks the more digits a number has the greater it is, regardless of place value. Double-check each step in order decimals with a different number of digits to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the more digits a number has the greater it is, regardless of place value
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1548
Which diagram represents the largest fraction?
  • A. ![An equilateral triangle divided into 4 smaller equilateral triangles that are identical to each other in size and shape. 2 of the smaller triangles are shaded orange, the rest are white.]()
  • B. ![A rectangle divided horizontally into 3 smaller rectangles that are identical to each other in size and shape. The top rectangle, of the 3, is shaded orange, the rest are white.]()
  • C. ![A rectangle divided into a grid of 30 smaller rectangles (5 high by 6 wide) that are identical to each other in size and shape. 8 of these smaller rectangles are shaded orange, the rest are white.]()
  • D. ![A rectangle divided horizontally into 3 smaller rectangles that are identical to each other in size and shape. The top 2 rectangles, of the 3, are shaded orange, the other is white.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A rectangle divided horizontally into 3 smaller rectangles that are identical to each other in size and shape. The top 2 rectangles, of the 3, are shaded orange, the other is white.](). A common mistake here: Thinks that a larger shaded area represents a larger fraction. Double-check each step in order fractions whose denominators are not multiples of the same number to a…
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that a larger shaded area represents a larger fraction
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_156
Write the following fraction in its simplest form: 6 3/18
  • A. 3 1/2
  • B. 6 1/6
  • C. 2 1/6
  • D. Cannot be simplified
Explanation: The correct answer is 6 1/6. A common mistake here: Simplifies integers when simplifying a mixed number. Double-check each step in simplify fractions that simplify to a unit fraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Simplifies integers when simplifying a mixed number
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1582
Calculate: 1/9 × 1/5
  • A. 1/45
  • B. 2/14
  • C. 5/9
  • D. 1/14
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/45. A common mistake here: When multiplying fractions, adds the numerators and the denominators. Double-check each step in multiply proper fractions in the form: fraction × fraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When multiplying fractions, adds the numerators and the denominators
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1587
What fraction is the arrow pointing to? (Diagram: An image of a numberline with 5 dashes. On the leftmost dash is the number 1/5. On the rightmost dash is the number 3/5. An arrow points to the 4th dash from the left)
  • A. 1/2
  • B. 2/5
  • C. 3/4
  • D. There is no such fraction
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/2. A common mistake here: Does not realise you can use equivalent fractions to break fractions up into smaller divisions. Double-check each step in read a fraction on a scale where the required number is marked by a dash between two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise you can use equivalent fractions to break fractions up into smaller di...
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1592
What fraction of the rectangle is shaded? (Diagram: Rectangle split into 100 equal parts with 27 parts shaded in yellow)
  • A. 27/100
  • B. 27/73
  • C. 27
  • D. 73/100
Explanation: The correct answer is 27/100. A common mistake here: Denominator used for other parts rather than total parts. Double-check each step in identify non-unit fractions when part of a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Denominator used for other parts rather than total parts
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1605
0.2 × 0.8=
  • A. 0.28
  • B. 1.6
  • C. 0.16
  • D. 1
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.16. A common mistake here: When multiplying just writes the numbers next to each other. Double-check each step in multiply two decimals together with the same number of decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When multiplying just writes the numbers next to each other
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1612
0.51-0.202=
  • A. 0.151
  • B. 0.312
  • C. 0.308
  • D. 0.318
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.308. A common mistake here: Believes subtraction is commutative . Double-check each step in subtract decimals where the numbers involved have a different number of decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes subtraction is commutative
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1640
1/6 × 6=?
  • A. 1
  • B. 6/36
  • C. 1/36
  • D. 6
Explanation: The correct answer is 1. A common mistake here: When multiplying a fraction by an integer, multiplies both numerator and denominator by the integer. Double-check each step in multiply proper fractions in the form: fraction × integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When multiplying a fraction by an integer, multiplies both numerator and denominator by...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1657
What is 0.03 written as a percentage?
  • A. 3 %
  • B. 30 %
  • C. 0.03 %
  • D. 0.3 %
Explanation: The correct answer is 3 %. A common mistake here: Multiplied by 1000 instead of 100. Double-check each step in convert decimals less than 1 with 2 decimal place to percentages to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Multiplied by 1000 instead of 100
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1684
5.39-2.1=
  • A. 3.38
  • B. 7.49
  • C. 5.18
  • D. 3.29
Explanation: The correct answer is 3.29. A common mistake here: Hasn't lined up the place value columns when writing out the calculation. Double-check each step in subtract decimals where the numbers involved have a different number of decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Hasn't lined up the place value columns when writing out the calculation
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1685
Samia has 12 cakes to share between 5 people. What fraction of a cake does each person get?
  • A. 12/5
  • B. 5/12
  • C. 1/5
  • D. 1/12
Explanation: The correct answer is 12/5. A common mistake here: Mixes up the numerator and denominator when writing a division as a fraction. Double-check each step in express a division as a fraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up the numerator and denominator when writing a division as a fraction
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1686
How should you line up the digits to calculate 7-0.612 ?
  • A. ![A column subtraction written as 7.000 - 0.612 with the decimal points lined up.]()
  • B. ![A column subtraction written as 0.007 - 0.612 with the decimal points lined up.]()
  • C. ![A column subtraction written as 0.700 - 0.612 with the decimal points lined up.]()
  • D. ![A column subtraction written as 7.0 - 0.612 The 7 is above the 6, the decimal point in 7.0 is above the 1 and the 0 of 7.0 is above the 2.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A column subtraction written as 7.000 - 0.612 with the decimal points lined up.](). A common mistake here: Does not know the units column is before the decimal point. Double-check each step in subtract decimals from integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the units column is before the decimal point
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1689
2 4/5-1 2/5= Leave your answer as a mixed number
  • A. 1 2/5
  • B. 7/5
  • C. 7/0
  • D. 1 2/0
Explanation: The correct answer is 1 2/5. A common mistake here: Confused mixed numbers with improper fractions. Double-check each step in subtract fractions involving mixed number fractions with the same denominator to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confused mixed numbers with improper fractions
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1690
A group of 8 friends share $6 equally. What fraction of the money do they each get?
  • A. 1/8
  • B. 3/4
  • C. 1/6
  • D. 8/6
Explanation: The correct answer is 3/4. A common mistake here: Thinks the fraction 1/n can express sharing any number of items between n people. Double-check each step in express one quantity as a fraction of another to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the fraction 1/n can express sharing any number of items between n people
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1691

  • A. 33/40
  • B. 17/40
  • C. 4/3
  • D. 1/10
Explanation: The correct answer is 17/40. A common mistake here: Adds instead of subtracts. Double-check each step in subtract proper fractions with different denominators which do not share a common factor to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds instead of subtracts
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1693
5/15=
  • A. 1/5
  • B. 0.5
  • C. 33 1/3 %
  • D. A and B
Explanation: The correct answer is 33 1/3 %. A common mistake here: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using only the numerator after the decimal point. Double-check each step in convert fractions to percentages containing 2 or more decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Converts a fraction to a decimal by using only the numerator after the decimal point

  • A. Chocolate
  • B. Vanilla
  • C. Lemon
  • D. There is the same amount of each.

  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct

By comparing the fraction of the rectangle that is shaded in each diagram, decide which is the odd one out?
  • A. ![A rectangle with 3/4 shaded in red]()
  • B. ![A rectangle with 6/8 shaded in blue]()
  • C. ![A rectangle with 8/12 shaded in purple]()
  • D. ![A rectangle with 12/16 shaded in orange]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A rectangle with 8/12 shaded in purple](). A common mistake here: Does not see fraction diagrams as equivalent if the shading is in a different pattern. Double-check each step in identify equivalent fractions when presented as shapes or objects to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not see fraction diagrams as equivalent if the shading is in a different pattern

0.15+0.2=
  • A. 0.35
  • B. 0.17
  • C. 0.37
  • D. 0.152
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.35. A common mistake here: When adding decimals with a different number of decimal places, lines up the digits incorrectly. Double-check each step in add decimals where the numbers involved have a different number of decimal places to avoid this error.

  • A. 99/20
  • B. 4 19/20

  • D. 4 8/14

  • B. 2 1/3

⚠ Common trap: Ignores the negative sign
L1 eedi diagram eedi_54
What fraction of the rectangle is shaded? (Diagram: A rectangle split into 10 equal parts with 3 parts shaded in yellow)
  • A. 7/10
  • B. 3
  • C. 3/7
  • D. 3/10
Explanation: The correct answer is 3/10. A common mistake here: Does not know how to write a fraction - just given an integer answer. Double-check each step in identify non-unit fractions when part of a shape to avoid this error.

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_541
What sign would make this statement true? 5/9 ? 7/12
  • A. >
  • B. <
  • C. =
  • D. ≈

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_568

  • A. 7

⚠ Common trap: Thinks finding a fraction of an amount means subtracting from that amount
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_598
Write 0.15 as a percentage.
  • A. 0.15 %
  • B. 15/100
  • C. 1.5 %
  • D. 15 %
Explanation: The correct answer is 15 %. A common mistake here: Thinks you need to just add a % sign to a decimal to convert to a percentage. Double-check each step in convert decimals less than 1 with 2 decimal place to percentages to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks you need to just add a % sign to a decimal to convert to a percentage
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_60

  • A. 1 16/21
  • B. 1 1/3
  • C. 3 1/3
  • D. Cannot be simplified

⚠ Common trap: Simplifies integers when simplifying a mixed number

3/5 of 30=
  • A. 6
  • B. 50
  • C. 12
  • D. 18

⚠ Common trap: Finds the unit fraction when asked to find a non-unit fraction of an amount
L1 eedi number_line eedi_61

  • A. 3/8
  • B. 5/8
  • C. 1/2
  • D. None of these

The left hand section of these is colored green and is further divided into 3 equal sections with vertical dotted lines. A curly bracket shows that the width of one of these green sections is denoted by a question mark.)
  • A. 3/6 × 1/3
  • B. 1/4 ÷ 1/3
  • C. 3/6 ÷ 3
  • D. 1/4 × 1/3

⚠ Common trap: Does not understand parts must be equal in a fraction diagram

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_649
Tom and Katie are discussing multiplying decimals. Tom says 5 × 3.9=3 × 5.9 Katie says 5 × 3.9=3.9 × 5 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Does not correctly apply the commutative property of multiplication. Double-check each step in multiply a decimal by an integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not correctly apply the commutative property of multiplication

  • B. Only Paul

  • C. 9/33
  • D. 1/11
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/11. A common mistake here: when dividing a fraction by an integer, multiplies the numerator by the integer. Double-check each step in divide proper fractions in the form: fraction ÷ integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: when dividing a fraction by an integer, multiplies the numerator by the integer
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1659
4 ÷ 2 4/5 Which of the following is a correct way to write the answer to this calculation?
  • A. 1 3/7
  • B. 2 1/5
  • C. 1 2/5
  • D. 7/10
Explanation: The correct answer is 1 3/7. A common mistake here: Believes division is commutative . Double-check each step in divide mixed number fraction in the form: integer ÷ fraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes division is commutative
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1714
Calculate: 6/9 ÷ 3
  • A. 6/3
  • B. 2/18
  • C. 2/9
  • D. 2/3
Explanation: The correct answer is 2/9. A common mistake here: When dividing a fraction by an integer, divides the denominator by the integer. Double-check each step in divide proper fractions in the form: fraction ÷ integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When dividing a fraction by an integer, divides the denominator by the integer
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1842
Calculate: 2/5 ÷ 2
  • A. 1/5
  • B. 1/10
  • C. 4/5
  • D. 4/10
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/5. A common mistake here: when dividing a fraction by an integer, multiplies the numerator by the integer. Double-check each step in divide proper fractions in the form: fraction ÷ integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: when dividing a fraction by an integer, multiplies the numerator by the integer
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1851
0.5=1/2=? %
  • A. 12 %
  • B. 8 %
  • C. 2 %
  • D. 50 %
Explanation: The correct answer is 50 %. A common mistake here: Converts a fraction to a percentage by writing the numerator followed by the denominator. Double-check each step in solve problems involving converting between fractions, decimals and percentages to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Converts a fraction to a percentage by writing the numerator followed by the denominator

  • A. 1 1/3
  • B. 1 2/3
  • C. 2 1/3
  • D. 3/4

Double-check each step in divide mixed number fractions in the form: fraction ÷ integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When the dividend is a mixed number and the divisor is an integer, thinks they only per...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_337
Jo and Paul are arguing about percentages. Jo says 52 %=0.052 Paul says 52 %<20/41 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Divides by 1000 instead of 100 when converting a percentage to a decimal. Double-check each step in solve problems involving converting between fractions, decimals and percentages to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Divides by 1000 instead of 100 when converting a percentage to a decimal

L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_476

  • A. 7 2/100
  • B. 1/72
  • C. 18/25
  • D. None of these options
Explanation: The correct answer is 18/25. A common mistake here: Only reads the percentage symbol as applying to the units digit. Double-check each step in convert two digit integer percentages to fractions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Only reads the percentage symbol as applying to the units digit

Calculate 1/6 ÷ 3
  • A. 2
  • B. 1/18
  • C. 18
  • D. 1/2

⚠ Common trap: When dividing fractions, does the reciprocal of the first fraction instead of the second
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_6

  • A. 62/10
  • B. 31/50
  • C. 6/2
  • D. None of these
Explanation: The correct answer is 31/50. A common mistake here: Does not understand a percentage is out of 100. Double-check each step in convert two digit integer percentages to fractions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand a percentage is out of 100
L2 eedi diagram eedi_617

  • A. 6/8
  • B. 6
  • C. 6/3
  • D. 6/4

⚠ Common trap: Thinks you just add a % sign to the numerator to make a fraction into a percentage

L2 eedi diagram eedi_786
What fraction replaces the question mark? (Diagram: A bar split into 7 equal pieces. 3 are shaded, and one of these is labelled with a question mark.)
  • A. 1/3
  • B. 1/4
  • C. 1/6

function_transformations

· 6 questions · function_transformations
L1 inline_ts ft-1-2
If g(x) = f(x − 3), how is the graph of f transformed?
Explanation: Replacing x with x − 3 shifts the graph right 3 units. Horizontal transformations inside the function work in the opposite-looking direction.
L1 inline_ts ft-1-3
If g(x) = −f(x), how is the graph of f transformed?
Explanation: The negative sign outside f changes every output y to −y. That reflects the graph across the x-axis.
L2 inline_ts ft-2-2
If f(x) = x², which equation represents f shifted left 2 units and up 5 units?
Explanation: A left shift by 2 replaces x with x + 2. An upward shift by 5 adds 5 outside. So y = (x + 2)² + 5.
L2 inline_ts ft-2-3
The graph of y = 3f(x) is related to y = f(x) by which transformation?
Explanation: Multiplying the function output by 3 triples every y-value. This is a vertical stretch by a factor of 3.
L3 inline_ts ft-3-2
Which transformation takes y = |x| to y = −|x − 4| + 6?
Explanation: Inside x − 4 shifts the graph right 4. The negative outside reflects it across the x-axis. The +6 outside shifts it up 6.
L3 inline_ts ft-3-3
If f(x) has vertex (3, −2), what is the vertex of g(x) = 2f(x − 5) + 7?
Explanation: The x − 5 inside shifts the graph right 5, so x-coordinate 3 becomes 8. The output y = −2 is multiplied by 2 and then increased by 7: 2(−2) + 7 = 3. Vertex is (8, 3).

Basics of Functions

core · 85 questions · functions_basics
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t06_q05
If f ( x ) = x 2 + 9 and g ( x ) = 24 + 4 x, what is the value of ?
  • A. 0.75
  • B. 0.8
  • C. 1.2
  • D. 1.25
  • E. 1.75
Explanation: Step 1: Identify the two functions f and g from the problem. Step 2: Evaluate f at the specified input (e.g., f(4)) by substituting the input into the formula for f. Step 3: Evaluate g at the specified input (e.g., g(−1)) by substituting into g. Step 4: Divide the two results: f(4)/g(−1). Step 5: Simplify — result is 5/4 = 1.25 = J.

  • B. 7/3
  • C. 6 2/3

Explanation: The correct answer is 6 2/3. A common mistake here: When finding an input of a function machine thinks you apply the operations given rather than the inverse operation. Double-check each step in for a given output find the input of a function machine to avoid this error.

  • A. ![A function machine showing x as the input then +5 then squaring to give 14 as the output. Then a reverse function machine underneath showing 14 (the output) now as the input square root and then -5]()
  • B. ![A function machine showing 14 as the input then squaring and +5 to give the output]()
  • C. ![A function machine showing x as the input then squaring then +5 to give 14 as the output. Then a reverse function machine underneath showing 14 (the output) now as the input -5 and then square root]()
  • D. ![A function machine showing x as the input then x2 then +5 to give 14 as the output. Then a reverse function machine underneath showing 14 (the output) now as the input -5 and then divide by 2]()

A common mistake here: Does not follow the arrows through a function machine, changes the order of the operations asked. Double-check each step in solve a non-linear equatio…

  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie

  • D. ![A function machine which has 4 parts joined by arrows pointing from left to right. "𝑥" is the first part, written on the left, followed by a horizontal arrow pointing to a rectangle that has "+ 1" written inside it, followed by a horizontal arrow pointing to a rectangle that has "✕ 5" written inside it, followed by a horizontal arrow pointing to "y"]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A function machine which has 4 parts joined by arrows pointing from left to right. "𝑥" is the first part, written on the left, followed by a horizontal arrow pointing to a rectangle that has "+ 1" written inside it, followed by a horizontal arrow pointing to a rectangle that has "✕ 5" written inside it, followed by a horizontal arrow pointing t…
⚠ Common trap: Answers order of operations questions with brackets as if the brackets are not there
L1 eedi diagram eedi_623
Cally wants to use the function machine below to solve this problem: I think of a number, subtract 2, triple it, and the answer is 12 . Which of the following should replace the star? (Diagram: A function machine with the input box blank. The first function box blank. The second function box has a purple star in and the output box is blank.)
  • A. x
  • B. -2
  • C. × 3
  • D. 12
Explanation: The correct answer is × 3. A common mistake here: Does not understand where to place inputs/ operations/ outputs when drawing a function machine. Double-check each step in for a given input find the output of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand where to place inputs/ operations/ outputs when drawing a function...
L1 eedi diagram eedi_712
(Diagram: A function machine with 3 rectangles in a row, joined by arrows pointing from left to right. The first rectangle on the left is empty and says "input" above it. The middle rectangle has "+¼" written inside it and the final rectangle has "output" written above it and "8" written inside it.) What is the input of this function machine?
  • A. 8 1/4
  • B. 7 3/4
  • C. 2
  • D. 32
Explanation: The correct answer is 7 3/4. A common mistake here: When solving a problem that requires an inverse operation (e.g. missing number problems), does the original operation. Double-check each step in for a given output find the input of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When solving a problem that requires an inverse operation (e.g. missing number problems...
L1 eedi diagram eedi_725
(Diagram: Image of a function machine. The function is subtract 3 and the output is 7) What is the input of this function machine?
  • A. 21
  • B. -4
  • C. 4
  • D. 10
Explanation: The correct answer is 10. A common mistake here: Thinks the inverse of subtraction is multiplication. Double-check each step in for a given output find the input of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the inverse of subtraction is multiplication
L1 eedi diagram eedi_745
(Diagram: A function machine with 4 rectangles in a row, joined by arrows pointing from left to right. The first rectangle on the left has "input" written above it and "8" written inside it. The next rectangle has "✕2" written inside it, the next rectangle has "−1" written inside it and the final rectangle on the right is empty and has "output" written above it.) What is the output of this function machine?
  • A. 15
  • B. 5
  • C. 14
  • D. 4.5
Explanation: The correct answer is 15. A common mistake here: When using a function machine, confuses the input and the output. Double-check each step in for a given input find the output of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When using a function machine, confuses the input and the output
L1 eedi diagram eedi_757
(Diagram: A function machine with 3 rectangles in a row, joined by arrows pointing from left to right. The first rectangle on the left is empty and says "input" above it. The middle rectangle has "Square root" written inside it and the final rectangle has "output" written above it and "12" written inside it.) What is the input of this function machine?
  • A. 24
  • B. 6
  • C. √(12)
  • D. 144
Explanation: The correct answer is 144. A common mistake here: Believes the inverse of square rooting is doubling. Double-check each step in calculate the square of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the inverse of square rooting is doubling
L1 eedi diagram eedi_907
(Diagram: A function machine with 3 rectangles in a row, joined by arrows pointing from left to right. The first rectangle on the left is empty and says "input" above it. The middle rectangle has "-4" written inside it and the final rectangle has "output" written above it and "11" written inside it.) What is the input of this function machine?
  • A. 15
  • B. -7
  • C. 4
  • D. 44
Explanation: The correct answer is 15. A common mistake here: Believes subtraction is commutative . Double-check each step in for a given output find the input of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes subtraction is commutative
L1 eedi diagram eedi_911
(Diagram: A function machine with 3 rectangles in a row, joined by arrows pointing from left to right. The first rectangle on the left has "input" written above it and "3m + 1" written inside it. The middle rectangle has "✕ 2" written inside it and the final rectangle is empty and has "output" written above it.) What is the output of this function machine?
  • A. 6 m+2
  • B. 3 m+2
  • C. 6 m+1
  • D. 6 m+3
Explanation: The correct answer is 6 m+2. A common mistake here: Only multiplies second term in the expansion of a bracket. Double-check each step in for a given algebraic input, find the output of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Only multiplies second term in the expansion of a bracket
L1 eedi diagram eedi_972
(Diagram: A function machine with 3 rectangles in a row, joined by arrows pointing from left to right. The first rectangle on the left is empty and says "input" above it. The middle rectangle has "square root" written inside it and the final rectangle has "output" written above it and "16" written inside it.) What is the input of this function machine?
  • A. 32
  • B. 8
  • C. 4
  • D. 256
Explanation: The correct answer is 256. A common mistake here: Believes the inverse of square rooting is doubling. Double-check each step in calculate the square of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the inverse of square rooting is doubling
L1 inline_ts fn-1-10
f(x) = mx + b, m = 2, and f(3) = 7. What is b?
Explanation: 7 = 2(3)+b → 7 = 6+b → b = 1.
L1 inline_ts fn-1-2
What is the domain of f(x) = 1/(x − 3)?
Explanation: Division by zero is undefined, so x − 3 ≠ 0, meaning x ≠ 3.
L1 inline_ts fn-1-3
If g(x) = x² + 1, what is g(−3)?
Explanation: g(−3) = (−3)² + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10. Remember: (−3)² = +9, not −9.
L1 inline_ts fn-1-4
Which function has a range of all real numbers y ≥ 0?
Explanation: x² is always non-negative regardless of x, so its range is y ≥ 0.
L1 inline_ts fn-1-6
If g(x) = x² + 2, what is g(−3)?
Explanation: g(−3) = (−3)²+2 = 9+2 = 11.
L1 inline_ts fn-1-7
Which set of pairs represents y as a function of x?
Explanation: A function needs each x to have exactly one y. Only option B has all distinct x values.
L1 inline_ts fn-1-8
If h(x) = 2x + 1, what is h(a + 1)?
Explanation: h(a+1) = 2(a+1)+1 = 2a+2+1 = 2a+3.
L1 inline_ts fn-1-9
What is the domain of f(x) = √(x − 4)?
Explanation: The radicand must be ≥ 0: x−4 ≥ 0 → x ≥ 4.
L2 act_master word_problem act_math_t02_q08
Anderson has a phone plan that charges a monthly rate of $50 for the first 1,000 minutes plus $0.25 for each additional minute. Which of the following functions models Anderson’s plan for all m > 1,000, with m as the number of minutes per month and f ( m ) as the monthly charge?
  • A. f ( m ) = 0.25 m
  • B. f ( m ) = 0.25 m + 50
  • C. f ( m ) = 0.25 m – 200
  • D. f ( m ) = 0.25 m – 950
  • E. f ( m ) = 0.25 m + 1,000
Explanation: Functions → monthly charge is $50 for first 1000 minutes plus $0.25 for each minute above 1000 → f(m)=50+0.25(m-1000) → simplify to 0.25m-200.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t04_q10
When calculating a certain tax, Simone found that no tax is due for all income up to p dollars, with p > 0. Income greater than p dollars is taxed at a rate of 15%. Which of the following graphs accurately represents this tax?
  • A. E.
Explanation: Step 1: The tax rule has two pieces: (1) income ≤ $10,000 → $0 tax; (2) income > $10,000 → 15% tax on the amount ABOVE $10,000. Step 2: The graph is FLAT at zero for income from $0 to $10,000, then rises linearly for income above $10,000 (slope = 0.15). Step 3: There is no stated maximum (no cap or upper plateau) — the tax keeps increasing linearly for any income above $10,000. Step 4: The corr…
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t11_q09
If g ( x ) is a transformation that moves f ( x ) three units to the right and then reflects it across the x- axis, then g ( x ) =
  • A. f (– x ) + 3
  • B. f (– x ) – 3
  • C. f ( x ) + 3
  • D. – f ( x + 3)
  • E. – f ( x – 3)
Explanation: Step 1: Move f(x) three units to the RIGHT: replace x with (x−3) → f(x−3). Step 2: Reflect across the x-axis: multiply by −1 → −f(x−3). Step 3: g(x) = −f(x−3). Answer: E.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t14_q10
The table below gives the values of two functions, g and h , for various values of x . One of the functions expresses a relationship that can be expressed by the formula a + bx , where a and b are real number coefficients. What is the value of that function for x = 0? x g ( x) h ( x ) -3 4 -2 2 3 -1 1 6 0 1 1 2 15 3 18
  • A. 0
  • B. 0.5
  • C. 1
  • D. 2
  • E. 9
Explanation: Step 1: h(x) is the linear function a+bx. From table: h(-2)=3, h(-1)=6. Step 2: Slope b=(6-3)/(-1-(-2))=3. Step 3: 3=a+3(-2)=a-6 → a=9. Step 4: h(x)=9+3x → h(0)=9 → E.
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1208
This graph shows how far Jodie is from home whilst she is out on a walk. What might the positive sloping section represent? (Diagram: A distance time graph formed by three line segments in the shape of a trapezium. The first line segment is upward sloping and starts at 0. The end of this line segment is connected to a horizontal line that is extending to the right. The third line connects this line to the x axis and has a relatively steep negative gradient.)
  • A. Jodie is accelerating
  • B. Jodie is walking away from home
  • C. Jodie is walking towards home
  • D. Jodie has stopped walking
Explanation: The correct answer is Jodie is walking away from home. A common mistake here: Believes the gradient of a distance-time graph is the acceleration. Double-check each step in interpret sloping linear sections of a displacement-time graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the gradient of a distance-time graph is the acceleration
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1277
The graph can be used to work out how much Kay's company pays her for travel. Kay’s company paid her $ 100 How many miles did she travel? (Diagram: The graph shows distance in miles on the x axis and amount in pounds on the y axis. The graph consists of a straight line, starting at the origin (0,0) and passing through the points (20,16) and (25,20))
  • A. 80
  • B. 100
  • C. 125
  • D. 120
Explanation: The correct answer is 125. A common mistake here: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes. Double-check each step in read values off a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1417
Sunita accelerates quickly, then travels at a constant speed, then slowly comes to rest. Which of the following velocity (v) time (t) graphs is most likely to illustrate Sunita's journey?
  • A. ![A graph with time on the horizontal axis and velocity on the vertical axis. The graph at a point above the origin, travels horizontally right, travels down and right in a straight line, then travels horizontally right again.]()
  • B. ![A graph with time on the horizontal axis and velocity on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin, travels right and up in a steep straight line, travels horizontally right, then travels right and down in a less steep straight line than the start, until it reaches the horizontal axis.]()
  • C. ![A graph with time on the horizontal axis and velocity on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin, travels right and up in a straight line, travels horizontally right, then travels right and up in a more steep straight line than the start.]()
  • D. ![A graph with time on the horizontal axis and distance on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin, travels right and up in a straight line, travels horizontally right, then travels right and down in a more steep straight line than the start, until it reaches the horizontal axis.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A graph with time on the horizontal axis and velocity on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin, travels right and up in a steep straight line, travels horizontally right, then travels right and down in a less steep straight line than the start, until it reaches the horizontal axis.](). A common mistake here: Believes a steeper downwards line on a graph means a s…
⚠ Common trap: Believes a steeper downwards line on a graph means a slower decrease
L2 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_1448
The graph on the right shows the mass of sand in a bucket over time The gradient of the positive sloping section would tell us... (Diagram: A graph with time (secs) on the horizontal axis and mass (g) on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin, travels in a straight line up and right, travels horizontally, then travels in a straight line down and right back to the x-axis, more steeply than the start.)
  • A. The temperature of the sand is rising
  • B. The rate the mass of sand in the bucket is decreasing
  • C. The mass of sand in the bucket
  • D. The rate the mass of sand in the bucket is increasing
Explanation: The correct answer is The rate the mass of sand in the bucket is increasing. A common mistake here: Does not realise that gradient represents rate of change. Double-check each step in interpret linear sections of real life graphs to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise that gradient represents rate of change
L2 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_1464
Jadon decides to go for a run starting from home. The graph on the right shows Jadon's distance in meters (d) over time in seconds (t) . The gradient of the negative sloping section of the graph is 0.5 . Which of the following does that 0.5 best represent? (Diagram: A sketch of a graph with d on the y axis and t on the x axis. A straight line slopes upwards from the origin 2/3 of the way up the graph, 1/5 across the graph. It then becomes horizontal for the next 1/5 of the graph, before sloping downwards in a straight line for the remainder of the graph.)
  • A. Jadon is travelling away from home at a speed of 0.5 m / s
  • B. Jadon is decelerating at a rate of 0.5 m / s^2
  • C. Jadon is accelerating at a rate of 0.5 m / s^2
  • D. Jadon is travelling back home at a speed of 0.5 m / s
Explanation: The correct answer is Jadon is travelling back home at a speed of 0.5 m / s. A common mistake here: Believes a downward slope on a distance-time graph means travelling away. Double-check each step in interpret the gradient on a linear section on a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes a downward slope on a distance-time graph means travelling away
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1515
John walks from home at a constant speed to a bench, stops for a rest, and then runs home at a constant speed. Which of the following displacement (d) time (t) graphs is most likely to illustrate John's journey?
  • A. ![The graph has three sections. The first section is a horizontal line, then a sloped line with negative gradient. The last section is a horizontal line.]()
  • B. ![The graph has three sections. The first section starts at (0, 0) and is a sloped line with steep, positive, uphill gradient, then a horizontal line. The third section is a sloped line with gentle, negative gradient that ends when it touches the horizontal axis.]()
  • C. ![The graph has three sections. The first section starts at (0, 0) and is a sloped line with positive, uphill gradient, then a horizontal line. The third section is another sloped line with a steeper positive gradient.]()
  • D. ![The graph has three sections. The first section starts at (0, 0) and is a sloped line with gentle, positive, uphill gradient, then a horizontal line. The third section is a sloped line with very steep negative gradient that ends when it touches the horizontal axis.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![The graph has three sections. The first section starts at (0, 0) and is a sloped line with gentle, positive, uphill gradient, then a horizontal line. The third section is a sloped line with very steep negative gradient that ends when it touches the horizontal axis.](). A common mistake here: Believes a less steep gradient corresponds to a faster speed. Double-check each st…
⚠ Common trap: Believes a less steep gradient corresponds to a faster speed
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1580
Johan wants to plot a graph to show the distance a ball travels over time. After 2 seconds the ball has travelled 3 meters. Where on the graph should Johan plot this?
  • A. ![A graph with time (sec) on the horizontal axis and distance (m) on the vertical axis. A is plotted above 2 on the horizontal axis and right of 3 on the vertical axis.]()
  • B. ![A graph with time (sec) on the horizontal axis and distance (m) on the vertical axis. B is plotted above 3 on the horizontal axis and right of 2 on the vertical axis.]()
  • C. ![A graph with time (sec) on the horizontal axis and distance (m) on the vertical axis. C is plotted below 3 on the horizontal axis and right of -2 on the vertical axis.]()
  • D. ![A graph with time (sec) on the horizontal axis and distance (m) on the vertical axis. D is plotted below 2 on the horizontal axis and right of -3 on the vertical axis.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A graph with time (sec) on the horizontal axis and distance (m) on the vertical axis. A is plotted above 2 on the horizontal axis and right of 3 on the vertical axis.](). A common mistake here: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes. Double-check each step in given a situation sketch an appropriate real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1595
This graph shows the position of a ball, relative to a fixed point, over time. Approximately how far away from its starting point is the ball after 8 seconds? (Diagram: A graph showing time (secs) on the horizontal axis and position (m) on the vertical axis. The graph starts at (0,6) travels in a straight line to (8,15), then a straight line to (16,-15) then a straight line to (24,0).)
  • A. 2 m
  • B. 9 m
  • C. 14.5 m
  • D. 15 m
Explanation: The correct answer is 9 m. A common mistake here: Assumes that a graph always starts at the origin. Double-check each step in read values off a displacement-time graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Assumes that a graph always starts at the origin
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1631
Tom and Katie are discussing graphs that show the volume of water (V) flowing from a kitchen tap over time (t). Tom says this graph shows water flowing at a constant rate (Diagram: A sketch of a graph showing V on the y axis and t on the x axis. A line starts at the origin and slopes upwards getting gradually steeper.) Katie says this graph shows water flowing at a constant rate (Diagram: A sketch of a graph showing V on the y axis and t on the x axis. A horizontal straight line goes from halfway up the y axis straight across the graph.) Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Believes a curve can show a constant rate. Double-check each step in compare real life graphs to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes a curve can show a constant rate
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1787
Imran slept really well until a car alarm woke him up and he couldn't get back to sleep. Which of the following sketches best shows Imran's sleep?
  • A. ![This graph shows depth of sleep increasing quickly and then maintaining a consistent level until it starts to gradually decrease until the end of the night when it reaches 0.]()
  • B. ![This graph shows depth of sleep increasing quickly and then maintaining a consistent level until it suddenly drops all the way to 0 and stays there for the rest of the night.]()
  • C. ![This graph shows depth of sleep staying around 0 for some time before gradually increasing, maintaining a consistent level for a short period and then gradually decreasing until the end of the night.]()
  • D. ![This graph shows depth of sleep staying at 0 for some time before gradually increasing, maintaining a consistent level for a short period and then gradually decreasing until the end of the night.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![This graph shows depth of sleep increasing quickly and then maintaining a consistent level until it suddenly drops all the way to 0 and stays there for the rest of the night.](). A common mistake here: Believes infinite gradient is not possible in real life. . Double-check each step in given a situation sketch an appropriate real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes infinite gradient is not possible in real life
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1838
The cost of a taxi-ride is shown on the graph. What is the cost per mile? (Diagram: The graph is Cost ($) on the vertical axis and Distance (miles) on the horizontal axis. The graph is a straight line from (0, 2) to (5, 6))
  • A. $6
  • B. $ 1.20
  • C. $ 1
  • D. $ 0.80
Explanation: The correct answer is $ 0.80. A common mistake here: Believes change in y for gradient is always calculated from the origin, regardless of the y-intercept of the line. Double-check each step in calculate the gradient on a linear section on a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes change in y for gradient is always calculated from the origin, regardless of t...
L2 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_1843
Jadon decides to go for a run. The graph on the right shows Jadon's speed in meters per second (s) over time in seconds (t) . The gradient of the negative sloping section of the graph is 0.8 . Which of the following does that 0.8 best represent? (Diagram: A sketch of a graph with s on the y axis and t on the x axis. A straight line slopes upwards from the origin 2/3 of the way up the graph, 1/5 across the graph. It then becomes horizontal for the next 1/5 of the graph, before sloping downwards in a straight line for the remainder of the graph.)
  • A. Jadon is travelling at a speed of 0.8 m / s
  • B. Jadon is decelerating at a rate of 0.8 m / s^2
  • C. Jadon is resting for 0.8 seconds
  • D. Jadon is accelerating at a rate of 0.8 m / s^2
Explanation: The correct answer is Jadon is decelerating at a rate of 0.8 m / s^2. A common mistake here: Believes the gradient of a speed-time graph is the speed. Double-check each step in interpret the gradient on a linear section on a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the gradient of a speed-time graph is the speed
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1850
This displacement-time graph shows 10 seconds of Mo's journey home. During that time he travelled at a speed of 15 m / s . What distance should replace the star? (Diagram: The graph has its vertical axis labelled Distance (m) and it has no scale written except for a star three squares up. The horizontal axis is labelled Time (secs) and has the scale 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 in even steps. The graph is a straight line sloping down from (0, star) to (10, 0).)
  • A. 6 m
  • B. 30 m
  • C. 1.5 m
  • D. 150 m
Explanation: The correct answer is 150 m. A common mistake here: Believes scales on graphs must match on both axes. Double-check each step in calculate distance where speed and time are in the appropriate units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes scales on graphs must match on both axes
L2 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_23
This graph shows how far Fido the dog is from his home. What might the negative-sloping section represent? (Diagram: A graph with time (secs) on the horizontal axis and distance (m) on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin, travels in a straight line up and right, travels horizontally, then travels in a straight line down and right back to the x-axis.)
  • A. Fido is walking home
  • B. Fido has fallen asleep
  • C. Fido is accelerating
  • D. Fido is walking away from home
Explanation: The correct answer is Fido is walking home. A common mistake here: Confuses rest and movement on a distance time graph. Double-check each step in interpret sloping linear sections of a displacement-time graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses rest and movement on a distance time graph
L2 eedi diagram eedi_33
Water is poured into a glass at a constant rate. This graph shows how the depth (d) of water in a glass changes over time (t) . Which of the following glasses is this most likely to be the graph of? (Diagram: A depth-time graph which is a curve starting at (0, 0), with a steep gradient to begin with but then the gradient decreases and the graph levels off to almost horizontal.)
  • A. ![A cylinder sitting on one of its circular faces.]()
  • B. ![A wide cylinder with a narrow cylinder on top.]()
  • C. ![A narrow cylinder with a wide cylinder on top.]()
  • D. ![A shape that looks like a beaker. It has a circular base, with sloped edges up to a larger circular face at the top.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A shape that looks like a beaker. It has a circular base, with sloped edges up to a larger circular face at the top.](). A common mistake here: Does not interpret a sloping gradient as a change in rate. Double-check each step in given a situation sketch an appropriate real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not interpret a sloping gradient as a change in rate
L2 eedi diagram eedi_40
Lethna pays at one rate for the first 100 units of electricity she uses. She pays at a different rate for all the other units of electricity she uses. How much must she pay for using a total of 1000 units of electricity? (Diagram: A graph showing how the cost changes for different numbers of units. The first 100 units cost $0.25 per unit. Then the rate changes to $0.10 per unit.)
  • A. $ 130
  • B. $ 250
  • C. $ 115
  • D. $ 175
Explanation: The correct answer is $ 115. A common mistake here: Thinks terms in linear sequence are in direct proportion. Double-check each step in read values off a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks terms in linear sequence are in direct proportion
L2 eedi diagram eedi_438
This velocity-time graph shows the first 5 seconds of Mo's journey which starts at home. At what time did he first start travelling at 4 m / s ? (Diagram: The image is of a graph. Its vertical axis labelled Velocity (m/s) and has the scale 0, 2, 4, 6 marked in even steps. Its horizontal axis is labelled Time (secs) and has the scale 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 marked in even steps. The graph is in three sections. The first section is a straight slanted line from (0,0) to (1, 4). The second section is a horizontal line from (1, 4) to (3, 4). The third section is a slanted line from (3, 4) to (5, 0).)
  • A. 1 sec
  • B. 2 sec
  • C. 3 secs
  • D. 4 secs
Explanation: The correct answer is 1 sec. A common mistake here: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes. Double-check each step in read values off a speed-time graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes
L2 eedi diagram eedi_632
Lethna pays at one rate for the first 100 units of electricity she uses. She pays at a different rate for all the other units of electricity she uses. How much must she pay for using a total of 800 units of electricity? (Diagram: A graph showing how the cost changes for different numbers of units. The first 100 units cost $0.25 per unit. Then the rate changes to $0.10 per unit.)
  • A. $ 110
  • B. $ 95
  • C. $ 200
  • D. $ 140
Explanation: The correct answer is $ 95. A common mistake here: Thinks terms in linear sequence are in direct proportion. Double-check each step in read values off a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks terms in linear sequence are in direct proportion
L2 eedi diagram eedi_707
(Diagram: A linear graph showing that 10 miles = $8.) The graph can be used to work out how much Kay's company pays her for travel. Kay's company paid her $ 80 How many miles did she travel?
  • A. 96
  • B. 100
  • C. 64
  • D. 80
Explanation: The correct answer is 100. A common mistake here: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes. Double-check each step in read values off a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes
L2 eedi diagram eedi_959
This graph shows how far Jodie is from home whilst she is out on a walk. What might the horizontal represent? (Diagram: The image is a distance-time graph. The graph is in three sections. The first section starts at (0,0) and is a sloped line with positive gradient. The second section is a horizontal line. The third section is a sloped line with negative gradient that ends when it touches the Time axis.)
  • A. Jodie is accelerating
  • B. Jodie is walking away from home
  • C. Jodie is walking towards home
  • D. Jodie has stopped walking
Explanation: The correct answer is Jodie has stopped walking. A common mistake here: Confuses rest and movement on a distance time graph. Double-check each step in interpret horizontal sections of a displacement-time graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses rest and movement on a distance time graph
L2 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_999
The graph on the right shows the mass of sand in a bucket over time What might the horizontal section represent? (Diagram: A graph with time (secs) on the horizontal axis and mass (g) on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin, travels in a straight line up and right, travels horizontally, then travels in a straight line down and right back to the x-axis, more steeply than the start.)
  • A. The temperature of the sand is falling
  • B. Sand is being added to the bucket
  • C. Sand is being tipped out
  • D. The bucket is full
Explanation: The correct answer is The bucket is full. A common mistake here: Believes a horizontal line can show a constant rate of change. Double-check each step in interpret linear sections of real life graphs to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes a horizontal line can show a constant rate of change
L2 inline_ts fn-2-10
If f(x + 1) = 3x + 7, what is f(x)?
Explanation: Let u = x+1, so x = u−1. f(u) = 3(u−1)+7 = 3u+4. Replace u→x: f(x) = 3x+4.
L2 inline_ts fn-2-2
If f(x) = x² − 4x + 3 and f(a) = 0, what are the possible values of a?
Explanation: Set x² − 4x + 3 = 0 → factor: (x−1)(x−3) = 0 → x = 1 or x = 3.
L2 inline_ts fn-2-3
The function g(x) = f(x − 3) represents what transformation of f?
Explanation: Replacing x with (x − 3) shifts the graph horizontally 3 units to the right.
L2 inline_ts fn-2-4
If f(x) = 3x + 5 and f(g(x)) = x, what is g(x)?
Explanation: g is the inverse of f. Swap x and y in y = 3x+5: x = 3y+5 → y = (x−5)/3.
L2 inline_ts fn-2-6
For what value of x is f(x) = (x + 1)/(x − 3) undefined?
Explanation: Undefined when denominator = 0: x−3 = 0 → x = 3.
L2 inline_ts fn-2-7
f(x) = 2x + 5 and f(a) = 13. What is a?
Explanation: 2a+5 = 13 → 2a = 8 → a = 4.
L2 inline_ts fn-2-8
For f(x) = x² with domain {−2, −1, 0, 1, 2}, what is the range?
Explanation: Outputs: 4,1,0,1,4. Unique values: {0, 1, 4}.
L2 inline_ts fn-2-9
What are the zeros of f(x) = x² − 3x?
Explanation: x²−3x = 0 → x(x−3) = 0 → x = 0 or x = 3.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t01_q15
If f ( x ) = 4 x 2 – 5 x – 5 and g ( x ) = 2 x – 4, what is the value of ?
  • A. 1
  • B. 2
  • C. 1/15
  • D. 4/15
  • E. 8/5
Explanation: Step 1: Evaluate g(6): g(x)=2x−4 → g(6)=2(6)−4=12−4=8. Step 2: Evaluate f(−5): f(x)=4x²−5x−5 → f(−5)=4(−5)²−5(−5)−5. Step 3: Compute each term: 4(25)=100; −5(−5)=+25; −5 stays −5. Step 4: f(−5) = 100+25−5 = 120. Step 5: g(6)/f(−5) = 8/120 = 1/15.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t03_q13
In the real numbers, which of the following is the domain of the function ?
  • A. x ≥ 3
  • B. x > 3
  • C. x < –3
  • D. –3 < x < 3
  • E. x < –3 or x > 3
Explanation: Step 1: f(x) = √(x–3) / (x–3) has TWO domain restrictions: the expression under the square root must be non-negative (x–3≥0), AND the denominator cannot be zero (x–3≠0). Step 2: Combine: x–3≥0 AND x–3≠0 means x–3>0, i.e. x>3.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t04_q15
The equation y = ax b + c produces the following ( x , y ) coordinate pairs: (0, 2), (1, 7), and (2, 42) . What is the value of abc ?
  • A. 10
  • B. 20
  • C. 30
  • D. 40
  • E. 60
Explanation: Step 1: Given f(x) = x², evaluate and sum over consecutive integers: f(1)+f(2)+f(3)+f(4) = 1+4+9+16 = 30.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t07_q15
Let f ( x ) = x 2 + 10 x + 2. If g ( x ) is a transformation that moves f ( x ) both one unit up and one unit to the right, then g ( x ) =
  • A. x 2 + 8 x – 6
  • B. x 2 + 9 x + 3
  • C. x 2 + 10 x – 6
  • D. x 2 + 11 x + 3
  • E. x 2 + 12 x + 6
Explanation: f(x) = x²+10x+2. Shift 1 right: replace x with (x-1). f(x-1) = (x-1)²+10(x-1)+2 = x²-2x+1+10x-10+2 = x²+8x-7. Shift 1 up: add 1. g(x) = x²+8x-7+1 = x²+8x-6.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t08_q12
The following figure shows f ( x ), which includes points L, M, and n plus the line segments and . Which of the following functions is equivalent to f ( x )?
  • A. f ( x + 6)
  • B. f ( x – 6)
  • C. f (6 – x )
  • D. – f ( x + 6)
  • E. – f ( x – 6)
Explanation: Step 1: A function g is defined as g(x) = f(6−x) or the question asks which transformation creates a specific symmetry. Step 2: The reflection of f(x) about the vertical line x = 3 is obtained by replacing x with (6−x): f(6−x). Step 3: Verify: if f is symmetric about x=3, then f(x) = f(6−x) for all x. Step 4: The correct expression is C. f(6−x).
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t08_q15
Jacob works as a lifeguard at a local pool. At the beginning of a 12-hour overnight shift, the pool was full, and Jacob began draining it. After 2 hours, the pool was completely empty. He spent 3 hours cleaning the pool and then began filling it up again. The pool finished filling just as his shift ended. Which of the following graphs accurately describes the amount of water in the pool throughout Jacob’s shift?
  • A. K.
Explanation: Step 1: Read the graph showing pool water volume vs. time over a 2-week period. Step 2: Identify the key features: where volume decreases, stays constant, decreases again. Step 3: Match the graph behavior to the answer choices (verbal descriptions). Step 4: The correct description corresponds to H.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t11_q11
Which of the following is the domain of the function ?
  • A. –3 > x > 3
  • B. –3 ≤ x ≤ 3
  • C. –3 ≤ x < 3
  • D. x < –3 or x < 3
  • E. x ≤ –3 or x ≥ 3
Explanation: Step 1: The denominator √(x²−9) requires x²−9 > 0 (strictly positive — must be defined AND non-zero since it's in the denominator). Step 2: x²−9 > 0 → x² > 9 → |x| > 3 → x < −3 or x > 3. Step 3: The numerator 3−x has no restrictions. Step 4: Domain = {x : x < −3 or x > 3}. Answer: D.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t12_q13
Which of the following functions has a range of f ( x ) ≥ 4?
  • A. f ( x ) =
  • B. x + 4
  • C.
  • D. f ( x ) =
  • E. x – 4
  • F.
  • G. f ( x ) =
  • H. x + 4
  • I. – 4
  • J. f ( x ) =
  • K. x – 4
  • L. + 4
  • M. f ( x ) =
  • N. x – 4
  • O. – 4
Explanation: Step 1: f(x)≥4 means minimum value of f is at least 4. Step 2: For |x-c|+k, minimum is k (occurring at x=c). Step 3: Check each: F:|x+4| min=0 ✗. G:|x-4| min=0 ✗. H:|x+4|-4 min=-4 ✗. J:|x-4|+4 min=4 ✓. K:|x-4|-4 min=-4 ✗. Answer: J.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t13_q11
If f ( x ) = 7 x 2 - 9 x + 4, then f (-3) = ?
  • A. -32
  • B. -2
  • C. 32
  • D. 40
  • E. 94
Explanation: Step 1: f(x)=7x²-9x+4. Substitute x=-3. Step 2: 7(-3)²=7(9)=63. Step 3: -9(-3)=+27. Step 4: +4. Step 5: f(-3)=63+27+4=94 → E.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t15_q12
The table below gives the values of f ( x ) for selected values of x in the function f ( x ) = ( x + 4) 2 - 1, where x and y are both real numbers. x f(x) -7 8 -5 0 -3 0 -1 8 0 15 1 24 For the equation above, which of the following values of x gives the greatest value of f ( x )?
  • A. -4
  • B. -5
  • C. -6
  • D. -7
  • E. -8
Explanation: Step 1: f(x)=(x+4)²-1. f is largest when (x+4)² is largest, i.e., when |x+4| is maximum. Step 2: Choices: F=-4,G=-5,H=-6,J=-7,K=-8. Distances from -4: 0,1,2,3,4. Step 3: K=-8 is farthest: |(-8)+4|=4 → f=16-1=15 (largest) → K.
L3 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_1063
Here is the graph of y=4/x What is the value of y when x=-1 ? (Diagram: A graph with two curves. Y axis from -5 to 5 and x axis from -5 to 5 marked in increments of 1. One curve is in the positive x, positive y quadrant. It starts at the top close to the line x = 1, as we move right it drops very quickly towards the x axis then levels off to travel almost parallel to the line y =1. It goes through (1, 4) (2,2) and (4,1). One curve is in the negative x negative y quadrant. It starts at the top left of the quadrant almost parallel to y = -1, then we move right and it curves downwards towards the y axis then levels off to travel almost parallel to the x = -1 line. It goes through (-4, -1) (-2, -2) and (-1,-4))
  • A. 4
  • B. -4
  • C. 1
  • D. -1
Explanation: The correct answer is -4. A common mistake here: Believes dividing a positive by a negative gives a positive answer. Double-check each step in given an x value, find the corresponding y value from a non-linear graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes dividing a positive by a negative gives a positive answer
L3 eedi word_problem eedi_1081
A taxi company has a $ 1.50 fixed charge and then charges 60 p per mile. Sam correctly sketches a graph to show the cost of different length journeys with the company. Which of the following graphs would best match Sam's sketch?
  • A. ![A graph with distance on the horizontal axis and cost on the vertical axis. The graph starts at a point on the positive horizontal axis and travels horizontally right and upwards in a steep straight line.]()
  • B. ![A graph with distance on the horizontal axis and cost on the vertical axis. The graph starts at a point on the positive vertical axis and travels horizontally right.]()
  • C. ![A graph with distance on the horizontal axis and cost on the vertical axis. The graph starts at the origin and travels in a straight line right and upwards.]()
  • D. ![A graph with distance on the horizontal axis and cost on the vertical axis. The graph starts at a point on the positive vertical axis and travels horizontally right and upwards in a straight line.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A graph with distance on the horizontal axis and cost on the vertical axis. The graph starts at a point on the positive vertical axis and travels horizontally right and upwards in a straight line.](). A common mistake here: Believes a horizontal line can show a constant rate of change. Double-check each step in given a situation sketch an appropriate real life graph to avo…
⚠ Common trap: Believes a horizontal line can show a constant rate of change
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1398
Which of the following is most likely to be the graph of a reciprocal function?
  • A. ![A curved graph that starts just above the x-axis on the left and travels right and very gradually up. Just before it reaches the y-axis it travels more steeply up and right, going through a point on the y-axis above the origin and continually steeply rising up and right. ]()
  • B. ![A graph with two curves, in the top right and bottom left quadrants. The top right curves starts up and right of the origin and travels steeply down and right, it then levels off to travel alongside the x-axis without touching it. The bottom left curve starts down and left of the origin, travels steeply up and left, it then levels off just below the x-axis without touching it.]()
  • C. ![A curved graph that starts in the top left, travels steeply down and right, turns to travel steeply up and right, to the origin, where it turns around to travel down and right again.]()
  • D. ![A curved graph, an upside down symmetrical U shape. It starts in the bottom left, travels steeply up and right, turns at a point on the y-axis above the origin and travels steeply right and down.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A graph with two curves, in the top right and bottom left quadrants. The top right curves starts up and right of the origin and travels steeply down and right, it then levels off to travel alongside the x-axis without touching it. The bottom left curve starts down and left of the origin, travels steeply up and left, it then levels off just below the x-axis without touching …
⚠ Common trap: Confuses reciprocal and exponential graphs
L3 inline_ts fn-3-2
For f(x) = √(2x − 4), what is the minimum value in the domain of f?
Explanation: Square roots require a non-negative input: 2x − 4 ≥ 0 → 2x ≥ 4 → x ≥ 2.
L3 inline_ts fn-3-3
If f(x) = 2x and g(x) = x + 3, what is (f∘g)(x) − (g∘f)(x)?
Explanation: f(g(x)) = 2(x+3) = 2x+6. g(f(x)) = 2x+3. Difference: (2x+6)−(2x+3) = 3.
L3 inline_ts fn-3-5
f(x) = ax²+bx+c, f(0)=3, f(1)=6, f(−1)=2. Find a.
Explanation: f(0)=c=3. f(1)→a+b=3. f(−1)→a−b=−1. Adding: 2a=2 → a=1.
L3 inline_ts fn-3-6
f(x) = x/(x+2) for x ≠ −2. What is f(f(2))?
Explanation: f(2) = 2/4 = 1/2. f(1/2) = (1/2)/(5/2) = 1/5.
L3 inline_ts fn-3-7
f satisfies f(2x) = 2f(x) for all x. If f(3) = 9, what is f(6)?
Explanation: f(6) = f(2×3) = 2×f(3) = 18.
L3 inline_ts fn-3-8
f is even: f(−x) = f(x). If f(3) = 7 and f(2) = 5, what is f(−3) + f(−2)?
Explanation: Even function: f(−3)=f(3)=7, f(−2)=f(2)=5. Sum = 12.
L3 inline_ts fn-3-9
h(x) = f(g(x)), f(x) = x+3, g(x) = x². What is h(−2)?
Explanation: g(−2) = 4. f(4) = 7.

Geometric Transformations

core · 47 questions · geometric_transformations
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1243
Which image completes a symmetric figure using the red line of symmetry? (Diagram: A shape composed of colored squares is drawn on the left side of a vertical line of symmetry)
  • A. ![The shape has been reflected in the line of symmetry, but the colored squares are not in the corresponding places]()
  • B. ![The shape has been translated right by 4 squares so that it is now on the right hand side of the line of symmetry, but has not changed orientation]()
  • C. ![The shape has been reflected in the line of symmetry, The colored squares have been shaded in their corresponding places]()
  • D. ![The shape has been reflected in the line of symmetry, but the colored squares are not in the corresponding places]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![The shape has been reflected in the line of symmetry, The colored squares have been shaded in their corresponding places](). A common mistake here: Fails to reflect across mirror line. Double-check each step in complete a symmetric figure with respect to a specific line of symmetry to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Fails to reflect across mirror line
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1450
(Diagram: A solid yellow equilateral triangle (3 sided polygon with all 3 sides of equal length).) What is the order of rotational symmetry of this equilateral triangle?
  • A. 0
  • B. 1
  • C. 2
  • D. 3
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry. Double-check each step in identify the order of rotational symmetry of a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry
L1 eedi diagram eedi_180
Which image completes a symmetric figure using the red line of symmetry? (Diagram: A figure drawn in the upper half of a box with a red dotted line immediately below.)
  • A. ![The correct completion of the figure given in the question. ]()
  • B. ![An incorrect completion of the figure given in the question. ]()
  • C. ![An incorrect completion of the figure given in the question. ]()
  • D. ![An incorrect completion of the figure given in the question. ]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![The correct completion of the figure given in the question. ](). A common mistake here: Translates rather than reflects across a line of symmetry. Double-check each step in complete a symmetric figure with respect to a specific line of symmetry to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Translates rather than reflects across a line of symmetry

  • A. ![Trapezium]()
  • B. ![Irregular quadrilateral]()
  • C. ![Parallelogram]()
  • D. They each have the same order of rotational symmetry
Explanation: The correct answer is ![Parallelogram](). A common mistake here: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry. Double-check each step in identify the order of rotational symmetry of a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry
L1 eedi diagram eedi_544
(Diagram: A rectangle divided into 3 equal horizontal sections. The middle section is white and the other 2 sections are blue. In the centre of the white section are five 5-pointed blue stars in the same formation as the 5 dots on a dice. All the stars are in exactly the same orientation, with one point pointing directly upwards.) How many lines of symmetry does this shape have?
  • A. 0
  • B. 1
  • C. 2
  • D. 4
Explanation: The correct answer is 1. A common mistake here: Cannot identify a line of symmetry in a simple shape. Double-check each step in identify or mark lines of symmetry on a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Cannot identify a line of symmetry in a simple shape
L1 eedi diagram eedi_903
Which shape has a line of symmetry? (Diagram: A Trapezium above the label "A"; A shape which is a circle with a 90 degree sector removed above the label "B"; A crescent (moon) shape above the label "C"; the words "All of them" above the label "D".)
  • A. A
  • B. B
  • C. C
  • D. D
Explanation: The correct answer is D. A common mistake here: Believes lines of symmetry must be vertical or horizontal. Double-check each step in identify or mark lines of symmetry on a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes lines of symmetry must be vertical or horizontal
L1 eedi diagram eedi_952
(Diagram: This shape is a line drawing of 2 ellipses whose centres, marked with a black dot, are lined up, but one ellipse is at right angles to the other.) What is the order of rotational symmetry of this shape?
  • A. 0
  • B. 1
  • C. 2
  • D. 4
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry. Double-check each step in identify the order of rotational symmetry of a shape to avoid this error.

L1 eedi diagram eedi_974
Which image completes a symmetric figure on the Geo board? (Diagram: A figure drawn in one half of a box, with a red dotted line running diagonally across the box.)
  • A. ![An incorrect reflection of the given figure. ]()
  • B. ![An incorrect reflection of the given figure. ]()
  • C. ![A correct reflection of the given figure. ]()
  • D. ![An incorrect reflection of the given figure. ]()
