⚠ Common trap: Selects y>a solutions instead of y<a solutions for a quadratic inequality
L3 inline_ts li-3-2
What is the largest integer satisfying 3(2x − 4) ≤ 2(x + 7)?
Explanation: Expand: 6x − 12 ≤ 2x + 14. Subtract 2x: 4x − 12 ≤ 14. Add 12: 4x ≤ 26. Divide: x ≤ 6.5. Largest integer is 6.
L3 inline_ts li-3-3
The perimeter of a rectangle must be at most 60 cm. The length is twice the width. What is the maximum possible width?
Explanation: Let w = width, L = 2w. Perimeter: 2(w + 2w) ≤ 60 → 6w ≤ 60 → w ≤ 10. Max width = 10 cm.

Lines and Angles

core · 39 questions · lines_angles
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t02_q02
In the following figure, the two horizontal lines are parallel. Which of the following does NOT equal 180°?
  • A. ( p + r )°
  • B. ( p + t )°
  • C. ( q + s )°
  • D. ( r + s + t )°
  • E. ( t + u )°
Explanation: Lines and Angles → identify parallel horizontal lines and transversals → use linear pairs/vertical angles/corresponding relationships → test each expression for whether it must equal 180° → p + t is the one that does not necessarily equal 180°.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t03_q03
The following figure is a quadrilateral with one right angle. The other angles are labeled. What is the value of x ?
  • A. 54
  • B. 56.25
  • C. 60
  • D. 67.5
  • E. 72
Explanation: Step 1: The quadrilateral's four interior angles are 2x°, x° (top-right), 90° (bottom-left, marked right angle), and x° (bottom-right). Step 2: All quadrilateral interior angles sum to 360°. Step 3: 2x + x + 90 + x = 360 → 4x + 90 = 360 → 4x = 270 → x = 67.5.
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t10_q01
In the following figure, each of the four angles in the parallelogram is as shown. What is the value of y ?
  • A. 30
  • B. 35
  • C. 40
  • D. 45
  • E. 50
Explanation: Step 1: In a parallelogram, consecutive (adjacent) angles are supplementary (sum to 180°). Step 2: Read the given angle from the figure. If one angle = 130°, then its consecutive angle = 180° − 130° = 50°. Step 3: Answer: K. 50°.
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1087
What is the size of angle x ? (Diagram: Angles around a point split into two parts, one is labelled 290 degrees and the other x)
  • A. 290^°
  • B. 70^°
  • C. 50^°
  • D. 45^°
Explanation: The correct answer is 70^°. A common mistake here: Incorrectly identifies angles as vertically opposite. Double-check each step in find missing angles using angles around a point to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Incorrectly identifies angles as vertically opposite

  • B. 100^°
  • C. 360^°
  • D. 90^°
Explanation: The correct answer is 180^°. A common mistake here: Believes angles on a straight line add to 100 degrees. Double-check each step in find missing angles using angles on a straight line to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes angles on a straight line add to 100 degrees

  • A. They add together to make 180^°
  • B. They add together to make 360^°
  • C. They are equal
  • D. They are parallel
Explanation: The correct answer is They are equal. A common mistake here: Believes vertically opposite angles sum to 180 degrees. Double-check each step in recognise the vertically opposite angles relationship to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes vertically opposite angles sum to 180 degrees

  • B. 385^°

  • A. ![A straight line, separated into an angle of 53 degrees and an angle of 127 degrees]()
  • B. ![A straight line, separated into an angle of 150 degrees and an angle of 50 degrees]()
  • C. ![A straight line, separated into three angles. One is unmarked, one is 90 degrees and one is 45 degrees]()

L1 eedi diagram eedi_409
What is the size of angle q ? (Diagram: A long straight line with two shorter lines joining it, but not crossing it. Where the line on the left joins it forms two angles, q on the left, 126 degrees on the right. Where the line on the right joins it forms two angles, the left is unmarked, 37 degrees on the right.)
  • A. 37^°
  • B. 54^°
  • C. 17^°
  • D. 64^°
Explanation: The correct answer is 54^°. A common mistake here: Thinks angles which look the same on diagram will be equal. Double-check each step in find missing angles using angles on a straight line to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks angles which look the same on diagram will be equal
L1 eedi diagram eedi_688
What is the relationship between these two angles? (Diagram: Angles around a point made up of two angles: a green one and a pink one)
  • A. They are equal
  • B. They are parallel
  • C. They add together to make 180^°
  • D. They add together to make 360^°
Explanation: The correct answer is They add together to make 180^°. A common mistake here: Student thinks that any two angles along a straight line are equal. Double-check each step in recognise the angles on a straight line relationship to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Student thinks that any two angles along a straight line are equal
L1 eedi diagram eedi_800
(Diagram: Two angles around a point, labelled 2y and y) What is the size of angle y ?
  • A. 180^°
  • B. 120^°
  • C. 90^°
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 120^°. A common mistake here: Does not think that a variable with no coefficient has a coefficient of 1. Double-check each step in use angles around a point to form and solve algebraic equations to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not think that a variable with no coefficient has a coefficient of 1
L1 eedi diagram eedi_97
Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the two marked angles? (Diagram: A diagram showing 3 lines crossing at a point to form 6 angles. Two angles are marked in orange, there are two unmarked angles between them on either side.)
  • A. Alternate angles
  • B. Corresponding angles
  • C. Vertically opposite angles
  • D. Angles on a straight line
Explanation: The correct answer is Vertically opposite angles. A common mistake here: Confuses alternate and opposite angles. Double-check each step in recognise the vertically opposite angles relationship to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses alternate and opposite angles
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t13_q10
In the figure below, F , G , H , and J are collinear. , , and are line segments of equivalent length, and the measure of JHK is 120Â°. What is the degree measure of GFK ?
  • A. 30Â°
  • B. 45Â°
  • C. 60Â°
  • D. 120Â°
  • E. 150Â°
Explanation: Step 1: F,G,H,J collinear; ∠JHK=120° → ∠GHK=180°-120°=60° (supplementary). Step 2: GK=HK → triangle GHK is isosceles (apex K). Base angles: ∠KGH=∠KHG=60°. So ∠GKH=60°; triangle GHK is equilateral → GH=GK. Step 3: FG=GK; ∠FGK=180°-∠KGH=180°-60°=120° (angles on line). Step 4: Triangle FGK: FG=GK, ∠FGK=120° → base angles ∠GFK=∠GKF=(180°-120°)/2=30° → F.
L2 eedi diagram eedi_12
Tom and Katie are arguing about alternate angles. Tom says angles p and q are alternate. Katie says angles p and q are equal. Who is correct? (Diagram: Shows two non parallel lines that are intersected by another line. Angles P and Q are both on the outside of the lines and on opposite sides of the transversal.)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Thinks alternate angles are always equal. Double-check each step in identify alternate angles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks alternate angles are always equal
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1224
Tom and Katie are arguing about angle relationships on parallel lines. Tom says allied (co-interior) angles are equal. Katie says corresponding angles are equal. Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Thinks co-interior angles are equal. Double-check each step in identify co-interior angles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks co-interior angles are equal
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1593
Line JK is.. (Diagram: A pair of parallel lines are drawn with the arrow on each pointing to the right. A straight red line, KJ, is drawn so that diagonally crosses both parallel lines.)
  • A. Parallel
  • B. A transversal
  • C. Skew
  • D. Perpendicular
Explanation: The correct answer is A transversal. A common mistake here: Does not know the term transversal. Double-check each step in identify a transversal to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the term transversal
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1621
Which of the following are corresponding angles on this diagram? (Diagram: Three almost horizontal lines, two marked with arrows. One almost vertical line crossing them. The twelve angles created are marked a-l.)
  • A. c and g
  • B. c and k
  • C. d and j
  • D. b and f
Explanation: The correct answer is c and k. A common mistake here: Mistakes lines as parallel. Double-check each step in identify corresponding angles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mistakes lines as parallel
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1739
What type of angles are highlighted in this diagram? (Diagram: The diagram is of a pair of parallel lines with a line crossing them. The two highlighted angles each have a parallel line above it and the transverse line to the left of it.)
  • A. alternate angles
  • B. corresponding angles
  • C. co-interior angles
  • D. opposite angles
Explanation: The correct answer is corresponding angles. A common mistake here: Confuses corresponding and alternate angles. Double-check each step in identify corresponding angles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses corresponding and alternate angles
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1830
What is the name given to the red line that intersects the two dashed lines? (Diagram: Shows two straight horizontal dashed lines that are converging and are both intersected by a solid red line)
  • A. Parallel
  • B. Corresponding
  • C. Alternate
  • D. Transversal
Explanation: The correct answer is Transversal. A common mistake here: Does not know the meaning of the word parallel. Double-check each step in identify a transversal to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the meaning of the word parallel
L2 eedi diagram eedi_358
Line X Y is... (Diagram: A pair of parallel lines are drawn with the arrows pointing up to the left. A straight red line, XY, is drawn so that it diagonally crosses the pair of parallel lines. The red line rises from X on the left up to Y.)
  • A. Parallel
  • B. Perpendicular
  • C. Skew
  • D. A transversal
Explanation: The correct answer is A transversal. A common mistake here: Believes any lines that cross are perpendicular. Double-check each step in identify a transversal to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes any lines that cross are perpendicular
L2 eedi diagram eedi_569
Calculate the missing angle: (Diagram: Two vertical lines marked with arrows are crossed by one diagonal line. Two angles are marked; they are both on the upper side of the diagonal line and to the right of the vertical lines. One is marked '70', the other '?'.)
  • A. 70^°
  • B. 110^°
  • C. 290^°
  • D. 220^°
Explanation: The correct answer is 70^°. A common mistake here: Thinks corresponding angles sum to 180 degrees. Double-check each step in find missing angles on parallel lines using corresponding angles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks corresponding angles sum to 180 degrees
L2 eedi diagram eedi_706
These marked angles are: (Diagram: A pair of parallel lines drawn pointing up to the right. A straight line is drawn that crosses both parallel lines. The angles formed above the straight line and to the right of each of the parallel lines is marked.)
  • A. Alternate
  • B. Corresponding
  • C. Co-Interior
  • D. Parallel
Explanation: The correct answer is Corresponding. A common mistake here: Confuses corresponding and alternate angles. Double-check each step in identify corresponding angles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses corresponding and alternate angles
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t05_q15
In the following figure, what is the value of y in terms of x ?
  • A. x + 80
  • B. 80 – x
  • C. x + 100
  • D. x – 100
  • E. 100 – x
Explanation: Step 1: Identify the triangle. The apex angle = 100°; the interior base-left angle = x°; the angle at base-right is the third interior angle. Step 2: Sum of interior angles = 180°: third interior = 180−100−x = 80−x. Step 3: The exterior angle at base-right is the supplement of (80−x): 180−(80−x) = 100+x. Step 4: Equivalently, exterior angle theorem states the exterior angle of a triangle equals th…
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t11_q15
An isosceles triangle contains three angles that measure 40°, x °, and y °. Which of the following CANNOT be true?
  • A. x = y
  • B. x = 50
  • C. x – y = 60
  • D. x = 70
  • E. x = 100
Explanation: Step 1: Sum of angles = 180°: 40 + x + y = 180 → x + y = 140°. Step 2: Isosceles triangle has two equal angles. Three cases: Case 1: x = y → x = y = 70°. (Triangle: 40,70,70.) Valid. Case 2: x = 40 → y = 100. (Triangle: 40,40,100.) Valid. Case 3: y = 40 → x = 100. (Triangle: 40,100,40.) Valid. Step 3: Check B: x=50 → y=90. Triangle would be 40,50,90 — no two angles equal. Not isosceles. Cann…
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t13_q15
In Î_x0094_ XYZ , Y is a right angle and Z measures less than 52Â°. Which of the following phrases best describes the measure of X ?
  • A. Greater than 38Â°
  • B. Equal to 38Â°
  • C. Equal to 45Â°
  • D. Equal to 142Â°
  • E. Less than 38Â°
Explanation: Step 1: Triangle angle sum: ∠X+∠Y+∠Z=180°. Step 2: ∠Y=90° → ∠X+∠Z=90°. Step 3: ∠Z<52° → ∠X=90°-∠Z > 90°-52°=38°. Answer: A. Greater than 38°.

Logarithmic Functions

core · 2 questions · logarithmic_functions
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t02_q15
If log 5 x = 2, what is ?
  • A. 5
  • B. 25
  • C. 32
  • D. K.
Explanation: Logarithms → log₅(x)=2 means 5²=x → x=25 → if the final prompt asks for √x, then √x=5.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t08_q13
If log 9 n = and n > 0, what is the value of ?
  • A. 3
  • B. K.
Explanation: Step 1: Given log_n(2) = 1/3, with n > 0. Step 2: Rewrite in exponential form: n^(1/3) = 2. Step 3: Cube both sides: n = 2³ = 8. Step 4: The question asks for √n = √8. Step 5: Answer: J. √8 (equivalently 2√2, but leave as √8 to match choice).

Units, Measurement, and Dimensional Reasoning

foundational · 59 questions · measurement_units
L1 act_master word_problem act_math_t05_q01
Jackson worked 25 hours and received $225. At the same rate of pay, how much would he make if he worked 40 hours?
  • A. $300
  • B. $325
  • C. $350
  • D. $360
  • E. $400
Explanation: Rate = $225÷25 = $9/hour. 40 hours × $9 = $360.
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1038
Which one of the following calculations would work out the number of hours in 10 weeks?
  • A. 10 × 5 × 24
  • B. 10 × 7 × 24
  • C. 10 × 24
  • D. 10 × 7 × 12
Explanation: The correct answer is 10 × 7 × 24. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 5 days in a week. Double-check each step in convert from weeks to hours to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 5 days in a week
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1041
How many hours is 3600 seconds?
  • A. 60
  • B. 36
  • C. 3.6
  • D. 1
Explanation: The correct answer is 1. A common mistake here: Converted seconds to minutes instead of seconds to hours. Double-check each step in convert from seconds to hours to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Converted seconds to minutes instead of seconds to hours
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1078
3000 kg is the same as ___ tonnes
  • A. 3
  • B. 30
  • C. 300
  • D. 3000000
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 100kg in a tonne. Double-check each step in convert between kg and tonne to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 100kg in a tonne
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1086
0.6 hours = _____ minutes
  • A. 6
  • B. 120
  • C. 36
  • D. 60
Explanation: The correct answer is 36. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 10 minutes in an hour. Double-check each step in change decimals of hours into minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 10 minutes in an hour
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1119
How should you write 5 kilometers and 7 centimetres in meters?
  • A. 5070 m
  • B. 5000.07 m
  • C. 5007 m
  • D. 5700 m
Explanation: The correct answer is 5000.07 m. A common mistake here: Incorrectly converted cm to m. Double-check each step in convert between cm and m to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Incorrectly converted cm to m
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1132
Tom and Katie are discussing units of area Tom says centimetres is a unit of area Katie says mm^2 is a unit of area Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Does not know units of area should be squared. Double-check each step in identify a unit of area to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know units of area should be squared
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1136
1/8 th of an hour = _______ minutes
  • A. 7.5
  • B. 8
  • C. 0.8
  • D. 12.5
Explanation: The correct answer is 7.5. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour. Double-check each step in calculate unit fractions of an amount to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1151
How many millimetres are there in 1 centimetre?
  • A. 0.1
  • B. 10
  • C. 100
  • D. 1000
Explanation: The correct answer is 10. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 10 centimetres in 1 mm. Double-check each step in convert between cm and mm to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 10 centimetres in 1 mm
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1167
4000 m is the same as ____ km
  • A. 400
  • B. 400000
  • C. 4
  • D. 40
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 10m in a km. Double-check each step in convert between m and km to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 10m in a km
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1175
Hannah's journey to camp is 3 hours and 26 minutes. Tori's journey to camp is 204 minutes. What is the difference in their journeys in minutes?
  • A. 206 minutes
  • B. 122 minutes
  • C. 2 minutes
  • D. 410 minutes
Explanation: The correct answer is 2 minutes. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes. Double-check each step in convert from hours to minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1199
Which one of the following calculations would work out the number of hours in 5 weeks?
  • A. 5 × 24
  • B. 5 × 7 × 12
  • C. 5 × 5 × 24
  • D. 5 × 7 × 24
Explanation: The correct answer is 5 × 7 × 24. A common mistake here: Forgets to multiply up for the correct time frame in a worded question. Double-check each step in convert from weeks to hours to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Forgets to multiply up for the correct time frame in a worded question
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_120
Square P has an area of 4 cm^2 Square Q has an area of 4 mm^2 What is the total area of squares P and Q in mm^2 ?
  • A. 8 mm^2
  • B. 44 mm^2
  • C. 404 mm^2
  • D. 40004 mm^2
Explanation: The correct answer is 404 mm^2. A common mistake here: Does not convert measurements to have the same units before calculating area or volume. Double-check each step in perform calculations with areas when given in different units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not convert measurements to have the same units before calculating area or volume
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1283
A 0.5 kilogram weight has 10 grams shaved off it. What is the mass of the weight that remains? Give your answer in grams.
  • A. 490 g
  • B. 40 g
  • C. 499 g
  • D. 0.49 g
Explanation: The correct answer is 490 g. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 100g in a kilogram. Double-check each step in convert between kg and g to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 100g in a kilogram
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_13
Hannah's journey to camp is 3 hours and 16 minutes. Tori's journey to camp is 214 minutes. What is the difference in their journeys in minutes?
  • A. 196 minutes
  • B. 18 minutes
  • C. 102 minutes
  • D. 12 minutes
Explanation: The correct answer is 18 minutes. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes. Double-check each step in convert from hours to minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1300
Which of the following would correctly calculate the number of seconds in 1 day?
  • A. 24 × 60
  • B. 24 × 60 × 60
  • C. 12 × 60
  • D. 12 × 60 × 60
Explanation: The correct answer is 24 × 60 × 60. A common mistake here: Converted hours to minutes instead of hours to seconds. Double-check each step in convert from days to seconds to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Converted hours to minutes instead of hours to seconds
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1479
4000 g is the same as k g
  • A. 400
  • B. 4000000
  • C. 4
  • D. 40
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 10g in a kilogram. Double-check each step in convert between g and kg to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 10g in a kilogram
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1487
Tom and Katie are discussing units of volume Tom says liters is a unit of volume Katie says m^3 is a unit of volume Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not know that units of volume are usually cubed. Double-check each step in identify a unit of volume or capacity to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know that units of volume are usually cubed
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1516
How many millimetres in 100 kilometers?
  • A. One hundred thousand
  • B. One million
  • C. One hundred million
  • D. One billion
Explanation: The correct answer is One hundred million. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 1000mm in a km. Double-check each step in complete multi-step conversions of length that use multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 1000mm in a km
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1554
Complete this statement: 5 liters = ________ cm^3
  • A. 5
  • B. 500
  • C. 5000
  • D. 50
Explanation: The correct answer is 5000. A common mistake here: Thinks 1l = 1 cubic centimetre. Double-check each step in convert between l and cm³ to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks 1l = 1 cubic centimetre
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1596
1/12 of an hour = ____ minutes
  • A. 12
  • B. 8.3
  • C. 5
  • D. 1.2
Explanation: The correct answer is 5. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour. Double-check each step in calculate unit fractions of an amount to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1682
A coach takes 2 hours 40 minutes to complete a journey from Manchester to Milton Keynes. If the coach leaves Manchester at 10:45, what time does it arrive in Milton Keynes?
  • A. 13: 25
  • B. 01:25
  • C. 12: 85
  • D. 13: 15
Explanation: The correct answer is 13: 25. A common mistake here: Writes times in the afternoon as if they are morning on a 24 hour clock. Double-check each step in solve problems involving adding a period of time on to a given start time to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Writes times in the afternoon as if they are morning on a 24 hour clock
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1694
A can of pop is 330 ml . What is this in liters?
  • A. 3.3 liters
  • B. 0.3 liters
  • C. 0.303 liters
  • D. 0.33 liters
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.33 liters. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 100 ml in a litre. Double-check each step in convert between l and ml to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 100 ml in a litre
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1722
A 2 meter long rope has 0.5 centimetres chopped off the end. What is the length of the rope that remains? Give your answer in millimetres
  • A. 1999.5 mm
  • B. 1995 mm
  • C. 1950 mm
  • D. 199.5 mm
Explanation: The correct answer is 1995 mm. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 100mm in a centimetre. Double-check each step in convert between m and mm to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 100mm in a centimetre
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1855
Complete this statement: 4 liters = _________ cm^3
  • A. 4
  • B. 4000
  • C. 400
  • D. 40
Explanation: The correct answer is 4000. A common mistake here: Thinks 1l = 1 cubic centimetre. Double-check each step in convert between l and cm³ to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks 1l = 1 cubic centimetre
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_198
2 km^2 is the same as _____ m^2
  • A. 2000
  • B. 2000000
  • C. 20000
  • D. 2000000000
Explanation: The correct answer is 2000000. A common mistake here: Does not square the conversion factor when converting squared units. Double-check each step in convert from km² to m² to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not square the conversion factor when converting squared units
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_209
Order the times from shortest to longest. A: 8 minutes B: 8 minutes 40 seconds C: 8 1/2 minutes D: 500 seconds
  • A. A, B, C, D
  • B. D, C, A, B
  • C. A, D, C, B
  • D. B, D, C, A
Explanation: The correct answer is A, D, C, B. A common mistake here: Mixes up shortest and longest when ordering times. Double-check each step in convert from minutes to seconds to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up shortest and longest when ordering times
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_259
3 hours is the same as ___________ minutes
  • A. 180
  • B. 90
  • C. 30
  • D. 300
Explanation: The correct answer is 180. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 30 minutes in a hour. Double-check each step in convert from hours to minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 30 minutes in a hour
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_261
90 minutes is the same as _________ hours
  • A. 9
  • B. 1.9
  • C. 1.5
  • D. 1.3
Explanation: The correct answer is 1.5. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 10 minutes in an hour. Double-check each step in change minutes into decimals of hours (e.g. 90 minutes = 1.5 hours) to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 10 minutes in an hour
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_287
Tom and Katie are discussing units of time Tom says days is a unit of time Katie says km / h is a unit of time Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Mistakes unit of speed for unit of time. Double-check each step in identify a unit of time to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mistakes unit of speed for unit of time
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_348
How many days in a non-leap year?
  • A. 350
  • B. 366
  • C. 365
  • D. 100
Explanation: The correct answer is 365. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 50 weeks in a year. Double-check each step in convert from years to days to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 50 weeks in a year
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_421
Which of the following would correctly calculate the number of seconds in 2 days?
  • A. 2 × 12 × 60
  • B. 2 × 12 × 60 × 60
  • C. 2 × 24 × 60
  • D. 2 × 24 × 60 × 60
Explanation: The correct answer is 2 × 24 × 60 × 60. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 12 hours in 1 day. Double-check each step in convert from days to seconds to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 12 hours in 1 day
L1 eedi diagram eedi_440
What temperature does the thermometer show? (Diagram: A thermometer showing the temperature in degrees Celsius. There are five steps on the scale between 10 and 0, and five steps between 0 and -10. The temperature is shown at 4 steps below 0.)
  • A. -8^° C
  • B. -9^° C
  • C. -4^° C
  • D. -11^° C
Explanation: The correct answer is -8^° C. A common mistake here: When reading integers on a number line, assumes each dash is 1. Double-check each step in read a negative integer temperature from a thermometer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading integers on a number line, assumes each dash is 1
L1 eedi diagram eedi_487
The perimeter of this rectangle is 1.6 m . What is the length of the side marked with a star? (Diagram: A rectangle with base length 0.35 m and the side length labelled with a gold, star)
  • A. 1.25 m
  • B. 0.45 m
  • C. 2.7 m
  • D. 0.55 m
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.45 m. A common mistake here: When calculating perimeter, only adds the marked lengths. Double-check each step in given the perimeter, work out a missing length requiring knowledge of the geometrical properties of the shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When calculating perimeter, only adds the marked lengths
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_498
2.5 hours =_____ minutes
  • A. 150
  • B. 125
  • C. 250
  • D. 205
Explanation: The correct answer is 150. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 10 minutes in an hour. Double-check each step in change decimals of hours into minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 10 minutes in an hour
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_522
5 hours +500 minutes =
  • A. 10 hours
  • B. 8 hours and 20 minutes
  • C. 13 hours and 20 minutes
  • D. 13 hours and 33 minutes
Explanation: The correct answer is 13 hours and 20 minutes. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes. Double-check each step in convert from minutes to hours to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_539
A regular octagon has a total perimeter of 12 cm . What is the length of each of its sides?
  • A. 1.5 cm
  • B. 4 cm
  • C. 96 cm
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 1.5 cm. A common mistake here: When given the perimeter of a regular polygon, subtracts instead of divides to find each side length. Double-check each step in given the perimeter, work out a missing length requiring knowledge of the geometrical properties of the shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When given the perimeter of a regular polygon, subtracts instead of divides to find eac...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_545
Which clock shows ten to twelve at night?
  • A. 10:12
  • B. 12: 50
  • C. 00:50
  • D. 23: 50
Explanation: The correct answer is 23: 50. A common mistake here: Gets the minutes and hours the wrong way around. Double-check each step in tell the time from a 24 hour digital clock with times after half past the hour to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Gets the minutes and hours the wrong way around
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_583
4 m is the same as ___ mm
  • A. 4000
  • B. 40000
  • C. 40
  • D. 400
Explanation: The correct answer is 4000. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 10000mm in a metre. Double-check each step in convert between m and mm to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 10000mm in a metre
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_588
0.2 hours = _______ minutes
  • A. 12
  • B. 20
  • C. 2
  • D. 120
Explanation: The correct answer is 12. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes. Double-check each step in change decimals of hours into minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_609
To work out how many hours in a year you could do...
  • A. 365 × 7
  • B. 365 × 60
  • C. 365 × 12
  • D. 365 × 24
Explanation: The correct answer is 365 × 24. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 7 hours in 1 day. Double-check each step in convert between any other time periods to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 7 hours in 1 day
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_621
A coach takes 3 hours 50 minutes to complete a journey from Manchester to London. If the coach leaves Manchester at 09:25, what time does it arrive in London?
  • A. 13: 25
  • B. 12: 75
  • C. 01:15
  • D. 13: 15
Explanation: The correct answer is 13: 15. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour. Double-check each step in solve problems involving adding a period of time on to a given start time to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour
L1 eedi diagram eedi_662
The perimeter of this rectangle is 5.6 cm . What is the length of the side marked with a star? (Diagram: Rectangle with base length labelled 1.02 cm and side length labelled with a gold star)
  • A. 4.58 cm
  • B. 1.6 cm
  • C. 1.78 cm
  • D. 4.4 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 1.78 cm. A common mistake here: When calculating perimeter, only adds the marked lengths. Double-check each step in given the perimeter, work out a missing length requiring knowledge of the geometrical properties of the shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When calculating perimeter, only adds the marked lengths
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_773
3000 ml is the same as ___ liters
  • A. 3
  • B. 30
  • C. 300
  • D. 3000000
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Thinks there are 100 ml in a litre. Double-check each step in convert between ml and l to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks there are 100 ml in a litre
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_775
3 hours +700 minutes =
  • A. 11 hours and 40 minutes
  • B. 10 hours
  • C. 14 hours and 66 minutes
  • D. 14 hours and 40 minutes
Explanation: The correct answer is 14 hours and 40 minutes. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes. Double-check each step in convert from minutes to hours to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_810
1.5 hours = _______ minutes
  • A. 150
  • B. 105
  • C. 90
  • D. 65
Explanation: The correct answer is 90. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes. Double-check each step in change decimals of hours into minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_877
Tom and Katie are discussing units of length Tom says miles is a unit of length Katie says m l is a unit of length Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Does not recognise millilitres as a unit of volume. Double-check each step in identify a unit of length to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise millilitres as a unit of volume
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_880
What time is 30 minutes before midnight?
  • A. 11: 30
  • B. 11:30 am
  • C. 11:30 pm
  • D. None of these answers is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is 11:30 pm. A common mistake here: Writes times in the afternoon as if they are morning on a 24 hour clock. Double-check each step in solve problems involving subtracting a period of time from a given end time to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Writes times in the afternoon as if they are morning on a 24 hour clock
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_893
How should you write 5 kilograms and 7 grams in tonnes?
  • A. 5007 t
  • B. 0.05007 t
  • C. 5.007 t
  • D. 0.005007 t
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.005007 t. A common mistake here: Thinks grams and tonnes are the same. Double-check each step in complete multi-step conversions of mass that use division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks grams and tonnes are the same
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_900
500000 cm^3 is the same as ______ liters
  • A. 0.5
  • B. 5
  • C. 500
  • D. 5000
Explanation: The correct answer is 500. A common mistake here: Thinks 1l = 10 000 cubic centimetres. Double-check each step in convert between cm³ and l to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks 1l = 10 000 cubic centimetres
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_922
1 cm^3 is the same as _______ mm^3
  • A. 10
  • B. 100
  • C. 1000
  • D. 10000
Explanation: The correct answer is 1000. A common mistake here: Does not cube the conversion factor when converting cubed units. Double-check each step in convert between cm³ and mm³ to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not cube the conversion factor when converting cubed units
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_961
A school trip lasted 5 hours and 25 minutes. How long is this in minutes?
  • A. 525 minutes
  • B. 325 minutes
  • C. 300 minutes
  • D. 5.25 minutes
Explanation: The correct answer is 325 minutes. A common mistake here: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes. Double-check each step in convert from hours to minutes to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Answers as if there are 100 minutes in an hour when changing from hours to minutes
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_99
A regular pentagon has a total perimeter of 17 cm . What is the length of each of its sides?
  • A. 85 cm
  • B. 12 cm
  • C. 3.4 cm
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 3.4 cm. A common mistake here: When given the perimeter of a regular polygon, multiplies instead of divides to find each side length. Double-check each step in given the perimeter, work out a missing length requiring knowledge of the geometrical properties of the shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When given the perimeter of a regular polygon, multiplies instead of divides to find ea...
L2 act_master word_problem act_math_t04_q09
Doug, who runs track for his high school, was challenged to a race by his younger brother, Matt. Matt started running first, and Doug didn’t start running until Matt had finished a quarter-mile lap on the school track. Doug passed Matt as they both finished their sixth lap. If both boys ran at a constant speed, with Doug running 2 miles an hour faster than Matt, what was Matt’s speed?
  • A. 10.5 miles per hour
  • B. 10 miles per hour
  • C. 9 miles per hour
  • D. 8 miles per hour
  • E. 7.5 miles per hour
Explanation: Step 1: Let Hiroshi's speed be r (mph) and Akemi's be (r–5). They travel the same total distance in the same time T. Step 2: distance = rate × time. Hiroshi: d = r·T; Akemi: d = (r–5)·T — wait, if same distance and same time, same speed, which can't be. Re-read: if they travel for the SAME time and end up a different total distance, or if they're going to the same destination but Akemi takes longe…
L2 act_master word_problem act_math_t07_q09
Ansgar is writing a novel. He writes seven days a week. On each of those days he writes for at least 4 hours but never more than 8 hours. Last week, he wrote for exactly 46 hours. What is the maximum number of days on which he could have written for 8 hours?
  • A. 2 days
  • B. 3 days
  • C. 4 days
  • D. 5 days
  • E. 6 days
Explanation: 7 days, each 4–8 hours, total 46 hours. Maximize days at exactly 4 hours (minimum). Let m = days at 4 hours. Remaining (7-m) days fill 46-4m hours, each between 4 and 8. Max for remaining days: 8(7-m) ≥ 46-4m → 56-8m ≥ 46-4m → 10 ≥ 4m → m ≤ 2.5. So m ≤ 2. Check m=2: remaining 5 days fill 38 hours; 38/5=7.6 hrs each (valid: 4≤7.6≤8). Max days at 4 hours = 2.
L2 act_master word_problem act_math_t09_q07
Annette took 10 minutes to walk around a rectangular field. The length of the field is 4 times its width. How long would it take Annette to walk the width of the field?
  • A. 1 minute
  • B. 2 minutes
  • C. 3 minutes
  • D. 4 minutes
  • E. 5 minutes
Explanation: Length l=4w (width). Perimeter = 2(l+w) = 2(4w+w) = 10w. Annette takes 10 minutes for perimeter = 10w, so she takes 1 minute per w. Walking the width w takes 1 minute.
L3 act_master word_problem act_math_t06_q14
Danielle’s phone plan charges her $30 per month for the first 200 minutes and then $0.10 per minute for each subsequent minute. Which of the following functions takes an input of any whole number value of x ≥ 200 and outputs the value for f ( x ) as the amount of dollars Danielle would pay for x minutes of phone usage?
  • A. f ( x ) = 0.1 x + 10
  • B. f ( x ) = 0.1 x + 20
  • C. f ( x ) = 0.1 x + 30
  • D. f ( x ) = 0.1 x + 200
  • E. f ( x ) = 0.1 x + 230
Explanation: For x≥200 minutes: f(x) = 30 + 0.10(x–200) = 30 + 0.1x – 20 = 0.1x + 10.
L3 act_master word_problem act_math_t06_q15
Danielle’s phone plan charges her $30 per month for the first 200 minutes and then $0.10 per minute for each subsequent minute. If Danielle paid exactly $100 last month, how many minutes did she use?
  • A. 700
  • B. 800
  • C. 900
  • D. 1,000
  • E. 1,200
Explanation: From q14: f(x) = 0.1x + 10 = 100. Solve: 0.1x = 90, x = 900 minutes.
L3 act_master word_problem act_math_t08_q11
At 10:00, Angela starts from her home and runs at a constant pace to Kathleen’s house, which is exactly 2 miles away. Immediately, she and Kathleen turn around and walk back to Angela’s house exactly 4 miles an hour slower than Angela ran. When they arrive at Angela’s house, the time is 10:45. At what speed did Angela run?
  • A. 6 miles per hour
  • B. 6.5 miles per hour
  • C. 7 miles per hour
  • D. 7.5 miles per hour
  • E. 8 miles per hour
Explanation: Step 1: Set up the rate-time-distance equation: d = r × t. Step 2: Angela runs part of a route at one speed, another part at a different speed. Set up equations for each segment. Step 3: The total time and/or total distance is given — form an equation combining both segments. Step 4: Solve the resulting equation for the unknown speed. Step 5: Result is 8 mph = K.

Number Properties, Factors, and Divisibility

foundational · 233 questions · number_properties
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t01_q04
The number 4 is the smallest positive integer that has exactly three factors: 1, 2, and 4. If k is the next-highest integer that also has exactly three factors, what is the sum of the three factors of k ?
  • A. 13
  • B. 14
  • C. 16
  • D. 18
  • E. 20
Explanation: Step 1: Recall why 4 has exactly 3 factors: 4=2², factors are {1, 2, 4}. Step 2: Recognize the pattern — a number has exactly 3 factors when it equals p² for a PRIME p (factors are always {1, p, p²}). Step 3: Next prime after 2 is 3. So k = 3² = 9. Step 4: Sum of factors of 9 = 1+3+9 = 13.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t02_q01
If you multiply two integers together and then add 4, the result is 40. Which of the following could NOT be the sum of the two numbers?
  • A. 12
  • B. 13
  • C. 15
  • D. 18
  • E. 20
Explanation: Number Properties + Algebraic Translation → let integers be x and y → xy + 4 = 40 so xy = 36 → list integer factor pairs of 36 → possible positive sums are 37, 20, 15, 13, 12 → the option that cannot be the sum is 18.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t09_q02
Which of the following is the greatest common factor of 60 and 64?
  • A. 2
  • B. 4
  • C. 6
  • D. 8
  • E. 10
Explanation: 60 = 2²×3×5. 64 = 2⁶. GCF = 2² = 4.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t12_q04
Eldridge opened a savings account with an initial balance of $1,000. After that, every month for 8 months, she made one deposit to the account, always for the same amount. During that time, she made just one withdrawal. If each of Eldridge’s 8 deposits was in the amount of $200 and her withdrawal was $350, how much money did her account have at the end of 8 months?
  • A. $850
  • B. $1,550
  • C. $2,250
  • D. $2,350
  • E. $2,950
Explanation: Step 1: Starting balance = $1,000. Step 2: 8 equal deposits of $200 = $1,600 added. Step 3: 1 withdrawal of $350 subtracted. Step 4: Final = 1000 + 1600 − 350 = $2,250 → C.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t13_q04
A school offered its students an optional field trip. If 15 or fewer students went on the field trip, the charge for each student would be $11.50. If more than 15 students chose to go on the field trip, the charge for each student would be $10.25. 18 students opted to go on the tour, but each pre-paid $11.50. The students agreed to put the extra amount toward dinner on the trip. How much total money will be put toward dinner on the trip?
  • A. $12.50
  • B. $14.75
  • C. $21.75
  • D. $22.50
  • E. $33.00
Explanation: Step 1: 18 students went (more than 15), so actual cost = $10.25 each. Step 2: Each student pre-paid $11.50. Step 3: Extra per student = $11.50-$10.25=$1.25. Step 4: Total extra = 18×$1.25=$22.50 → J.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t14_q01
Among the following arithmetic operations, which could the emoticon represent given that the equation (8 2) 3 - (4 1) 2 = 48 is true? I. Subtraction II. Multiplication III. Division
  • A. I only
  • B. III only
  • C. II and III only
  • D. I and III only
  • E. I, II, and III
Explanation: Step 1: Test Subtraction: (8-2)³-(4-1)²=216-9=207≠48 ✗. Step 2: Test Multiplication: (8×2)³-(4×1)²=4096-16=4080≠48 ✗. Step 3: Test Division: (8÷2)³-(4÷1)²=4³-4²=64-16=48 ✓. Step 4: Only III (Division) works → G.
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1001
What is 3 . 5 1 6 3 rounded to 2 decimal places?
  • A. 3.51
  • B. 3.52
  • C. 4.51
  • D. 4.52
Explanation: The correct answer is 3.52. A common mistake here: Rounds down instead of up. Double-check each step in round numbers to two decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1010
Tom and Katie are discussing written division strategies. Tom says 918 ÷ 34=934 ÷ 18 Katie says 918 ÷ 34=34 ÷ 918 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Believes division is commutative . Double-check each step in understand the non-commutativity of division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes division is commutative
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1015
n × 5 ≥ n/5 If n can represent any number, is the statement above...
  • A. always true
  • B. sometimes true
  • C. never true
  • D. Need more information
Explanation: The correct answer is sometimes true. A common mistake here: Assumes a fact without considering enough examples. Double-check each step in carry out division problems involving one negative integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Assumes a fact without considering enough examples
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1031
When using long-division to solve this problem, what digit should replace the rectangle to give the correct answer (quotient)? (Diagram: Division sum written out in Bus Stop format with dividend = 735, divisor = 3, the digits of the quotient are represented by a yellow star, a purple triangle and a blue rectangle.)
  • A. 0
  • B. 2
  • C. 1
  • D. 5
Explanation: The correct answer is 5. A common mistake here: Starts at the wrong end of the dividend when dividing. Double-check each step in divide integers of 3 or more digits using long division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Starts at the wrong end of the dividend when dividing
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1062
Find the value of P . (Diagram: Horizontal green bar divided into 5 equal sections. Each section is labelled with a "P". Curly bracket shows that 3 of the sections sum to 615.)
  • A. 200
  • B. 123
  • C. 205
  • D. 1845
Explanation: The correct answer is 205. A common mistake here: Estimated when not appropriate. Double-check each step in interpret and carry out mental division using a bar model to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Estimated when not appropriate
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1071
What is 1 6 5 1 . 4 3 rounded to the nearest hundred?
  • A. 1700
  • B. 2000
  • C. 1600
  • D. 1650
Explanation: The correct answer is 1700. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little). Double-check each step in round non-integers to the nearest 100 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1084
What number is 100 less than 876,099 ?
  • A. 876.099
  • B. 876,199
  • C. 875,999
  • D. 875,099
Explanation: The correct answer is 875,999. A common mistake here: Divides rather than subtracts when given the command 'less than'. Double-check each step in find 100 less than a given number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Divides rather than subtracts when given the command 'less than'
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1089
What number is 10,000 less than 509,570 ?
  • A. 499,570
  • B. 519,570
  • C. 50.957
  • D. 409,570
Explanation: The correct answer is 499,570. A common mistake here: Divides rather than subtracts when given the command 'less than'. Double-check each step in find another power of 10 less than a given number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Divides rather than subtracts when given the command 'less than'
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1113
(-3) ÷(-5)=
  • A. 8
  • B. -8
  • C. 0.6
  • D. -0.6
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.6. A common mistake here: Believes dividing a negative by a negative gives a negative answer. Double-check each step in carry out division problems involving two negative integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes dividing a negative by a negative gives a negative answer
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_112
Which calculation is equivalent to the fraction 3/4 ?
  • A. 3-4
  • B. 3 ÷ 4
  • C. 4-3
  • D. 4 ÷ 3
Explanation: The correct answer is 3 ÷ 4. A common mistake here: Thinks the fraction bar means subtract rather than divide. Double-check each step in express a division as a fraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the fraction bar means subtract rather than divide
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1125
What is a correct way to set up this problem to solve it using long multiplication? 128 × 26
  • A. ![128 multiplied by 26 set out to perform long multiplication. 26 is written under 128. The 2 of 26 is under the 1 of 128 and the 6 of 26 is under the 2 of 128.]()
  • B. ![128 multiplied by 26 set out to perform long multiplication. 128 is written in 3 columns with 100 in the first column, 20 in the second column and 8 in the third column. 26 is written underneath this, with 20 under 100 and 6 under 20.]()
  • C. ![128 multiplied by 26 set out to perform long multiplication. 128 is written in 3 columns with 100 in the first column, 20 in the second column and 8 in the third column. 26 is written underneath this, with 20 under 20 and 6 under 8.]()
  • D. ![128 multiplied by 26 set out to perform long multiplication. 26 is written under 128. The 2 of 26 is under the 2 of 128 and the 6 of 26 is under the 8 of 128.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![128 multiplied by 26 set out to perform long multiplication. 26 is written under 128. The 2 of 26 is under the 2 of 128 and the 6 of 26 is under the 8 of 128.](). A common mistake here: Hasn't lined up the place value columns when writing out the calculation. Double-check each step in multiply 3 digit integers by 2 digit integers using long multiplication to avoid this err…
⚠ Common trap: Hasn't lined up the place value columns when writing out the calculation
L1 eedi number_line eedi_1128
Here is a number line: (Diagram: A horizontal number-line with 13 vertical, equally spaced, dashes representing the position of numbers. The 1st dash is labelled with "-6", the 3rd dash is labelled "-4", the 5th dash is labelled "-2", the 7th dash is labelled "0", the 9th dash is labelled "2", the 11th dash is labelled "4" and the 13th dash is labelled "6" A red arrow, labelled with a question mark is pointing halfway between the 6th and 7th dashes.) Which of the following numbers could be indicated by the arrow?
  • A. -1.5
  • B. -0.5
  • C. -1
  • D. -0.25
Explanation: The correct answer is -0.5. A common mistake here: Counts on in the wrong direction on a number line. Double-check each step in identify where negative non-integers lie on a number line to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Counts on in the wrong direction on a number line
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1129
(Diagram: Diagram of a thermometer showing positive and negative temperatures, with an arrow labelled +9 going from -3 up to 6) Which of the following calculations is represented on the thermometer?
  • A. -3+9=6
  • B. 3+6=9
  • C. 6+9=-3
  • D. -3+6=9
Explanation: The correct answer is -3+9=6. A common mistake here: Believes a negative number can be replaced with its positive equivalent when answering an addition problem from a numberline. Double-check each step in carry out addition problems involving one negative integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes a negative number can be replaced with its positive equivalent when answering...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1131
Miran says the following: "It doesn't matter if you do 7 ÷ 1 4 0 or 1 4 0 ÷ 7 , you get the same answer" What do you think?
  • A. Miran is correct because division is commutative
  • B. Miran is incorrect because division is commutative
  • C. Miran is correct because division is not commutative
  • D. Miran is incorrect because division is not commutative
Explanation: The correct answer is Miran is incorrect because division is not commutative. A common mistake here: Believes division is commutative . Double-check each step in understand the non-commutativity of division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes division is commutative
L1 eedi number_line eedi_1155
What is the value of A? (Diagram: A horizontal number-line with 11 vertical, equally spaced, dashes representing the position of numbers. "-4" below the 2nd dash and "5" below the last dash are the only numbers marked on the line. An arrow, labelled "A" is pointing halfway between the 5th and 6th dashes.)
  • A. 0.5
  • B. -7.5
  • C. -0.5
  • D. 3
Explanation: The correct answer is -0.5. A common mistake here: When placing a negative number on a number line student assumes numbers with a greater absolute value are placed to the right. Double-check each step in read a decimal on a scale where the required measurement is marked by a dash between two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When placing a negative number on a number line student assumes numbers with a greater...
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1162
Jacob makes a conjecture: If you have two different numbers, P and Q , the order you divide does not matter. P ÷ Q=Q ÷ P Is Jacob's conjecture...
  • A. Always true
  • B. Sometimes true
  • C. Never true
  • D. I don't know
Explanation: The correct answer is Never true. A common mistake here: Believes division is commutative . Double-check each step in understand the non-commutativity of division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes division is commutative
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1166
154.1762 to 1 decimal place is.
  • A. 154.1
  • B. 154.2
  • C. 155.2
  • D. 155.1
Explanation: The correct answer is 154.2. A common mistake here: Rounds down instead of up. Double-check each step in round numbers to one decimal place to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1169
What number is 100 more than 28902 ?
  • A. 2,890,200
  • B. 29,902
  • C. 28,002
  • D. 29,002
Explanation: The correct answer is 29,002. A common mistake here: Multiplies rather than adds when given the command word 'more'. Double-check each step in find 100 more than a given number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Multiplies rather than adds when given the command word 'more'
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1183
5 ×(-4)=
  • A. -9
  • B. 9
  • C. 20
  • D. -20
Explanation: The correct answer is -20. A common mistake here: Believes multiplying a positive by a negative gives a positive answer. Double-check each step in carry out multiplication problems involving one negative integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes multiplying a positive by a negative gives a positive answer
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1189
Find the value of M . (Diagram: Horizontal blue bar divided into 5 equal sections. Every section is labelled with M. A curly bracket shows that 4 of the sections sum to 420.)
  • A. 105
  • B. 84
  • C. 100
  • D. 1680
Explanation: The correct answer is 105. A common mistake here: Estimated when not appropriate. Double-check each step in interpret and carry out mental division using a bar model to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Estimated when not appropriate
L1 eedi diagram eedi_119
Tom and Katie are trying to write different calculations for the diagram on the left. Tom writes: -3 ×(-8)=-24 Katie writes: (-24)/-3=-8 Who is correct? (Diagram: A diagram showing an arrow to the left, marked with a value of -24. Underneath this arrow there is a smaller arrow, still pointing to the left, that is a third of the length of the larger arrow)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Believes multiplying two negatives gives a negative answer. Double-check each step in carry out division problems involving one negative integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes multiplying two negatives gives a negative answer
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1215
609 ÷ 1000=
  • A. 0.609
  • B. 0.69
  • C. 6.09
  • D. 609.000
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.609. A common mistake here: Divided by 100 rather than 1000. Double-check each step in divide integers by 1000 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Divided by 100 rather than 1000
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1240
What is 637 rounded to the nearest 1000 ?
  • A. 500
  • B. 1000
  • C. 0
  • D. 6000
Explanation: The correct answer is 1000. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little). Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest 1000 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1252
Tom and Katie are discussing related calculations Tom says if you know that 4122+426=4548 , then 4122+428 must equal 4 5 5 0 Katie says if you know that 4122-426=3696 , then 4122-428 must equal 3 6 9 4 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not realise that subtracting a larger number will give a smaller answer. Double-check each step in given the solution to one problem, deduce the answer to a related problem using the same operation to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise that subtracting a larger number will give a smaller answer
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1255
Nick wants to subtract 98 from a number. Which one of the following methods would give him the correct answer?
  • A. +100 then +2
  • B. -100 then -2
  • C. -100 then +2
  • D. +100 then -2
Explanation: The correct answer is -100 then +2. A common mistake here: Adds rather than subtracts when answering worded problems. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient strategies for mental subtraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds rather than subtracts when answering worded problems
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1264
Sara has $ 2.79 . Rhys has 3 times as much as Sara. How much do they have altogether? Which calculation would not find the answer?
  • A. $ 2.79 × 4
  • B. ($ 2.79 × 3)+$ 2.79
  • C. $ 2.80 × 4-$ 0.04
  • D. $ 2.79 × 3
Explanation: The correct answer is $ 2.79 × 3. A common mistake here: Does not think that the compensation strategy for the multiplication of 2 numbers is valid. Double-check each step in solve problems that combine addition or subtraction with multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not think that the compensation strategy for the multiplication of 2 numbers is valid
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1266
What is the lowest common multiple of 8 and 4 ?
  • A. 32
  • B. 4
  • C. 8
  • D. 16
Explanation: The correct answer is 8. A common mistake here: Confuses factors and multiples. Double-check each step in identify the lowest common multiple of two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses factors and multiples
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1281
What word do we use to describe the relationship: 7 × 9=5 × 9+2 × 9
  • A. Commutativity
  • B. Factors
  • C. Associativity
  • D. Distributivity
Explanation: The correct answer is Distributivity. A common mistake here: Does not recognise the distributive property. Double-check each step in recognise and use the law of distributivity to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise the distributive property
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1294
(-6)-(-8)=
  • A. 14
  • B. -14
  • C. 2
  • D. -2
Explanation: The correct answer is 2. A common mistake here: Ignores the negative signs when adding positive and negative numbers. Double-check each step in carry out subtraction problems involving two negative integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Ignores the negative signs when adding positive and negative numbers
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1308
Which of the following calculations would solve this problem? Fred is monitoring the growth of his plant. The plant grew 7 cm every week for 35 weeks. By how much did the plant grow in total?
  • A. 35 ÷ 7
  • B. 35-7
  • C. 35+7
  • D. 35 × 7
Explanation: The correct answer is 35 × 7. A common mistake here: Divides rather than multiplies when answering worded problems. Double-check each step in identify that a problem needs to be solved using multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Divides rather than multiplies when answering worded problems
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1314
Which of the following numbers is a common multiple of 4,6 and 12 ?
  • A. 2
  • B. 12
  • C. 18
  • D. 20
Explanation: The correct answer is 12. A common mistake here: Confuses factors and multiples. Double-check each step in identify common multiples of three or more numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses factors and multiples
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1325
How do you write this number in words? 107
  • A. Ten seven
  • B. One hundred and seven
  • C. One hundred and seventy
  • D. Seventeen
Explanation: The correct answer is One hundred and seven. A common mistake here: The student does not understand the place value of 3-digit numbers. Double-check each step in write 3 digit numbers as words to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: The student does not understand the place value of 3-digit numbers
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1337
Tom and Katie are discussing ways to calculate 28 × 15 mentally Tom does 28 × 5 and then multiplies his answer by 3 Katie does 14 × 15 and then doubles her answer Who would get the correct answer?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not realise that multiplication can be broken down into factors. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient methods for mental multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise that multiplication can be broken down into factors
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1355
Tom and Katie are arguing about rounding. Tom says 0 . 0 3 4 5 6 rounded to 2 significant figures is 0 . 0 3 Katie says 0.03456 rounded to 2 decimal places is 0 . 0 3 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Rounds to wrong degree of accuracy (decimal places rather than significant figures). Double-check each step in round numbers between 0 and 1 to two significant figures to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to wrong degree of accuracy (decimal places rather than significant figures)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1356
What is 1 . 5 1 6 rounded to the nearest 0 . 0 5 ?
  • A. 1.50
  • B. 1.51
  • C. 1.55
  • D. 1.52
Explanation: The correct answer is 1.50. A common mistake here: Rounds up instead of down. Double-check each step in round non-integers to other non-integer values (e.g. 0.02) to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds up instead of down
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_136
The product of two numbers is -24 . What could the two numbers be?
  • A. -3 and -8
  • B. -4 and 6
  • C. -10 and -14
  • D. -3 and 9
Explanation: The correct answer is -4 and 6. A common mistake here: Believes multiplying two negatives gives a negative answer. Double-check each step in carry out multiplication problems involving one negative integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes multiplying two negatives gives a negative answer
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1361
Miran says the following: “It doesn't matter if you do 81-53 or 53-81 , you get the same answer." What do you think?
  • A. Miran is correct because subtraction is commutative
  • B. Miran is incorrect because subtraction is commutative
  • C. Miran is correct because subtraction is not commutative
  • D. Miran is incorrect because subtraction is not commutative
Explanation: The correct answer is Miran is incorrect because subtraction is not commutative. A common mistake here: Believes subtraction is commutative . Double-check each step in understand the non-commutativity of subtraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes subtraction is commutative
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1367
What number is represented below? (Diagram: An image showing four rods, each cut into ten squares, three individual squares and one large square cut into ten rows and ten columns.)
  • A. 431
  • B. 341
  • C. 143
  • D. 134
Explanation: The correct answer is 143. A common mistake here: Writes digits in the order of the pictorial representation rather than using place value columns. Double-check each step in recognise integers expressed visually to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Writes digits in the order of the pictorial representation rather than using place valu...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1371
Tom and Katie are discussing negative numbers. Tom says -12>-9 Katie says -0.401>-0.41 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Believes negative numbers with a larger absolute value are greater. Double-check each step in order negative decimals with a different number of digits to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes negative numbers with a larger absolute value are greater
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1420
What number is 10,000 more than 298,603 ?
  • A. 398,603
  • B. 2,986,030,000
  • C. 208,603
  • D. 308,603
Explanation: The correct answer is 308,603. A common mistake here: When adding powers of 10 adds onto the wrong column. Double-check each step in find another power of 10 more than a given number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When adding powers of 10 adds onto the wrong column
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1433
(-9)+(-4)=
  • A. -5
  • B. 5
  • C. 13
  • D. -13
Explanation: The correct answer is -13. A common mistake here: Believes that adding a negative will increase the value of the original number. Double-check each step in carry out addition problems involving two negative integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that adding a negative will increase the value of the original number
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1449
-4+(-5) × 3=
  • A. 27
  • B. -27
  • C. -11
  • D. -19
Explanation: The correct answer is -19. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in answer negative number problems that require the use of bidmas to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1457
Tom and Katie are discussing ways to calculate 21 × 12 mentally Tom does 12 × 7 and then multiplies his answer by 3 Katie does 21 × 6 and then doubles her answer Who would get the correct answer?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not correctly apply the distributive property of multiplication. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient methods for mental multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not correctly apply the distributive property of multiplication
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1472
There are 8 shelves of plates. Four of the shelves hold 15 plates each. Two of the shelves hold 23 plates each. The other shelves hold 17 plates each. How many plates are there in total?
  • A. 123
  • B. 55
  • C. 63
  • D. 140
Explanation: The correct answer is 140. A common mistake here: Ignores numbers written as words in worded questions, and just calculates using those written in digits. Double-check each step in solve problems that combine addition or subtraction with multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Ignores numbers written as words in worded questions, and just calculates using those w...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1491
(-2)^4=
  • A. -16

⚠ Common trap: Believes multiplying two negatives gives a negative answer
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1500
What is 29.156828 rounded to 3 decimal places?
  • A. 29.267
  • B. 29.16
  • C. 29.156
  • D. 29.157
Explanation: The correct answer is 29.157. A common mistake here: Rounds incorrectly by changing multiple place values. Double-check each step in round numbers to three or more decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds incorrectly by changing multiple place values
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1503
Two different numbers are equal to 7.46 to two decimal places. The numbers could be....
  • A. 7.4651 and 7.4559
  • B. 7.4628 and 7.4528
  • C. 7.463 and 6.461
  • D. 7.4608 and 7.4551

⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up when next digit is 5
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_152
What is 5050 rounded to the nearest 10 ?
  • A. 5050
  • B. 5100
  • C. 5000
  • D. 5010
Explanation: The correct answer is 5050. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much). Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest 10 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much)
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1534

(Diagram: Division sum written out in Bus Stop format with dividend = 771, divisor = 3, the digits of the quotient are represented by a yellow star, a purple triangle and a blue rectangle.)
  • A. 7
  • B. 2
  • C. 0
  • D. 1
Explanation: The correct answer is 7. A common mistake here: When dividing, ignores any remainders. Double-check each step in divide integers of 3 or more digits using long division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When dividing, ignores any remainders
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1555
Tom and Katie want to share $2 between 4 people. Tom says you would calculate the amount each person gets by doing 4 ÷ 2 Katie says you would calculate the amount each person gets by doing 2 ÷ 4 Who do you agree with?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both of them
  • D. Neither of them
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Believes division is commutative . Double-check each step in understand the non-commutativity of division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes division is commutative
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1562
What is the value of: 54+58/2
  • A. 56
  • B. 224
  • C. 85
  • D. 51
Explanation: The correct answer is 56. A common mistake here: Multiplies rather than divides. Double-check each step in complete multi-step mental calculations to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Multiplies rather than divides
L1 eedi number_line eedi_1598
Here is a number line: (Diagram: A horizontal number-line with 13 vertical, equally spaced, dashes representing the position of numbers. The 1st dash is labelled with "-6", the 3rd dash is labelled "-4", the 5th dash is labelled "-2", the 7th dash is labelled "0", the 9th dash is labelled "2", the 11th dash is labelled "4" and the 13th dash is labelled "6" A red arrow, labelled with a question mark is pointing halfway between the 3rd and 4th dashes.) Which of the following numbers could be indicated by the arrow?
  • A. -3
  • B. -2.75
  • C. -4.5
  • D. -3.5

⚠ Common trap: Reads the position of a number from the space between the line marked, rather than the...
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1608
(Diagram: Diagram of a thermometer showing positive and negative temperatures, with an arrow labelled +5 going from -1 up to 4) Which of the following calculations is represented on the thermometer?
  • A. 4+5=-1
  • B. -1+5=4
  • C. -1+4=5
  • D. 1+4=5
Explanation: The correct answer is -1+5=4. A common mistake here: Believes we move down a number line when we add. Double-check each step in carry out addition problems involving one negative integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes we move down a number line when we add
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1610
Which of the following is the correct way to write 900 using Roman numerals?
  • A. CM
  • B. DCCCC
  • C. IXOO
  • D. ССССССССС
Explanation: The correct answer is CM. A common mistake here: Writes four symbols after rather than one before in Roman numerals. Double-check each step in can write roman numerals between 100 and 1000 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Writes four symbols after rather than one before in Roman numerals
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1611
Jo and Paul are arguing about rounding. Jo says 1 2 0 6 . 9 7 8 rounded to the nearest hundred is 1 2 0 0 Paul says 1 2 0 6 . 9 7 8 rounded to the nearest ten is 1 2 0 0 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Jo. A common mistake here: Rounds down instead of up. Double-check each step in round non-integers to the nearest 10 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1624
Which of these is not a factor of 18 ?
  • A. Thirty-six
  • B. Six
  • C. Nine
  • D. Eighteen
Explanation: The correct answer is Thirty-six. A common mistake here: Does not think a number can be a factor of itself. Double-check each step in identify factors of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not think a number can be a factor of itself
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1625

  • A. 3.2
  • B. 3.12
  • C. 3.16
  • D. 3.15
Explanation: The correct answer is 3.16. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much). Double-check each step in round non-integers to other non-integer values (e.g. 0.02) to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1629
Tom and Katie are discussing factors Tom says 3 is a common factor of 12 x and 15 y^2 Katie says x is a common factor of 12 x and 15 y^2 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Does not know how to identify common factors from algebraic terms. Double-check each step in identify factors of algebraic expressions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to identify common factors from algebraic terms
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1630
What is 356,958 rounded to the nearest 100 ?
  • A. 356,900
  • B. 356,960
  • C. 400,000
  • D. 357,000
Explanation: The correct answer is 357,000. A common mistake here: Rounds down instead of up. Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest 100 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1639
Colm wins $ 55750 on the lottery and gives $ 25250 to charity. How much money does Colm keep?
  • A. $ 81000
  • B. $ 30500
  • C. $ 35500
  • D. $ 80000
Explanation: The correct answer is $ 30500. A common mistake here: Subtracts instead of adds when answering worded problems . Double-check each step in subtract integers with more than 4 digits using a written method where carrying is not needed to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Subtracts instead of adds when answering worded problems
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_165
If you triple 8 you get...
  • A. 38
  • B. 11
  • C. 888
  • D. 24

L1 eedi number_line eedi_167
What is the value of B? (Diagram: A vertical number-line with 7 horizontal, equally spaced, dashes representing the position of numbers. "-10" next to the bottom dash and "2" next to the top dash are the only numbers marked on the line. An arrow, labelled "B" is pointing at the middle dash.)
  • A. -7
  • B. -1
  • C. -4
  • D. 8

  • A. 11.47
  • B. 11.48

  • D. 11.59

L1 eedi number_line eedi_1687
What is the value of A? (Diagram: A horizontal number-line with 11 vertical, equally spaced, dashes representing the position of numbers. "-6" below the 2nd dash and "3" below the last dash are the only numbers marked on the line. An arrow, labelled "A" is pointing halfway between the 4th and 5th dashes.)
  • A. -4.5
  • B. -3.5

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1703

  • A. always true
  • B. sometimes true
  • C. never true
  • D. Need more information

⚠ Common trap: Assumes a fact without considering enough examples
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_171
What is a correct way to set up this problem to solve it using long multiplication? 426 × 82
  • A. ![426 multiplied by 82 set out to perform long multiplication. 426 is written in 3 columns with 400 in the first column, 20 in the second column and 6 in the third column. 82 is written underneath this, with 80 under 400 and 2 under 20.]()
  • B. ![426 multiplied by 82 set out to perform long multiplication. 82 is written under 426. The 8 of 82 is under the 4 of 426 and the 2 of 82 is under the 2 of 426.]()
  • C. ![426 multiplied by 82 set out to perform long multiplication. 82 is written under 426. The 8 of 82 is under the 2 of 426 and the 2 of 82 is under the 6 of 426]()
  • D. ![426 multiplied by 82 set out to perform long multiplication. 426 is written in 3 columns with 400 in the first column, 20 in the second column and 6 in the third column. 82 is written underneath this, with 80 under 20 and 2 under 6.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![426 multiplied by 82 set out to perform long multiplication. 82 is written under 426. The 8 of 82 is under the 2 of 426 and the 2 of 82 is under the 6 of 426](). A common mistake here: Hasn't lined up the place value columns when writing out the calculation. Double-check each step in multiply 3 digit integers by 2 digit integers using long multiplication to avoid this erro…
⚠ Common trap: Hasn't lined up the place value columns when writing out the calculation
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1719

  • A. Four
  • B. Six
  • C. Twelve
  • D. Twenty-four
Explanation: The correct answer is Twenty-four. A common mistake here: Does not think a number can be a factor of itself. Double-check each step in identify factors of a number to avoid this error.

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1720

  • A. 1
  • B. 13
  • C. 42
  • D. They do not have a lowest common multiple
Explanation: The correct answer is 42. A common mistake here: Confuses factors and multiples. Double-check each step in identify the lowest common multiple of two numbers to avoid this error.

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1724
