  • A. 4
  • B. 40
  • C. 400
  • D. 4000

⚠ Common trap: Does not equate the value of a number with the place value column it is in
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1729
Which of the following calculations would solve this problem? Harvey is monitoring the growth of his plant. The plant was 35 cm tall. It grew 7 cm more. How tall is the plant now?
  • A. 35 ÷ 7
  • B. 35-7
  • C. 35+7
  • D. 35 × 7
Explanation: The correct answer is 35+7. A common mistake here: Divides rather than adds when answering worded problems. Double-check each step in identify that a problem needs to be solved using addition to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Divides rather than adds when answering worded problems
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1732
321 children are going on a school trip. One adult is needed for every seven children. How many adults are needed?
  • A. 45 r6
  • B. 46
  • C. 45 6/7
  • D. 45
Explanation: The correct answer is 46. A common mistake here: Difficulty with translating a mathematical answer into a real world context. Double-check each step in interpret remainders from written division problems to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Difficulty with translating a mathematical answer into a real world context
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1770
There are 7 shelves of plates. Three of the shelves hold 17 plates each. Two of the shelves hold 21 plates each. The other shelves hold 19 plates each. How many plates are there in total?
  • A. 112
  • B. 69
  • C. 131
  • D. 57
Explanation: The correct answer is 131. A common mistake here: Does not recognise when to find a multiple from a worded question. Double-check each step in solve problems that combine addition or subtraction with multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise when to find a multiple from a worded question
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1785

  • A. 870,000
  • B. 900,000
  • C. 880,000
  • D. 876,000
Explanation: The correct answer is 880,000. A common mistake here: Rounds down instead of up. Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest other power of 10 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1789
(-20) ×(-5)=2 ×(-10) ×(-5) Is this...
  • A. true because of distributivity
  • B. true because of associativity
  • C. true because of commutativity
  • D. false
Explanation: The correct answer is true because of associativity. A common mistake here: Does not correctly apply the distributive property of multiplication. Double-check each step in recognise and use associativity of multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not correctly apply the distributive property of multiplication
L1 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_179
Dave is trying to solve this problem using Napier's bones What number does the circled digit represent? 356 × 93 (Diagram: A Napier's bones grid, with 356 along the top and 93 down the side. From left to right on the top row, with / representing a diagonal line through through the box, the boxes read 2/7, 4/5, 5/4. From left to right on the bottom row, the boxes read 0/9, 1/5, 1/8. The digit 5 is circled in the second box of the top row, which is in the column under the 5 of 356 and the row along from the 9 of 93.)
  • A. 5
  • B. 50
  • C. 500
  • D. 5000
Explanation: The correct answer is 500. A common mistake here: Does not understand the place value of answers from a Napier's bones calculation. Double-check each step in multiply 3 digit integers by 2 digit integers using napier’s bones to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand the place value of answers from a Napier's bones calculation
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1799
0.009753 What is this number rounded to 2 decimal places?
  • A. 0.0098
  • B. 0.0097
  • C. 0.01
  • D. 0.00
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.01. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (significant figures rather than decimal places). Double-check each step in round numbers to two decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (significant figures rather than decimal places)
L1 eedi number_line eedi_18
(Diagram: Number line with -12 and -7 marked. Starting at -12, follow an arrow pointing to the right labelled +10. Then there is an arrow labelled b pointing to the left, which ends at -7 on the number line.) What is the value of b ?
  • A. -7
  • B. -5
  • C. -2
  • D. -6
Explanation: The correct answer is -5. A common mistake here: Assumes that an arrow on a number line starts at zero. Double-check each step in carry out missing number subtraction problems involving negative integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Assumes that an arrow on a number line starts at zero
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1814
Tom and Katie are discussing ways to calculate 49 × 9 mentally Tom multiplies 49 by 10 and then subtracts 49 from the answer. Katie does 50 × 9 and then subtracts 9 from the answer Who would get the correct answer?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Believes that the compensation strategy for the multiplication of 2 numbers can only be used one way round. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient methods for mental multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that the compensation strategy for the multiplication of 2 numbers can only be...
L1 eedi number_line eedi_1816
What number is the arrow pointing to? (Diagram: Number line from 100 to 200 with an arrow pointing to the value 145)
  • A. 145
  • B. 140
  • C. 149
  • D. 148
Explanation: The correct answer is 145. A common mistake here: When reading integers on a number line, assumes each dash is 10. Double-check each step in read an integer on a scale where the required measurement is marked by a dash between two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading integers on a number line, assumes each dash is 10
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1821
A common factor of 8 and 20 is...
  • A. 40
  • B. 20
  • C. 8
  • D. 4
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Confuses factors and multiples. Double-check each step in identify common factors of two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses factors and multiples
L1 eedi number_line eedi_1847
Tom and Katie are trying to write calculations based on the diagram below. Tom writes: 15-20=-5 Katie writes: (-5)+20=15 Who is correct? (Diagram: Number line with an arrow marked -20 going from 15 to -5)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not recognise that addition is the inverse operation of subtraction. Double-check each step in carry out a subtraction problem with positive integers where the answer is less than 0 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise that addition is the inverse operation of subtraction
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_186
How do you write this number in digits? Fifty-six thousand and thirteen
  • A. 5613
  • B. 56030
  • C. 56013
  • D. 560013
Explanation: The correct answer is 56013. A common mistake here: Believes that they only need to write non-zero digits when giving a number in digits. Double-check each step in express words as 5 digit numbers and above to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that they only need to write non-zero digits when giving a number in digits
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1861
What is 326 rounded to the nearest 10 ?
  • A. 300
  • B. 320
  • C. 330
  • D. 350
Explanation: The correct answer is 330. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much). Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest 10 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1863
12 ÷(-4)=
  • A. -16
  • B. 16
  • C. -3
  • D. 3
Explanation: The correct answer is -3. A common mistake here: Believes dividing a positive by a negative gives a positive answer. Double-check each step in carry out division problems involving one negative integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes dividing a positive by a negative gives a positive answer
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_187
Sarah has some apples. She shares them into 6 equal groups and there is one left over. How many apples could she have?
  • A. 66
  • B. 30
  • C. 35
  • D. 43
Explanation: The correct answer is 43. A common mistake here: Thinks the remainder is the difference between the dividend and the next biggest multiple. Double-check each step in carry out mental divisions when the answer leaves a remainder to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the remainder is the difference between the dividend and the next biggest multiple
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_191
Which number is closest to zero?
  • A. -2.1
  • B. -2.01
  • C. -2.105
  • D. -3
Explanation: The correct answer is -2.01. A common mistake here: Does not know how to compare decimal numbers. Double-check each step in order negative decimals with a different number of digits to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to compare decimal numbers
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_204
How do you write this number in words? 204050
  • A. Two million and four thousand and fifty
  • B. Two hundred and four thousand and fifty
  • C. Two hundred and four and fifty
  • D. Two hundred and forty thousand and fifty
Explanation: The correct answer is Two hundred and four thousand and fifty. A common mistake here: Mistakes the place value column of hundred thousands with millions. Double-check each step in write 5 digit numbers and above as words to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mistakes the place value column of hundred thousands with millions
L1 eedi diagram eedi_205
What does the product of the two circled numbers represent? (Diagram: 3847 times 28 written as a long multiplication question. The 8 in 3847 and the 2 in 28 are circled.)
  • A. 16000
  • B. 1600
  • C. 160
  • D. 16
Explanation: The correct answer is 16000. A common mistake here: When multiplying, treats digits in other columns as if they are units. Double-check each step in interpret the value of digits in multiplication calculations using long multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When multiplying, treats digits in other columns as if they are units
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_206
If you can connect dots to draw a regular octagon in a ring of equally spaced dots, which other regular shape can you definitely draw?
  • A. Equilateral triangle
  • B. Square
  • C. Pentagon
  • D. Hexagon
Explanation: The correct answer is Square. A common mistake here: Does not use the associative property of multiplication to find other factors of a number. Double-check each step in understand the relationship between different factors of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not use the associative property of multiplication to find other factors of a number
L1 eedi number_line eedi_215
What is the value of B? (Diagram: A vertical number-line with 7 horizontal, equally spaced, dashes representing the position of numbers. "-8" next to the bottom dash and "4" next to the top dash are the only numbers marked on the line. An arrow, labelled "B" is pointing at the dash that is 2 below the "4" label and 4 above the "-8" label.)
  • A. 0
  • B. 2
  • C. -12
  • D. -4
Explanation: The correct answer is 0. A common mistake here: When reading integers on a number line, assumes each dash is 1. Double-check each step in read an integer on a scale where the required measurement is marked by a dash between two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading integers on a number line, assumes each dash is 1
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_22
Tom and Katie are discussing related calculations Tom says if you know that 3748+426=4174 , then 3748+427 must equal 4 1 7 5 Katie says if you know that 3748-426=3322 , then 3748 - 427 must equal 3 3 2 3 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Does not realise that adding a larger number gives a larger answer. Double-check each step in given the solution to one problem, deduce the answer to a related problem using the same operation to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise that adding a larger number gives a larger answer
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_232
Tom and Katie are discussing factors Tom says 3 is a common factor of 6 x and 9 y Katie says x is a common factor of 6 x and 9 y Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Does not know how to identify common factors from algebraic terms. Double-check each step in identify factors of algebraic expressions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to identify common factors from algebraic terms

Alicia wants to buy some bottles of water. Each bottle of water costs $ 1.65 Alicia has $ 10 to spend. What is the greatest number of bottles can she buy?
  • A. 10
  • B. 6
  • C. 16
  • D. 7
Explanation: The correct answer is 6. A common mistake here: Believes you can go above an amount of money when finding how much of something you can buy. Double-check each step in multiply amounts of money up to £10 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes you can go above an amount of money when finding how much of something you can...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_247

  • A. $ 13,180
  • B. $ 4,426
  • C. $ 2,970
  • D. $ 10,268

⚠ Common trap: Assumes they add all values in a worded problem, thereby not interpreting the problem c...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_258

  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Does not correctly apply the commutative property of multiplication. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient methods for mental multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not correctly apply the commutative property of multiplication
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_262
1,2,4,8 These are the only factors of...
  • A. 1
  • B. 8

  • A. 19,102
  • B. 19,099
  • C. 19,098
  • D. 19,097

  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct

  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct

  • B. Only Katie

  • C. -100 then +3

  • A. 600

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_580
Tom and Katie are discussing ways to calculate 59 × 4 mentally Tom doubles 59 and then doubles the answer Katie does 60 × 4 and then subtracts 4 from the answer Who would get the correct answer?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not think that the compensation strategy for the multiplication of 2 numbers is valid. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient methods for mental multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not think that the compensation strategy for the multiplication of 2 numbers is valid
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_619
A Zumba teacher wants to split her group of 60 clients into equal sized classes. To do this she needs to know the _________ of 60 Which word best fills in the gap?
  • A. Remainders
  • B. Fractions
  • C. Multiples
  • D. Factors
Explanation: The correct answer is Factors. A common mistake here: Does not understand that factors are divisors which split the number into equal groups. Double-check each step in recognise when to find a factor to solve a problem to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that factors are divisors which split the number into equal groups
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_641
Tom and Katie are discussing factors Tom says 4 is a common factor of 6 x and 9 y^2 Katie says y is a common factor of 6 x and 9 y^2 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Does not know how to identify common factors from algebraic terms. Double-check each step in identify factors of algebraic expressions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to identify common factors from algebraic terms
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_643
(-3) ×(-2)/(-0.2)=
  • A. -30
  • B. -3
  • C. 30
  • D. 3
Explanation: The correct answer is -30. A common mistake here: When dividing a decimal by a whole number, ignores decimal point and just divides the digits. Double-check each step in carry out division problems involving negative non-integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When dividing a decimal by a whole number, ignores decimal point and just divides the d...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_657
Tom and Katie are discussing how to order integers Tom says 5000 is greater than 4987 Katie says 3050 is greater than 3500 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Does not equate the value of a number with the place value column it is in. Double-check each step in order integers with the same number of digits to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not equate the value of a number with the place value column it is in
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_663
This is how Teddy calculates the answer to 600 ÷ 25 mentally "There are 4 lots of 25 in 100. I then multiplied 4 by 6 to get 24 . So 600 divided by 25 is 24 " Teddy uses the same method to calculate 800 ÷ 25 . Which of the following shows how he would do this?
  • A. There are 4 lots of 25 in 100 Then I multiplied 4 by 8 So the answer is 32
  • B. There are 4 lots of 25 in 100 Then I multiplied 8 by 6 So the answer is 48
  • C. There are 6 lots of 25 in 100 Then I multiplied 6 by 4 So the answer is 24
  • D. There are 8 lots of 25 in 100 Then I multiplied 8 by 6 So the answer is 48
Explanation: The correct answer is There are 4 lots of 25 in 100 Then I multiplied 4 by 8 So the answer is 32. A common mistake here: Does not copy a given method accurately. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient methods for mental division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not copy a given method accurately
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_664
Two different numbers are equal to 4.37 to two decimal places. The numbers could be....
  • A. 4.3708 and 4.3651
  • B. 4.3728 and 4.3628
  • C. 4.373 and 3.371
  • D. 4.3751 and 4.3659
Explanation: The correct answer is 4.3708 and 4.3651. A common mistake here: Rounds up instead of down. Double-check each step in round numbers to two decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds up instead of down
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_666
34590.8549 What is this number rounded to the nearest 100 ?
  • A. 34590
  • B. 34600
  • C. 34500
  • D. 35000
Explanation: The correct answer is 34600. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little). Double-check each step in round non-integers to the nearest 100 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_670
A magazine costs $ 2.70 . Isaac pays for it with a $ 5 note. How much change should he receive?
  • A. $ 2.70
  • B. $ 3.30
  • C. $ 7.70
  • D. $ 2.30
Explanation: The correct answer is $ 2.30. A common mistake here: In a worded problem, does not complete all calculations. Double-check each step in calculate the change from purchases between £1 and £5 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: In a worded problem, does not complete all calculations
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_678
What is the value of: 485+78-9
  • A. 398
  • B. 554
  • C. 572
  • D. 444
Explanation: The correct answer is 554. A common mistake here: Thinks positive or negative sign affects the term before it. Double-check each step in complete multi-step mental calculations to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks positive or negative sign affects the term before it
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_679
John has been asked to work out the answer to: 231 ÷ 4 He gets the answer 57 remainder 3 What is the exact value of that remainder?
  • A. 3
  • B. 0.34
  • C. 0.3
  • D. 0.75
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.75. A common mistake here: Does not understand that a remainder can be written as a fraction. Double-check each step in convert the reminders from written division problems into decimals to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that a remainder can be written as a fraction
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_685
A common factor of 6 and 9 is...
  • A. 3
  • B. 6
  • C. 9
  • D. 18
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Does not know the 9 timestable. Double-check each step in identify common factors of two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the 9 timestable
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_687
What is 8 6 9 8 . 9 rounded to the nearest ten?
  • A. 8699
  • B. 8700
  • C. 8690
  • D. 9000
Explanation: The correct answer is 8700. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much). Double-check each step in round non-integers to the nearest 10 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_702
0.059623 What is this number rounded to the nearest integer?
  • A. 0.06
  • B. 0.05
  • C. 1
  • D. 0
Explanation: The correct answer is 0. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little). Double-check each step in round decimals to the nearest whole number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_705
A number rounds to 20,000 when rounded to the nearest ten thousand, thousand and hundred. Which number could this be?
  • A. 19199
  • B. 20074
  • C. 20043
  • D. 19949
Explanation: The correct answer is 20043. A common mistake here: Rounds up instead of down. Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest 100 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds up instead of down
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_714
609-1000=
  • A. -401
  • B. 391
  • C. -391
  • D. This problem cannot be solved
Explanation: The correct answer is -391. A common mistake here: Performs subtraction in wrong order. Double-check each step in carry out a subtraction problem with positive integers where the answer is less than 0 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Performs subtraction in wrong order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_718
Tom and Katie are discussing factors of 1 2 y Tom says y is a factor of 12 y Katie says 3 is a factor of 12 y Who do you agree with?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not understand the term factor. Double-check each step in identify factors of algebraic expressions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand the term factor
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_719
What is 3 2 0 050 rounded to the nearest hundred?
  • A. 321000
  • B. 320050
  • C. 320100
  • D. 320000
Explanation: The correct answer is 320100. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little). Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest 100 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_73
What is 326 rounded to the nearest 1000 ?
  • A. 1000
  • B. 3000
  • C. 500
  • D. 0
Explanation: The correct answer is 0. A common mistake here: Rounds up instead of down. Double-check each step in round integers to the nearest 1000 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds up instead of down
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_749
What is 9 8 9 . 9 8 9 rounded to the nearest ten?
  • A. 990
  • B. 1000
  • C. 989
  • D. 980
Explanation: The correct answer is 990. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little). Double-check each step in round non-integers to the nearest 10 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too little)
L1 eedi diagram eedi_759
(Diagram: A rectangle with one part shaded, labelled with dimensions 20, 8 and 6) Which of the following calculations would correctly work out the area of the section shaded with a solid color?
  • A. 8 × 20-8 × 6
  • B. 8 × 20+8 × 6
  • C. 8 × 14-8 × 6
  • D. 8 × 14+8 × 6
Explanation: The correct answer is 8 × 20-8 × 6. A common mistake here: Adds instead of subtracts when using an area model for multiplication. Double-check each step in calculate the area of a rectangle where the dimensions are given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds instead of subtracts when using an area model for multiplication
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_764
"Common multiples of 2 and 5 are also multiples of 25 " Is this statement...
  • A. always true
  • B. sometimes true
  • C. never true
  • D. Impossible to say
Explanation: The correct answer is sometimes true. A common mistake here: Does not use the associative property of multiplication to find other factors of a number. Double-check each step in identify common multiples of two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not use the associative property of multiplication to find other factors of a number
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_768
What number is 10,000 more than 899,051 ?
  • A. 909,051
  • B. 809,051
  • C. 999,051
  • D. 8,990,510,000
Explanation: The correct answer is 909,051. A common mistake here: When two digits sum to 10 or more during an addition problem, does not add one to the preceding digit. Double-check each step in find another power of 10 more than a given number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When two digits sum to 10 or more during an addition problem, does not add one to the p...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_779
Tom and Katie are discussing factors Tom says 3 is a common factor of 18 x and 29 y Katie says x is a common factor of 6 x and 9 y Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Thinks a factor of the final digit is also a factor of the whole number. Double-check each step in identify factors of algebraic expressions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks a factor of the final digit is also a factor of the whole number
L1 eedi diagram eedi_791
When using long-division to solve this problem, what digit should replace the triangle to give the correct answer (quotient)? (Diagram: Division sum written out in Bus Stop format with dividend = 92, divisor = 4, the digits of the quotient are represented by a yellow star and a purple triangle.)
  • A. 3
  • B. 0
  • C. 2
  • D. 1
Explanation: The correct answer is 3. A common mistake here: Starts at the wrong end of the dividend when dividing. Double-check each step in divide integers up to 2 digits using long division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Starts at the wrong end of the dividend when dividing
L1 eedi diagram eedi_805
When using long-division to solve this problem, what digit should replace the triangle to give the correct answer (quotient)? (Diagram: Division sum written out in Bus Stop format with dividend = 84, divisor = 6, quotient is represented by a yellow star and a purple triangle.)
  • A. 1
  • B. 2
  • C. 4
  • D. 0
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Starts at the wrong end of the dividend when dividing. Double-check each step in divide integers up to 2 digits using long division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Starts at the wrong end of the dividend when dividing
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_824
Amelie is counting backwards in steps of 4 . She starts at the number 24,993 what is the next number she will say?
  • A. 24,988
  • B. 24,992
  • C. 24,990
  • D. 24,989
Explanation: The correct answer is 24,989. A common mistake here: Counts on by 1, when asked to count forward/backwards in a step other than 1. Double-check each step in count backwards in steps of of something other than one to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Counts on by 1, when asked to count forward/backwards in a step other than 1
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_825
If 12 is a factor of a number, then another factor of that number must be...
  • A. 5 and 7
  • B. 4
  • C. 24
  • D. Impossible to say
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Believes numbers are divisible by numbers which sum to their factors. Double-check each step in understand the relationship between different factors of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes numbers are divisible by numbers which sum to their factors
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_826
9.4 × 50=
  • A. 4700
  • B. 420
  • C. 940
  • D. 470

⚠ Common trap: When multiplying a decimal by an integer, ignores decimal point and just multiplies the...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_846
Tom and Katie are trying to calculate the answer to 19 × 8 Tom does this: 20 × 8-1 × 8 Katie does this: 10 × 8+9 × 8 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not correctly apply the distributive property of multiplication. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient methods for mental multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not correctly apply the distributive property of multiplication
L1 eedi diagram eedi_848
Here is the completed Venn diagram from earlier in the quiz. What calculation works out the lowest common multiple of 36 and 90 ? (Diagram: A Venn diagram showing the Prime factors of 36 set and the Prime factors of 90 set. There is a 2 in the prime factors of 36 set, there is 2, 3, 3 in the intersection and there is a 5 in the prime factors of 50 set.)
  • A. 2 × 2 × 3 × 3 × 5
  • B. 2 × 3 × 3
  • C. 2 × 5
  • D. 2 × 3 × 5
Explanation: The correct answer is 2 × 2 × 3 × 3 × 5. A common mistake here: Confuses the calculations for HCF and LCM. Double-check each step in use prime factor decomposition to find the lowest common multiple of two numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses the calculations for HCF and LCM
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_849
What number is this CCXLIX?
  • A. 271
  • B. 269
  • C. 249
  • D. 259
Explanation: The correct answer is 249. A common mistake here: When finding the value of a number given in roman numerals, adds the value of all letters together without considering the position of the letter. Double-check each step in can read roman numerals between 100 and 1000 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When finding the value of a number given in roman numerals, adds the value of all lette...
L1 eedi number_line eedi_866
(Diagram: A number line going from -8 up to 7) Asha's office is on level 7 . Her car is parked in the basement on level -2 . How many floors does Asha need to go down to get from the office to her car?
  • A. 10
  • B. 5
  • C. 9
  • D. 7
Explanation: The correct answer is 9. A common mistake here: Counts the starting number when counting backwards. Double-check each step in count backwards through zero starting from a positive integer to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Counts the starting number when counting backwards
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_875
Round 27.48 to one decimal place
  • A. 27.4
  • B. 27
  • C. 27.5
  • D. 28.0
Explanation: The correct answer is 27.5. A common mistake here: Rounds down instead of up. Double-check each step in round numbers to one decimal place to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_878
What is the value of the 2 in the number below? 12030
  • A. 20000
  • B. 2000
  • C. 200
  • D. 2
Explanation: The correct answer is 2000. A common mistake here: When asked for the value of a digit, gives an answer 10 times too big. Double-check each step in recognise the place value of each digit in integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When asked for the value of a digit, gives an answer 10 times too big
L1 eedi number_line eedi_882
(Diagram: Number line with -7 and -5 marked on it. From the -7 there is an arrow to the right labelled +5. Then there is an arrow pointing to the left labelled b and ending at -5.) What is the value of b ?
  • A. -7
  • B. -4
  • C. -2
  • D. -3
Explanation: The correct answer is -3. A common mistake here: Counts the starting number when counting backwards. Double-check each step in carry out missing number subtraction problems involving negative integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Counts the starting number when counting backwards
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_898
Which statement about the temperatures -5^° C and 2^° C is true?
  • A. 2^° C is 3^° C hotter than -5^° C
  • B. 2^° C is 7^° C hotter than -5^° C
  • C. 2^° C is 3^° C colder than -5^° C
  • D. 2^° C is 7^° C colder than -5^° C
Explanation: The correct answer is 2^° C is 7^° C hotter than -5^° C. A common mistake here: Ignores the negative sign. Double-check each step in perform calculations with temperature when given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Ignores the negative sign
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_912
Nick wants to subtract 199 from a number. Which one of the following methods would give him the correct answer?
  • A. +200 then +1
  • B. -200 then +1
  • C. -200 then -1
  • D. +200 then -1
Explanation: The correct answer is -200 then +1. A common mistake here: Does not understand the effect of consecutive operations. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient strategies for mental subtraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand the effect of consecutive operations
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_916
Jo and Paul are arguing about rounding. Jo says 9 9 . 9 6 rounded to 1 significant figure is 1 0 0 Paul says 9 9 . 9 6 rounded to 1 decimal place is 1 0 0 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Jo. A common mistake here: When rounding to decimal places and the only digits after the decimal point will be zeros, just gives the integer as the answer. Double-check each step in round numbers greater than 1 to one significant figure to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When rounding to decimal places and the only digits after the decimal point will be zer...

  • A. 20.153
  • B. 20.15
  • C. 20.154
  • D. 20.253
Explanation: The correct answer is 20.153. A common mistake here: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much). Double-check each step in round numbers to three or more decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to the wrong degree of accuracy (rounds too much)
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_926
Emma has 24 cakes to give away to her friends. To work out what numbers of friends Emma could give equal amounts of cake to without having any cakes left over, Emma needs to know the ___ of 24
  • A. factors
  • B. digits
  • C. multiples
  • D. reciprocal
Explanation: The correct answer is factors. A common mistake here: Does not recognise when to find a factor from a worded question. Double-check each step in recognise when to find a factor to solve a problem to avoid this error.

  • A. 640

L1 eedi word_problem eedi_947
Tom and Katie are discussing the conjecture: All numbers have an even number of factors because factors come in pairs Tom says a counter-example is 24 Katie says a counter-example is 16 Who do you agree with?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Chooses an example when asked for a counter-example. Double-check each step in identify factors of a number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Chooses an example when asked for a counter-example

  • A. 324,976
  • B. 3250.76
  • C. 325,176
  • D. 324,076
Explanation: The correct answer is 324,976. A common mistake here: Divides rather than subtracts when given the command 'less than'. Double-check each step in find 100 less than a given number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Divides rather than subtracts when given the command 'less than'
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_954
4514-328=
  • A. 4196
  • B. 1234
  • C. 4214
  • D. 4186

Which working out is correct for $72 × 36$?
  • A. ![ Long multiplication for 72 multiplied by 36 with incorrect working and incorrect final answer. First row of working is incorrect: 4 2 2. Second row of working is incorrect: 2 7. Final answer is incorrect: 4 4 9.]()
  • B. ![ Long multiplication for 72 multiplied by 36 with incorrect working and incorrect final answer. First row of working is correct: 4 3 2. Second row of working is incorrect: 2 1 6. Final answer is incorrect: 6 4 8.]()
  • C. ![ Long multiplication for 72 multiplied by 36 with correct working and incorrect final answer. First row of working is correct: 4 3 2. Second row of working is correct: 2 1 6 0. Final answer is incorrect: 2 7 0 0.]()
  • D. ![ Long multiplication for 72 multiplied by 36 with correct working and correct final answer. First row of working is correct: 4 3 2. Second row of working is correct: 2 1 6 0. Final answer is correct: 2 5 9 2.]()

Second row of working is correct: 2 1 6 0. Final answer is correct: 2 5 9 2.](). A common mistake here: When two digits multiply to 10 or more during a multiplication problem, does not add carried value to the preceding digit. Double-check eac…

L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_965
Tom and Katie are discussing mental addition and subtraction strategies. Tom says a good way to add 9 is to add 10 and then subtract 1 . Katie says a good way to subtract 9 is to subtract 10 and then subtract 1 . Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Does not understand the effect of consecutive subtractions. Double-check each step in recognise and use efficient strategies for mental subtraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand the effect of consecutive subtractions
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_969
Which statement about the temperatures -7^° C and 5^° C is true?
  • A. 5^° C is 2^° C colder than -7^° C
  • B. 5^° C is 12^° C colder than -7^° C
  • C. 5^° C is 2^° C hotter than -7^° C
  • D. 5^° C is 12^° C hotter than -7^° C
Explanation: The correct answer is 5^° C is 12^° C hotter than -7^° C. A common mistake here: Does not know that 7 and -7 are different. Double-check each step in perform calculations with temperature when given in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know that 7 and -7 are different
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_971
For which of these questions would you calculate 50+25 ?
  • A. Decrease 50 by 25 .
  • B. Increase 50 by 25 .
  • C. Find the product of 50 and 25 .
  • D. How many 25 s are there in 50 ?
Explanation: The correct answer is Increase 50 by 25 .. A common mistake here: Adds rather than subtracts when answering worded problems. Double-check each step in identify command words that mean addition to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds rather than subtracts when answering worded problems
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_977
Which of the numbers is the same when rounded to 2 decimal places or to 2 significant figures?
  • A. 15.983
  • B. 0.0199
  • C. 0.038
  • D. 0.472
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.472. A common mistake here: Rounds to wrong degree of accuracy (decimal places rather than significant figures). Double-check each step in round numbers between 0 and 1 to two significant figures to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds to wrong degree of accuracy (decimal places rather than significant figures)
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_994
A number, n , has been rounded to 2 decimal places to give 4 . 0 0 What is the smallest n can be?
  • A. 3.9
  • B. 3.995
  • C. 3.99
  • D. 3.9995
Explanation: The correct answer is 3.995. A common mistake here: Gives a rounded whole number instead of a decimal. Double-check each step in find the lower bound of a number rounded to 2 decimal places to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Gives a rounded whole number instead of a decimal
L1 inline_ts np-1-2
If n is an odd integer, which expression is always even?
Explanation: An odd number plus 1 is always even. For example, if n = 5, then n + 1 = 6.
L1 inline_ts np-1-3
What is the greatest common factor of 18 and 42?
Explanation: Factors of 18 include 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18. Factors of 42 include 1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 14, 21, 42. The greatest common factor is 6.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t05_q09
How many different positive integers are factors of both 28 and 42?
  • A. 1
  • B. 2
  • C. 3
  • D. 4
  • E. More than 4
Explanation: 28 = 2²×7. 42 = 2×3×7. GCF = 2×7 = 14. Factors of 14: 1, 2, 7, 14 → 4 factors.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t15_q10
All of the following statements about rational and/or irrational numbers must be true EXCEPT:
  • A. the sum of any two rational numbers is rational.
  • B. the product of any two rational numbers is rational.
  • C. the sum of any two irrational numbers is irrational.
  • D. the product of a rational and an irrational number may be rational or irrational.
  • E. the product of any two irrational numbers is irrational.
Explanation: Step 1: Test each statement. F: sum of two rationals = rational ✓ always true. G: product of two rationals = rational ✓ always true. H: sum of two irrationals = irrational — FALSE: √2+(-√2)=0 (rational). J: product of rational and irrational may be either ✓ true. K: product of two irrationals is irrational — FALSE: √2×√2=2 (rational). Step 2: K is the most clearly stated false claim with a clean c…
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1034
Which number line shows a good estimate for the position of the number shown?
  • A. ![A number line from 0 to 10000 with the value 857 incorrectly labelled, with an arrow, between the midpoint and 10000]()
  • B. ![A number line from 0 to 1000 with the value 499 incorrectly labelled, with an arrow, just after the midpoint]()
  • C. ![A number line from 1000 to 2000 with the value 1250 correctly labelled, with an arrow, between 1000 and the midpoint]()
  • D. ![A number line from 0 to 10000 with the value 8967 incorrectly labelled, with an arrow, after the midpoint]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A number line from 1000 to 2000 with the value 1250 correctly labelled, with an arrow, between 1000 and the midpoint](). A common mistake here: Reads 10,000 as 1000. Double-check each step in place or identify the location of an integer between 100 and 1000 on a number line to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Reads 10,000 as 1000

L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1257
Problem 1: What is the product of 17 and 32? Problem 2: Each person in a group of 17 people owns 32 pieces of art. How many pieces of art does the group own in total? Which of these problems needs multiplication to solve it?
  • A. Only Problem 1
  • B. Only Problem 2
  • C. Both problems
  • D. Neither problem
Explanation: The correct answer is Both problems. A common mistake here: Does not recognise when to find a multiple from a worded question. Double-check each step in identify that a problem needs to be solved using multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise when to find a multiple from a worded question

  • A. 0

L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1346
Tom and Katie are discussing how to estimate the answer to 38.8745 ÷ 7.9302 Tom says 40 ÷ 7.9302 would give an overestimate. Katie says 38.8745 ÷ 8 would give an overestimate. Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Believes that the larger the divisor, the larger the answer. Double-check each step in identify when rounding a calculation will give an over or under approximation to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that the larger the divisor, the larger the answer
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1375
Jane measures the length and width of her rectangular classroom as 9.1 m and 11.7 m . Which calculation will give the best estimate of the area?
  • A. 10 × 12
  • B. 9 × 11
  • C. 9 × 12
  • D. 10 × 11
Explanation: The correct answer is 9 × 12. A common mistake here: Rounds every number up when estimating. Double-check each step in round decimals to the nearest whole number to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Rounds every number up when estimating
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1549
Jo and Paul are discussing how to estimate the answer to 188.945 ÷ 15.3382 Jo says 200 ÷ 15.3382 would give an overestimate. Paul says 188.945 ÷ 15 would give an overestimate. Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Jo and Paul. A common mistake here: Believes that the smaller a divisor is, the smaller the answer will be. Double-check each step in identify when rounding a calculation will give an over or under approximation to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that the smaller a divisor is, the smaller the answer will be

  • A. Lowest Common Multiple
  • B. Highest Common Factor
  • C. Product

What is the smallest number of each packet that Sophie can buy? To solve this problem you need to work out the ___ of 18 and 15
  • A. Product
  • B. Sum
  • C. Lowest Common Multiple
  • D. Highest Common Factor

L2 inline_ts np-2-2
What is the least common multiple of 12 and 18?
Explanation: Prime factorize: 12 = 2²·3 and 18 = 2·3². Use the highest power of each prime: 2²·3² = 4·9 = 36.
L2 inline_ts np-2-3
When 5 consecutive integers are added, the sum is 80. What is the middle integer?
Explanation: For consecutive integers, the average equals the middle integer. The average is 80 ÷ 5 = 16, so the middle integer is 16.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t04_q14
If the least common multiple of 9, 10, 12, and v is 540, which of the following could be v ?
  • A. 18
  • B. 24
  • C. 27
  • D. 36
  • E. 45
Explanation: Step 1: Find the LCM of the given numbers using prime factorization. Step 2: For each prime base, take the highest power appearing in any factorization. Step 3: Multiply together to get the LCM. The specific answer 27 = 3³ means the LCM involves only the prime 3 — likely the question gives numbers like 9 and 27 (LCM=27) or asks for the smallest number divisible by specific factors of 27.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t10_q11
If m and n are both factors of 75, which of the following could equal m + n:
  • A. 12
  • B. 18
  • C. 22
  • D. 36
  • E. 54
Explanation: Factors of 75: 1, 3, 5, 15, 25, 75. Check each choice: F=12: no two factors sum to 12 (1+11 no, 3+9 no — 9 not a factor). G=18: 3+15=18 ✓. H=22: 22=2+20? no, 7+15? 7 not a factor. J=36: 11+25? 11 not a factor. K=54: 25+29? no. Answer: G. 18.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t11_q12
If a number sequence begins 1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 10, 12 . . ., which of the following numbers does NOT appear in the sequence?
  • A. 34
  • B. 43
  • C. 57
  • D. 65
  • E. 72
Explanation: Step 1: Identify the pattern: 1,3,4,6,7,9,10,12 — differences are +2,+1,+2,+1,+2,+1,+2. The sequence skips every 3rd integer starting from 2. Step 2: Missing integers: 2,5,8,11,14,... — these are all ≡ 2 (mod 3). Step 3: A number is IN the sequence iff it is NOT ≡ 2 (mod 3). Step 4: Test each choice: F.34: 34=33+1 → 34 mod 3=1 → IN sequence. G.43: 43=42+1 → 43 mod 3=1 → IN sequence. H.57: 57…
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t13_q12
What is the least common multiple of 25, 16, and 40?
  • A. 27
  • B. 32
  • C. 320
  • D. 400
  • E. 16,000
Explanation: Step 1: Factor each: 25=5², 16=2⁴, 40=2³×5. Step 2: LCM = product of highest prime powers: 2⁴×5²=16×25=400 → J.

  • A. 0.07
  • B. 0.1
  • C. 0.7

L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_886
Round 0.0572 to 1 significant figure
  • A. 0.05
  • B. 0.1
  • C. 0
  • D. 0.06

⚠ Common trap: Rounds down instead of up
L3 inline_ts np-3-2
The product of three consecutive positive integers is divisible by which number no matter where the sequence starts?
Explanation: Among any three consecutive integers, one is divisible by 3 and at least one is even. Therefore their product is always divisible by 2 × 3 = 6.
L3 inline_ts np-3-3
If p is a prime number greater than 3, what is the remainder when p² is divided by 12?
Explanation: A prime greater than 3 is odd and not divisible by 3, so it is congruent to 1, 5, 7, or 11 mod 12. Squaring any of these gives remainder 1 mod 12. For example, 5² = 25 leaves remainder 1.

Order of Operations

foundational · 34 questions · order_of_operations
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_0
3 × 2+4-5 Where do the brackets need to go to make the answer equal 13 ?
  • A. 3 ×(2+4)-5
  • B. 3 × 2+(4-5)
  • C. 3 ×(2+4-5)
  • D. Does not need brackets
Explanation: The correct answer is 3 ×(2+4)-5. A common mistake here: Confuses the order of operations, believes addition comes before multiplication . Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving powers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses the order of operations, believes addition comes before multiplication
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1032
5+4 × 3=
  • A. 27
  • B. 17
  • C. 60
  • D. 12
Explanation: The correct answer is 17. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and/or division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1195
2 × 3^2=
  • A. 18
  • B. 12
  • C. 36
  • D. 11
Explanation: The correct answer is 18. A common mistake here: Mixes up squaring and multiplying by 2 or doubling. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving powers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up squaring and multiplying by 2 or doubling
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1310
Which calculation should you do first? ((0.5 ÷(0.2+0.4)-0.3) × 0.6)
  • A. 0.5 ÷ 0.2
  • B. 0.2+0.4
  • C. 0.4-0.3
  • D. 0.3 × 0.6
Explanation: The correct answer is 0.2+0.4. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in answer decimal problems that require the use of bidmas to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1311
12 × 6-1+2 Where do the brackets need to go to make the answer equal 36 ?
  • A. 12 × 6-(1+2)
  • B. 12 ×(6-1+2)
  • C. 12 ×(6-(1+2))
  • D. Does not need brackets
Explanation: The correct answer is 12 ×(6-(1+2)). A common mistake here: Performs subtraction in wrong order. Double-check each step in insert brackets to make a calculation correct to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Performs subtraction in wrong order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1343
12 ÷ 2+1 × 2=
  • A. 9
  • B. 3
  • C. 14
  • D. 8
Explanation: The correct answer is 8. A common mistake here: Confuses the order of operations, believes addition comes before division. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and/or division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses the order of operations, believes addition comes before division
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1390
1/3+1/2 × 1/3=
  • A. 1/2
  • B. 5/18
  • C. 2/9
  • D. 1/15
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/2. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in answer fraction problems that require the use of bidmas to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1401
a, b and c are different numbers a+b/c=(a+b) ÷ c Is the above relationship...
  • A. always true
  • B. sometimes true
  • C. never true
  • D. Need more information
Explanation: The correct answer is always true. A common mistake here: Does not realize we can write a division as a fraction. Double-check each step in write algebraic expressions with correct algebraic convention to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realize we can write a division as a fraction
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1465
What does the following equal? 3-5+4 × 2
  • A. 6
  • B. 4
  • C. -12
  • D. -10
Explanation: The correct answer is 6. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and/or division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1474
6-3+2 Tom and Katie are trying to make the calculation above equal to 5 . Tom says you can write it like this: (6-3)+2 Katie says you can leave it like this: 6-3+2 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Applies BIDMAS in strict order (does not realize addition and subtraction, and multiplication and division, are of equal priority). Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving brackets to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Applies BIDMAS in strict order (does not realize addition and subtraction, and multipli...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1493
8-7 ÷ 6^3= Which calculation should you do first?
  • A. 6^3
  • B. 7 ÷ 6
  • C. 8-7
  • D. It doesn't matter: you get the same answer
Explanation: The correct answer is 6^3. A common mistake here: Believes division is carried out before a power. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving powers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes division is carried out before a power
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1508
3+6 ÷ 2+1=
  • A. 7
  • B. 3
  • C. 5.5
  • D. 5
Explanation: The correct answer is 7. A common mistake here: Confuses the order of operations, believes addition comes before division. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and/or division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses the order of operations, believes addition comes before division
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1512
√(8^2)=
  • A. 8
  • B. 64
  • C. 4
  • D. 16
Explanation: The correct answer is 8. A common mistake here: Has completed only one of the two operations. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving roots to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Has completed only one of the two operations
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1529
0.4-1/5 × 3=
  • A. 0.2
  • B. 0.6
  • C. -0.2
  • D. 0.06
Explanation: The correct answer is -0.2. A common mistake here: Believes subtraction is commutative . Double-check each step in answer decimal problems that require the use of bidmas to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes subtraction is commutative
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1574
Every week for 4 weeks, Cara buys a magazine for $ 6 and gets her nails painted for $ 28 . How much money does Cara spend altogether?
  • A. $ 34
  • B. $ 52
  • C. $ 118
  • D. $ 136
Explanation: The correct answer is $ 136. A common mistake here: Forgets to multiply up for the correct time frame in a worded question. Double-check each step in solve problems that combine addition or subtraction with multiplication to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Forgets to multiply up for the correct time frame in a worded question
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1728
3/4-1/4 × 1/5=
  • A. 2/16
  • B. 1/10
  • C. 7/10
  • D. 5/10
Explanation: The correct answer is 7/10. A common mistake here: Does not find a common denominator when adding/subtracting fractions. Double-check each step in answer fraction problems that require the use of bidmas to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not find a common denominator when adding/subtracting fractions
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1750
1/4 ×(2/3)^2=
  • A. 1/36
  • B. 1/6
  • C. 1/9
  • D. 1/4
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/9. A common mistake here: Believes squaring a fraction requires you to double both parts. Double-check each step in answer fraction problems that require the use of bidmas to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes squaring a fraction requires you to double both parts
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1845
Tom and Katie are discussing calculations involving square roots. Tom says √(16)+√(9)=5 Katie says √(16+9)=5 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: When adding roots, adds the numbers under the roots. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving roots to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When adding roots, adds the numbers under the roots
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1866
What does the following equal? 8-7+10 × 3
  • A. 36
  • B. 31
  • C. -29
  • D. 33
Explanation: The correct answer is 31. A common mistake here: Applies BIDMAS in strict order (does not realize addition and subtraction, and multiplication and division, are of equal priority). Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and/or division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Applies BIDMAS in strict order (does not realize addition and subtraction, and multipli...
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_304
1+2 ×(3-4)= Which calculation should you do first?
  • A. 1+2
  • B. 2 × 3
  • C. 3-4
  • D. It doesn't matter: you get the same answer
Explanation: The correct answer is 3-4. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving brackets to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_306
Problem P: 4-5+6= Problem Q: 4+5 × 6= Which calculation should you do first in each problem?
  • A. Problem P: 4-5 Problem Q: 4+5
  • B. Problem P: 4-5 Problem Q: 5 × 6
  • C. Problem P: 5+6 Problem Q: 4+5
  • D. Problem P: 5+6 Problem Q: 5 × 6
Explanation: The correct answer is Problem P: 4-5 Problem Q: 5 × 6. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and/or division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi diagram eedi_361
(Diagram: Think of a number, then subtract 5 , next divide by 8 , next add 4, and finally multiply by 7 .) Jo says you can write this as: 7((n-5)/8+4) Paul says you can write this as: 7((n-5) ÷ 8)+4 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Jo. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in solve problems that combine addition and subtraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from left to right regardless of priority order
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_435
Which calculation is equal to -20 ?
  • A. 2 ×(-2)-(-4) × 4
  • B. -28-(-4) × 2
  • C. (-5)^2+5
  • D. (-42) ÷(-2)+1
Explanation: The correct answer is -28-(-4) × 2. A common mistake here: Does not realise that subtracting a negative is the same as adding it's additive inverse. Double-check each step in answer negative number problems that require the use of bidmas to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise that subtracting a negative is the same as adding it's additive inverse
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_471
Summer buys a dress for $ 73 and two pairs of shoes for $ 41 each. She pays using a $ 40 gift voucher and some cash. How much cash does she pay?
  • A. $115
  • B. $154
  • C. $188
  • D. $195
Explanation: The correct answer is $115. A common mistake here: Adds rather than subtracts when answering worded problems. Double-check each step in solve problems that combine addition and subtraction to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Adds rather than subtracts when answering worded problems
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_507
a, b and c are different numbers a/b+c=a ÷(b+c) Is the above relationship...
  • A. never true
  • B. sometimes true
  • C. always true
  • D. Need more information
Explanation: The correct answer is always true. A common mistake here: Does not understand that the numerator and denominator of fractions represent groupings and have the same order of priority as brackets. Double-check each step in solve two-step linear equations, with the variable on one side, with all positive integers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that the numerator and denominator of fractions represent groupings...

  • A. Problem P: 3+5 Problem Q: 3 × 5
  • B. Problem P: 3+5 Problem Q: 6 ÷ 3
  • C. Problem P: 6 ÷ 3 Problem Q: 3 × 5
  • D. Problem P: 6 ÷ 3 Problem Q: 6 ÷ 3
Explanation: The correct answer is Problem P: 6 ÷ 3 Problem Q: 6 ÷ 3. A common mistake here: Carries out operations from right to left regardless of priority order. Double-check each step in use the order of operations to carry out calculations involving addition, subtraction, multiplication, and/or division to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Carries out operations from right to left regardless of priority order

  • A. Only Jo
  • B. Only Paul
  • C. Both Jo and Paul
  • D. Neither is correct
