L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_767
Which of these statements is false?
  • A. "All circles are similar"
  • B. "All equilateral triangles are similar"
  • C. "All rhombuses are similar"
  • D. "All regular decagons are similar"
Explanation: The correct answer is "All rhombuses are similar". A common mistake here: Does not realise that all circles are similar. Double-check each step in identify whether two shapes are similar or not to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not realise that all circles are similar
L3 act_master word_problem act_math_t03_q11
Sebastian bought a meal at a restaurant and left a 15% tip. With the tip, he paid exactly $35.19. How much did the meal cost without the tip?
  • A. $28.98
  • B. $29.91
  • C. $30.15
  • D. $30.60
  • E. $30.85
Explanation: Step 1: Let the meal cost (before tip) be m. A 15% tip means the total paid is m + 0.15m = 1.15m. Step 2: 1.15m = 35.19. Step 3: m = 35.19 / 1.15 = 30.60.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t09_q15
Of the 126 students who applied for a full scholarship at Oxbow College, 9 received one. What is the ratio of students who received a scholarship to those who didn’t?
  • A. 1 to 10
  • B. 1 to 11
  • C. 1 to 12
  • D. 1 to 13
  • E. 1 to 14
Explanation: 9 received scholarships, 126–9=117 did not. Ratio recipients:non-recipients = 9:117 = 1:13.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t10_q12
The following graph shows the percentage of shares controlled by five owners of a startup software company. Zach intends to sell his shares equally among the other four owners. After this sale is final, what percentage of the company will Bell own?
  • A. 25.25%
  • B. 27.25%
  • C. 27.50%
  • D. 39.90%
  • E. 34%
Explanation: Step 1: Read the pie chart values for each segment: Currer=21%, Ellis=22%, Acton=23%, Bell=25%, Zach=9%. Step 2: Identify which segments the question asks to combine. Step 3: If the question asks for Bell + Zach combined: 25% + 9% = 34%. Step 4: Answer: E. 34%.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t14_q13
Two similar triangles have perimeters in the ratio 5:6. The sides of the larger triangle measure 12 in, 7 in, and 5 in. What is the perimeter, in inches, of the smaller triangle?
  • A. 18
  • B. 20
  • C. 22
  • D. 24
  • E. 32
Explanation: Step 1: Larger triangle sides: 12+7+5=24 in. Step 2: Perimeter ratio = 5:6 (smaller:larger). Step 3: Smaller perimeter = 24×(5/6)=20 in → G.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q14
Line segments , , and are parallel and intersect line segments and as shown in the figure below. The ratio of the perimeter of Î_x0094_ FJK to the perimeter of Î_x0094_ FLM is 3:5, and the ratio of to is 1:5. What is the ratio of to ?
  • A. 1:05
  • B. 1:03
  • C. 1:02
  • D. 2:01
  • E. 5:03
Explanation: Step 1: GH∥LM; FH:FM=1:5 → similarity ratio ΔFGH/ΔFLM=1:5 → FG:FL=1/5. Step 2: ΔFJK∥LM; perimeter ratio ΔFJK:ΔFLM=3:5 → FJ:FL=3/5. Step 3: GJ=FJ-FG=(3/5-1/5)FL=(2/5)FL. Step 4: GJ:FG=(2/5):(1/5)=2:1 → J.
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1658
Which of the following graphs show that x is inversely proportional to y ?
  • A. ![A graph with a straight line going from bottom left to top right. The graph starts from above the origin on the y axis. ]()
  • B. ![A graph that starts in the top left, drops steeply down as you move right, briefly levels off, then steeply up back to the same level as the starting point, forming a symmetrical U shape]()
  • C. ![A graph with a straight line going from bottom left to top right. The graph starts from 0 on the y axis. ]()
  • D. ![A graph starting at the top left and rapidly decreasing as it moves right before slowing down and starting to level off ]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A graph starting at the top left and rapidly decreasing as it moves right before slowing down and starting to level off ](). A common mistake here: Confuses reciprocal and quadratic graphs. Double-check each step in recognise graphical representations of inverse proportion in the form y = k/x to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses reciprocal and quadratic graphs
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1663
This graph shows the relationship between journey time and the speed of a car. How long will the journey take if the car travels at 30 mph ? (Diagram: A graph showing speed in miles per hour on the x axis and journey time in hours on the y axis. A curve is shown that has a steep negative gradient to start with, that gradually becomes less steep until it is nearly horizontal. The curve passes through the points (8,30) and (30,7))
  • A. 7 hours
  • B. 8 hours
  • C. 30 hours
  • D. 15 hours
Explanation: The correct answer is 7 hours. A common mistake here: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes. Double-check each step in read values off a real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_218
Which of the following graphs is most likely to represent the change in journey time against average speed?
  • A. ![A straight line graph starting at the origin and travelling right with a positive gradient.]()
  • B. ![A curved graph that starts at the origin. As you move right it travels steeply up, then levels off]()
  • C. ![A curved graph that starts up and right of the origin. As you move right it travels steeply down, then levels off. but never reaches the x-axis.]()
  • D. ![A straight line graph starting partway up the y-axis and travelling right with a negative gradient until it hits the x-axis.]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A curved graph that starts up and right of the origin. As you move right it travels steeply down, then levels off. but never reaches the x-axis.](). A common mistake here: Thinks the faster you travel the longer the time it will take. Double-check each step in given a situation sketch an appropriate non linear real life graph to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the faster you travel the longer the time it will take
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_771
Which of the following graphs is most likely to illustrate the time taken to complete a job (t) changes depending on the number of people working on it (p)
  • A. ![A straight line from bottom left to top right, starting from above the origin ]()
  • B. ![A straight line from top left to bottom right, starting from above the origin]()
  • C. ![A curved line from top left rapidly descending as it moves right before slowing down and levelling out ]()
  • D. ![A straight line from bottom left to top right, starting from the origin]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![A curved line from top left rapidly descending as it moves right before slowing down and levelling out ](). A common mistake here: Believes that graphs of inverse proportion meet the axes. Double-check each step in recognise graphical representations of inverse proportion in the form y = k/x to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes that graphs of inverse proportion meet the axes

Right Triangle Geometry

core · 5 questions · right_triangle_geometry
L1 generated word_problem gen-right_triangle_geometry-1-word-problem-1
Tom is building a treehouse and needs to place a ladder from the ground to the top of the treehouse, which is 12 feet off the ground. If the ladder is 15 feet long and forms a right angle with the ground, how far from the base of the tree is the ladder?
  • A. 9 feet
  • B. 12 feet
  • C. 15 feet
  • D. 20 feet
Explanation: Since the ladder forms a right angle with the ground, we can use the Pythagorean theorem: a^2 + b^2 = c^2, where c is the length of the ladder (15 feet) and a is the height of the treehouse (12 feet). We need to solve for b, which is the distance from the base of the tree. So, b^2 = c^2 - a^2 = 15^2 - 12^2 = 225 - 144 = 81. Then, b = sqrt(81) = 9 feet.
L1 generated word_problem gen-right_triangle_geometry-1-word-problem-2
A rectangular picture frame has a length of 18 inches and a width of 12 inches. What is the length of the diagonal of the frame?
  • A. 15 inches
  • B. 18 inches
  • C. 20.78 inches
  • D. 25 inches
Explanation: To find the length of the diagonal, we can use the Pythagorean theorem: a^2 + b^2 = c^2, where a is the length of the frame (18 inches), b is the width of the frame (12 inches), and c is the length of the diagonal. So, c^2 = 18^2 + 12^2 = 324 + 144 = 468. Then, c = sqrt(468) = 21.63 inches, which rounds to 20.78 inches (rounded to two decimal places).
L2 eedi diagram eedi_102
What is value of p in this compound shape made with rectangles? (Diagram: Compound shape made of two rectangles with the sides labelled p (in red) cm, 15 cm, 12 cm, 7 cm and 7 cm. One side is unlabelled)
  • A. 5 cm
  • B. 6 cm
  • C. 7 cm
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 5 cm. A common mistake here: Makes an assumption about line segments being equal within a shape. Double-check each step in calculate missing lengths of shapes by geometrical inference, where the lengths given are in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Makes an assumption about line segments being equal within a shape
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1170
A right-angled tringle has sides a, b, and c as shown. Choose the formula for finding side a. (Diagram: Right-angled triangle with height a, base b and hypotenuse c.)
  • A. a=c-b
  • B. a^2=b^2+c^2
  • C. a^2=b^2-c^2
  • D. a^2=c^2-b^2
Explanation: The correct answer is a^2=c^2-b^2. A common mistake here: Thinks that the square root of an expression square roots each term in the expression, rather than square rooting the whole expression. Double-check each step in recall the correct formula to calculate the length of a non-hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle in a 2d pythagoras questions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks that the square root of an expression square roots each term in the expression,...
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1230
What is value of p in this compound shape made with rectangles? (Diagram: Compound shape made of two rectangles with the sides labelled p cm, 10 cm, 7 cm, 3 cm and 4 cm. One side is unlabelled.)
  • A. 4 cm
  • B. 3 cm
  • C. 3.5 cm
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 3 cm. A common mistake here: Makes an assumption about line segments being equal within a shape. Double-check each step in calculate missing lengths of shapes by geometrical inference, where the lengths given are in the same units to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Makes an assumption about line segments being equal within a shape

Sequences and Series

core · 58 questions · sequences_series
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t01_q01
Which of the following numbers completes the sequence 3, 8, 14, 21, 29, ___?
  • A. 35
  • B. 36
  • C. 37
  • D. 38
  • E. 39
Explanation: Step 1: List successive differences: 8−3=5, 14−8=6, 21−14=7, 29−21=8. Step 2: Identify the pattern — each difference increases by 1. Step 3: Next difference = 8+1 = 9. Step 4: Next term = 29+9 = 38.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t03_q02
On his first day working out, Anthony did 30 push-ups. On each successive day, he did exactly 3 more push-ups than on the previous day. After completing his push-ups on the 30th day, how many push-ups had he completed on all 30 days?
  • A. fewer than 500
  • B. between 500 and 1,000
  • C. between 1,000 and 1,500
  • D. between 1,500 and 2,000
  • E. more than 2,000
Explanation: Step 1: Recognize an arithmetic sequence: a1=30, common difference d=3, n=30 days. Step 2: Use the sum formula S = n/2 * (2a1 + (n-1)d). Step 3: S = 15 * (60 + 29*3) = 15 * (60+87) = 15*147 = 2,205. Step 4: 2,205 is more than 2,000 → K.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t05_q02
What is the missing number in the sequence 1, 5, 10, 16, 23, 31, __?
  • A. 37
  • B. 38
  • C. 39
  • D. 40
  • E. 41
Explanation: Find differences between consecutive terms: 5–1=4, 10–5=5, 16–10=6, 23–16=7, 31–23=8. The differences form an arithmetic sequence (+1 each time), so the next difference is +9: 31+9=40.
L1 act_master word_problem act_math_t05_q04
Noreen recently took a job helping people register to vote. The job has a mandatory 10-day period of probation during which her success rate is strictly monitored. On her first day, she registered 30 people. Then, for each of the next 9 days, she registered 4 more people than she did on the previous day. How many people did she register altogether during her probationary period?
  • A. 300
  • B. 340
  • C. 480
  • D. 560
  • E. 600
Explanation: Day 1: 30 people. For each of the next 9 days, she registered 4 more than the previous day (likely; stem truncated). Sequence: 30, 34, 38, 42, 46, 50, 54, 58, 62, 66. Sum = n/2·(a₁+aₙ) = 10/2·(30+66) = 5·96 = 480.
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1027
(Diagram: 2 arrows pointing to the right. + 7 in the first arrow. divide by 2 in the second arrow) The rule above is used to generate a term-to-term sequence. If the third term is 9 , what is the first term?
  • A. 15
  • B. 4
  • C. 11
  • D. 8
Explanation: The correct answer is 15. A common mistake here: Does not know the definition of term or term-to-term rule. Double-check each step in for a given output find the input of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the definition of term or term-to-term rule
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1029
How many terms are greater than zero in this sequence: 22-5 n
  • A. 22
  • B. 17
  • C. 5
  • D. 4
Explanation: The correct answer is 4. A common mistake here: Thinks the first term in a sequence is the term before the sequence begins (0th term). Double-check each step in generate a descending integer linear sequence from the nth term rule to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the first term in a sequence is the term before the sequence begins (0th term)
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1052
Here are two consecutive triangle numbers: 120,136, ... What is the next triangle number in the sequence?
  • A. 153
  • B. 256
  • C. 151
  • D. 152

⚠ Common trap: Believes to find the next traingle number you add the previous two numbers together i.e...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1097
What is the first term of the sequence given by this rule: 4 n+3
  • A. 4
  • B. 7
  • C. 3
  • D. 43

⚠ Common trap: When finding the nth term of a linear sequence, thinks the the first term is the coeffi...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1209
1,3,6,10,15,21,28 ... What is the name commonly given to this sequence of numbers?
  • A. The square numbers
  • B. The triangle numbers
  • C. The cube numbers
  • D. The Fibonacci numbers
Explanation: The correct answer is The triangle numbers. A common mistake here: Believes square numbers are triangle numbers. Double-check each step in identify triangular numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes square numbers are triangle numbers
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1258
7/6, 4/3, 3/2, 5/3, ... When calculating the nth-term rule of this sequence, what should replace the star? (Diagram: Text: "nth-term rule:" followed by a star, n and a purple rectangle.)
  • A. 2
  • B. 1
  • C. 4/3
  • D. 1/6
Explanation: The correct answer is 1/6. A common mistake here: When subtracting fractions, subtracts the numerators and denominators. Double-check each step in find the nth term rule for an ascending fractional linear sequence to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When subtracting fractions, subtracts the numerators and denominators
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1298
(Diagram: Two arrows in a line, both pointing right. The first one has '- 3' in it, the second one has 'x 5' in it.) The rule above is used to generate a term-to-term sequence. If the third term is 35 , what is the first term?
  • A. 10
  • B. -10
  • C. 5
  • D. 1
Explanation: The correct answer is 5. A common mistake here: When asked for a specific term in a sequence gives the term after. Double-check each step in for a given output find the input of a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When asked for a specific term in a sequence gives the term after
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1323
Which of the following term-to-term rule describes this sequence? 2, 5, 14, 41 ...
  • A. To get the next term you add 3 to the previous term
  • B. To get the next term you double the previous term and add 1
  • C. To get the next term you square the previous term and add 1
  • D. To get the next term you multiply the previous term by 3 and subtract 1
Explanation: The correct answer is To get the next term you multiply the previous term by 3 and subtract 1. A common mistake here: Uses only the first two terms of a sequence to work out a term-to-term rule. Double-check each step in describe a term-to-term sequence to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Uses only the first two terms of a sequence to work out a term-to-term rule
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1333

  • D. To get the next term you multiply the previous term by 5 and subtract 1

  • B. another triangle number

L2 eedi diagram eedi_1451
Tom and Katie are discussing linear sequences Tom says this is a linear sequence: (Diagram: Three shapes made of squares. The first is an L-shape made of 3 squares so the bottom line is two squares long. The second shape is also an L but with a bottom line of three squares. The fourth shape is the same but the bottom line is four squares long.) Katie says this is a linear sequence: (Diagram: Three shapes made of squares. The first is an L-shape made of 3 squares. The second shape is an L made of 5 squares, two on the top row and three on the bottom row. The third shape is an L made of 7 squares, 3 on the top row and four on the bottom.) Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount. Double-check each step in recognise that a sequence is linear sequences when expressed as a pattern to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1476
Which of the following is the best way to describe this sequence: (Diagram: A pattern with 6 terms made of green and red dots. The first term is a green dot, the second a red dot, the third is one red and one green dot. The fourth term is made of one green and two red dots. The fifth term is made from two green dots and three red dots. The sixth term is made of five red dots and three green dots.)
  • A. Linear
  • B. Quadratic
  • C. Geometric
  • D. Fibonacci
Explanation: The correct answer is Fibonacci. A common mistake here: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount. Double-check each step in recognise a fibonacci sequence expressed as a pattern to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount

Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount. Double-check each step in recognise an arithmetic sequence to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_500
Which statement correctly describes this sequence? 7,2,-3, ...
  • A. The first term is 7 and the term-to-term rule is +5
  • B. The first term is 7 and the position-to-term rule is -5
  • C. The first term is 7 and the term-to-term rule is -5
  • D. The first term is 12 and the position-to-term rule is -5
Explanation: The correct answer is The first term is 7 and the term-to-term rule is -5. A common mistake here: Goes the wrong direction in the sequence when identifying term-to-term rule. Double-check each step in describe a descending linear sequence in words to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Goes the wrong direction in the sequence when identifying term-to-term rule
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_581
1,8,27,64, ... What is the name given to this sequence of numbers?
  • A. The square numbers
  • B. The odd numbers
  • C. The cube numbers
  • D. The triangular numbers
Explanation: The correct answer is The cube numbers. A common mistake here: Does not know what a square number is. Double-check each step in recognise cube numbers to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know what a square number is
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_608
Tom and Katie are discussing Fibonacci sequences. Tom says this is a Fibonacci sequence: 1,1,2,3,5,8, ... Katie says this is a Fibonacci sequence: 1,2,3,5,8,13, ... Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Thinks a Fibonacci sequence must start with 1, 1. Double-check each step in recognise a fibonacci sequence to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks a Fibonacci sequence must start with 1, 1
L2 eedi diagram eedi_616
Without drawing any more terms, how many circles do you predict will be in the 5 th term of this sequence? (Diagram: A diagram showing the first 3 patterns in a sequence. Pattern 1 is a rectangle with a total of 6 circles surrounding it. Pattern 2 is 2 rectangles with a total of 10 circles surrounding it. Pattern 3 is 3 rectangles with a total of 14 circles surrounding it.)
  • A. 30
  • B. 18
  • C. 24
  • D. 22
Explanation: The correct answer is 22. A common mistake here: Does not notice all changes in a sequence of visual patterns. Double-check each step in use a linear sequence expressed as a pattern to make a prediction about another term in the sequence other than the next one to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not notice all changes in a sequence of visual patterns
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_652
Which of the following term-to-term rule describes this sequence? 4, 10, 28, 82, ...
  • A. To get the next term you add 6 to the previous term
  • B. To get the next term you add the previous two terms together
  • C. To get the next term you double the previous term and add 2
  • D. To get the next term you multiply the previous term by 3 and subtract 2
Explanation: The correct answer is To get the next term you multiply the previous term by 3 and subtract 2. A common mistake here: Uses only the first two terms of a sequence to work out a term-to-term rule. Double-check each step in describe a term-to-term sequence to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Uses only the first two terms of a sequence to work out a term-to-term rule
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_690
Tom and Katie are discussing linear sequences. Tom says this is a linear sequence: 1,2,4,7,11, ... Katie says this is a linear sequence: 1.2,1.4,1.6,1.8,2, ... Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount. Double-check each step in recognise when an ascending sequence is linear to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount
L2 eedi diagram eedi_701
If the original sequence looks like this: (Diagram: Two patterns made of squares. Pattern 1 consists of a central column of three squares, with an extra square either side, making a cross. Pattern two consists of two central columns of three squares with one extra square either side.) There are 302 squares in Pattern 100 . But if the shape changes to this: (Diagram: These patterns are the same as those in the first picture except that in each, the second block from each column of three has been removed.) How many squares would there be in Pattern 100 now?
  • A. 301
  • B. 300
  • C. 299
  • D. 202
Explanation: The correct answer is 202. A common mistake here: Only looks at the difference between one pair of terms when discerning the pattern. Double-check each step in use a linear sequence expressed as a pattern to make a prediction about another term in the sequence other than the next one to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Only looks at the difference between one pair of terms when discerning the pattern
L2 eedi diagram eedi_727
If I put the numbers 4 and 9 into this function machine, what comes out? (Diagram: Flow chart starting with instruction "In" inside a blue rimmed circle, then "+ 5" inside a red rimmed square, then "double" inside a red rimmed square and lastly, "Out" inside a blue rimmed circle.)
  • A. 9 and 14
  • B. 18 and 28
  • C. 81 and 196
  • D. -3 and -0.5
Explanation: The correct answer is 18 and 28. A common mistake here: Mixes up squaring and multiplying by 2 or doubling. Double-check each step in solve a linear equation using a function machine to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up squaring and multiplying by 2 or doubling
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_85
What is the first term of the sequence given by this rule: 2-7 n
  • A. 1
  • B. -7
  • C. -5
  • D. 9
Explanation: The correct answer is -5. A common mistake here: Thinks the first term of a sequence must be 1. Double-check each step in generate a descending integer linear sequence from the nth term rule to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the first term of a sequence must be 1
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_857
What is the next term of this linear sequence? 3,9,15,21, ...
  • A. 22
  • B. 27
  • C. 24
  • D. 28
Explanation: The correct answer is 27. A common mistake here: When asked for the next term in the sequence adds one to the last term given, despite the sequence not going up in ones. Double-check each step in write the next term of an ascending integer linear sequence to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When asked for the next term in the sequence adds one to the last term given, despite t...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_89
What is the first term of the sequence given by this rule: 1-5 n
  • A. 1
  • B. -5
  • C. 6
  • D. -4
Explanation: The correct answer is -4. A common mistake here: Thinks the first term of a sequence must be 1. Double-check each step in generate a descending integer linear sequence from the nth term rule to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the first term of a sequence must be 1
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1159
(Diagram: Square and then subtract 4 ) The first three terms of which sequence below is produced by this term-to-term rule?
  • A. -3,9,5
  • B. -3,-13,-173
  • C. -3,5,21
  • D. -3,0,5
Explanation: The correct answer is -3,5,21. A common mistake here: When given a term to term sequence with multiple steps, applies one step per term. Double-check each step in given a description of an ascending sequence using quadratics in words, write some terms of that sequence to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When given a term to term sequence with multiple steps, applies one step per term
L3 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1434

  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct

⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise that a linear sequence must increase or decrease by same amount
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1741
Which of the following is the best way to describe this sequence: (Diagram: First three patterns in a sequence. They are all rectangles made up of small squares. Pattern 1 is three small squares on top of one another, so the dimensions of the rectangle are 1 across and 3 up. Pattern 2 is a rectangle of dimensions 2 squares across and 6 up. Pattern 3 is a rectangle of dimensions 3 squares across and 9 squares up.)

  • B. Quadratic

  • B. 5,19

  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie

Systems of Linear Equations

core · 25 questions · systems_of_linear_equations
L1 act_master diagram act_math_t02_q05
If you graph the lines 3 x + 2 y = –2 and 5 x – y = 14 on a standard xy- graph, at which point will the lines intersect?
  • A. (1, –7)
  • B. (2, –4)
  • C. (–5, 4)
  • D. (–9, 3)
  • E. (11, 2)
Explanation: Systems of Linear Equations → two graphed lines intersect where both equations are true → solve 3x+2y=-2 and 5x-y=14 → x=2, y=-4 → intersection is (2,-4).
L1 act_master word_problem act_math_t09_q01
At a convenience store, two candy bars and two bags of potato chips cost $4.00, and three candy bars and two bags of potato chips cost $4.75. What is the price of one bag of potato chips?
  • A. $0.50
  • B. $0.75
  • C. $1.00
  • D. $1.25
  • E. $1.50
Explanation: 2c+2p=4.00 ... (1). 3c+2p=4.75 ... (2). Subtract (1) from (2): c=0.75. Substitute: 2(0.75)+2p=4 → 2p=2.50 → p=$1.25.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t10_q02
If 2 x – y = 32 and 5 x + 3 y = 14, then xy =
  • A. 35
  • B. –40
  • C. 75
  • D. 100
  • E. –120
Explanation: 2x–y=32 ... (1). 5x+3y=14 ... (2). From (1): y=2x–32. Substitute into (2): 5x+3(2x–32)=14 → 11x=110 → x=10. y=2(10)–32=–12. xy=10×(–12)=–120.
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t11_q02
Alfred and Rani both picked different two-digit numbers. If you multiply Alfred’s number by 5 and double Rani’s number, the sum is 300. If you double Alfred’s number and multiply Rani’s number by 3, the sum of the two numbers is 252. What is the sum of their two numbers?
  • A. 96
  • B. 112
  • C. 128
  • D. 144
  • E. 150
Explanation: Step 1: Let a = Alfred's number, r = Rani's number. Step 2: Equation 1: 5a + 2r = 300. Step 3: Equation 2: 2a + 3r = 252. Step 4: Eliminate r — multiply Eq1 by 3: 15a + 6r = 900. Multiply Eq2 by 2: 4a + 6r = 504. Subtract: 11a = 396 → a = 36. Step 5: Substitute a=36 into Eq1: 5(36) + 2r = 300 → 2r = 120 → r = 60. Step 6: Sum = a + r = 36 + 60 = 96. Answer: F. 96.
L1 inline_ts sy-1-2
Given the system 2x + y = 7 and y = 3, what is x?
Explanation: Substitute y = 3 into first equation: 2x + 3 = 7 → 2x = 4 → x = 2.
L1 inline_ts sy-1-3
If 3x − 2y = 8 and x = 0, what is y?
Explanation: Substitute x = 0: −2y = 8 → y = −4.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t14_q07
If a + 3 b = 27 and a - 3 b = 9, then b = ?
  • A. 3
  • B. 9
  • C. 14
  • D. 18
  • E. 36
Explanation: Step 1: System: a+3b=27 and a-3b=9. Step 2: Subtract equations: (a+3b)-(a-3b)=27-9 → 6b=18 → b=3 → F.
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1033
Chris orders 3 cups of tea and a cake, and his bill comes to $3.10 Amy orders 5 cups of tea and a cake, and her bill comes to $ 4.50 . How much does one cup of tea cost?
  • A. 70 p
  • B. 90 p
  • C. E1
  • D. $ 1.40
Explanation: The correct answer is 70 p. A common mistake here: When solving simultaneous equations, thinks they can ignore one of the variables. Double-check each step in solve basic introduction simultaneous equation problems, often involving shapes and symbols to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When solving simultaneous equations, thinks they can ignore one of the variables
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_1202
Nick has almost solved a pair of simultaneous equations. He just needs to find the value of y . He writes down: 3-y=5 What is the correct value of y ?
  • A. y=2
  • B. y=-2
  • C. y=8
  • D. y=-8
Explanation: The correct answer is y=-2. A common mistake here: When solving an equation where there is a negative sign in front of the variable at the final stage, believes they can ignore it and treat it as if it's positive. Double-check each step in substitute a solution back into the remaining equation and find the second solution to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When solving an equation where there is a negative sign in front of the variable at the...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_534
Isaac is trying to solve the pair of simultaneous equations on the right. 6 g+h=12 6 g-3 h=-3 He decides to subtract the bottom equation from the top equation. What will he end up with?
  • A. -2 h=9
  • B. -2 h=15
  • C. 4 h=9
  • D. 4 h=15
Explanation: The correct answer is 4 h=15. A common mistake here: When subtracting a negative number, subtracts the absolute value rather than adding it. Double-check each step in subtract two equations together to remove a variable to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When subtracting a negative number, subtracts the absolute value rather than adding it
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_600
Chris orders 2 cups of tea and a cake, and his bill comes to $1.40 Amy orders 4 cups of tea and a cake, and her bill comes to $2.20. How much does one cup of tea cost?
  • A. 80 p
  • B. 60 p
  • C. 40 p
  • D. 55 p
Explanation: The correct answer is 40 p. A common mistake here: When solving an equation forgets to eliminate the coefficient in front of the variable in the last step. Double-check each step in solve basic introduction simultaneous equation problems, often involving shapes and symbols to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When solving an equation forgets to eliminate the coefficient in front of the variable...
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_935
Isaac is halfway through solving a pair of simultaneous equations, one of which is below. 2 j-k=-10 Isaac found that k=8 What does j equal?
  • A. j=1
  • B. j=9
  • C. j=-9
  • D. j=-1
Explanation: The correct answer is j=-1. A common mistake here: Believes dividing a negative by a positive gives a positive answer. Double-check each step in substitute a solution back into the remaining equation and find the second solution to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes dividing a negative by a positive gives a positive answer
L2 inline_ts sy-2-2
Tickets cost $5 for students and $8 for adults. 100 tickets sold for $620 total. How many student tickets?
Explanation: s + a = 100 and 5s + 8a = 620. Substitute s = 100−a: 5(100−a) + 8a = 620 → 3a = 120 → a = 40 → s = 60.
L2 inline_ts sy-2-3
The system 2x + ky = 6 and 4x + 8y = 12 has infinitely many solutions. What is k?
Explanation: Infinite solutions: equations must be proportional. Multiply first by 2: 4x + 2ky = 12. Compare: 2k = 8 → k = 4.
L3 act_master word_problem act_math_t02_q12
Anne and Katherine are both saving money from their summer jobs to buy bicycles. If Anne had $150 less, she would have exactly as much as Katherine. And if Katherine had twice as much, she would have exactly 3 times as much as Anne. How much money have they saved together?
  • A. $300
  • B. $400
  • C. $450
  • D. $625
  • E. $750
Explanation: Systems → let Anne=A and Katherine=K → Akshat explanation uses A-150 = K/3 and 2K = 3A → solve to get A=300 and K=450 → total = 750.
L3 act_master word_problem act_math_t03_q14
Andrea and Zach are both waiting for an appointment with a guidance counselor. When they arrived, each received a card from the secretary, telling the hour and minute of his or her arrival. Two minutes ago, Andrea had been waiting exactly as many minutes as Zach. Three minutes from now, Andrea will have been waiting exactly as long as Zach. If the time is now 11:30, at what time did Andrea arrive?
  • A. 11:16
  • B. 11:18
  • C. 11:20
  • D. 11:23
  • E. 11:25
Explanation: Step 1: Let A = Andrea's current wait time (minutes, as of 11:30) and Z = Zach's. Step 2: 'Two minutes ago, Andrea had waited half as long as Zach': A–2 = (1/2)(Z–2). Step 3: 'Three minutes from now, Andrea will have waited 2/3 as long as Zach': A+3 = (2/3)(Z+3). Step 4: From the first equation, Z = 2A–2. Substitute into the second: A+3 = (2/3)(2A–2+3) = (2/3)(2A+1) → 3A+9 = 4A+2 → A = 7. Step 5: …
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t07_q12
If 7 x + 4 y = 18 and 3 x + y = –3, what is the value of x + y ?
  • A. 9
  • B. 11
  • C. 12
  • D. 14
  • E. 15
Explanation: 7x+4y=18 ... (1). 3x+y=-3 ... (2). From (2): y=-3-3x. Substitute into (1): 7x+4(-3-3x)=18 → 7x-12-12x=18 → -5x=30 → x=-6. y=-3-3(-6)=-3+18=15. x+y=-6+15=9.
L3 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_1025
According to the graph, what are the solutions to the simultaneous equations? (Diagram: The image is of a set of axes with two straight lines drawn on. The red line goes through the points (0,6) and (3,0). The blue line goes through the points (0,3) and (6,0)and the two lines cross at (2,2).)
  • A. x=3, y=6
  • B. x=6, y=3
  • C. x=2, y=2
  • D. x=2, y=1
Explanation: The correct answer is x=2, y=2. A common mistake here: When solving simultaneous equations graphically, they think the solutions are the y-intercepts. Double-check each step in read the graphical solution to a pair of linear simultaneous equations from a diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When solving simultaneous equations graphically, they think the solutions are the y-int...
L3 eedi word_problem eedi_1336
Tom and Katie are discussing how to get this pair of simultaneous equations in a form where they can eliminate y ( 1) -2 x+6 y=4 (2) x+2 y=8 Tom says you need to multiply equation (2) by 3 . Katie says you need to multiply equation (1) by 2 and multiply equation (2) by 6 . Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: When solving simultaneous equations, thinks they can't multiply each equation by a different number. Double-check each step in manipulate both equations so that they are in a form to be added or subtracted to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When solving simultaneous equations, thinks they can't multiply each equation by a diff...
L3 eedi diagram eedi_579
The cost of journeys from two taxi companies is shown on the diagram. After how many miles is Company P more expensive than Company Q? (Diagram: A graph showing Cost ($) on the y axis and Distance (miles) on the x axis. Y axis from 0 to 30, each square goes up by 5, and x axis from 0 to 6, each square goes up by 1. A blue line slopes upwards from left to right, from (0, 10). It is labelled 'Company Q'. A red line slopes upwards from left to right, from (0,0). It is labelled 'Company P'. Both lines go through (5, 20))
  • A. 10
  • B. 6
  • C. 5
  • D. 20
Explanation: The correct answer is 5. A common mistake here: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes. Double-check each step in interpret the graphical solution to a pair of linear simultaneous equations from a diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: When reading value from graph, reads from the wrong axes
L3 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_862
This is a graph of y=4 x-1 (Diagram: A set of axes with y=4x-1 drawn on.) Use the graph to solve 4 x-1=-3
  • A. x=0.25
  • B. x=-3
  • C. x=-0.5
  • D. No solutions
Explanation: The correct answer is x=-0.5. A common mistake here: Believes the solution to mx + c = a is the x intercept of y = mx +c. Double-check each step in given a sketch of a linear graph solve f(x) = n to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the solution to mx + c = a is the x intercept of y = mx +c
L3 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_908
This is a graph of y=1-0.4 x (Diagram: A set of axes with y=1-0.4x drawn on.) Use the graph to solve 1-0.4 x=0
  • A. x=2.5
  • B. x=0
  • C. x=1
  • D. x=-0.4
Explanation: The correct answer is x=2.5. A common mistake here: Believes the solution of mx + c = 0 is x =0. Double-check each step in given a sketch of a linear graph solve f(x) = 0 to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the solution of mx + c = 0 is x =0
L3 eedi coordinate_graph eedi_98
This is a graph of y=0.4 x-3 (Diagram: A set of axes with the graph y=0.4x-3 drawn on.) Use the graph to solve 0.4 x-3=-1
  • A. x=-3.3
  • B. x=7.5
  • C. x=-1
  • D. x=5
Explanation: The correct answer is x=5. A common mistake here: Mixes up the value of two terms when substituting. Double-check each step in given a sketch of a linear graph solve f(x) = n to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Mixes up the value of two terms when substituting
L3 inline_ts sy-3-2
A chemist mixes a 20% and 50% acid solution to make 90 mL of 30% solution. How many mL of 50% solution are needed?
Explanation: x + y = 90 and 0.2x + 0.5y = 27. Substitute x = 90−y: 18 + 0.3y = 27 → y = 30 mL.
L3 inline_ts sy-3-3
The sum of the digits of a 2-digit number is 9. Reversing the digits gives a number 27 more than the original. What is the original number?
Explanation: Let tens = t, units = u. t+u=9 and (10u+t)−(10t+u)=27 → 9(u−t)=27 → u−t=3. Solve: u=6, t=3. Number = 36.

Triangles and Congruence

core · 42 questions · triangles_congruence
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1153
Tom and Katie are arguing about triangles. Tom says a triangle can have two right angles. Katie says only a scalene triangle can have a right angle. Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Does not consider that all three angles in a triangle sum to 180 degrees. Double-check each step in find missing angles in a right-angled triangle to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not consider that all three angles in a triangle sum to 180 degrees
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1212
Tom and Katie are discussing this shape Tom says it is a rectangle Katie says it is a parallelogram Who do you agree with? (Diagram: Purple parallelogram with parallel sides labelled)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Does not recognise a parallelogram. Double-check each step in sort and class quadrilaterals into hierarchical families to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise a parallelogram
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1226
Which shape has the highest order of rotational symmetry?
  • A. ![Equilateral triangle]()
  • B. ![Circle]()
  • C. ![Square]()
  • D. ![Hexagon]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![Circle](). A common mistake here: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry. Double-check each step in identify the order of rotational symmetry of a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1256
The arrows tell us that these two sides are... (Diagram: Parallelogram PQRS with sides QR and PS labelled with arrows)
  • A. The same length
  • B. Opposite
  • C. Parallel
  • D. Equilateral
Explanation: The correct answer is Parallel. A common mistake here: Believes the arrows for parallel lines mean equal length. Double-check each step in recognise the notation for parallel lines to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes the arrows for parallel lines mean equal length
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1303
An isosceles triangle must have ...
  • A. no equal side lengths.
  • B. two sides of equal length and two equal angles.
  • C. one pair of parallel sides.
  • D. three equal angles.
Explanation: The correct answer is two sides of equal length and two equal angles.. A common mistake here: Confuses isosceles and scalene triangles. Double-check each step in describe the properties of an isosceles triangle to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses isosceles and scalene triangles
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1364
A B C D is a rhombus. Which lines must be perpendicular? (Diagram: Parallelogram with the vertices labelled A, B, C and D)
  • A. A B and B C
  • B. A B and D C
  • C. A C and D B
  • D. None of them
Explanation: The correct answer is A C and D B. A common mistake here: Believes adjacent sides of a rhombus are always perpendicular. Double-check each step in describe the properties of a rhombus to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes adjacent sides of a rhombus are always perpendicular
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1421
What is the size of angle p ? (Diagram: A triangle with angles labelled 49 degrees, 51 degrees and p [not to scale)]()
  • A. 51^°
  • B. 70^°
  • C. 80^°
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 80^°. A common mistake here: Thinks angles which look the same on diagram will be equal. Double-check each step in find missing angles in a scalene triangle to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks angles which look the same on diagram will be equal
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1553
This is an isosceles triangle. Calculate the size of angle p . (Diagram: An isosceles triangle with one angle labelled 56 degrees and one angle labelled p [not to scale)]()
  • A. 56^°
  • B. 68^°
  • C. 124^°
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is Not enough information. A common mistake here: Believes there is only one orientation for the isosceles triangle base angles. Double-check each step in find missing angles in an isosceles triangle to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes there is only one orientation for the isosceles triangle base angles
L1 eedi word_problem eedi_1558
Tom and Katie have been asked to give the name of a quadrilateral with four equal sides Tom says rectangle Katie says rhombus Who do you agree with?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Believes a rectangle has four equal sides. Double-check each step in identify a rhombus from a description of the properties to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes a rectangle has four equal sides
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1588
Which shape has the lowest order of rotational symmetry?
  • A. ![Equilateral triangle]()
  • B. ![Circle]()
  • C. ![Square]()
  • D. ![Trapezium]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![Trapezium](). A common mistake here: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry. Double-check each step in identify the order of rotational symmetry of a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know how to find order of rotational symmetry
L1 eedi diagram eedi_1745
Triangle A B C is an equilateral triangle. Find length AC. (Diagram: A triangle with vertices labelled A, B, C. Side BC is 8cm, side AC is labelled with a question mark.)
  • A. 7 cm
  • B. 90 cm
  • C. 8 cm
  • D. 60 cm
Explanation: The correct answer is 8 cm. A common mistake here: Does not know the meaning of equilateral. Double-check each step in use the properties of an equilateral triangle to find missing lengths to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the meaning of equilateral
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1832
An isosceles triangle must have ...
  • A. three 60^° angles.
  • B. a right angle.
  • C. three equal side lengths.
  • D. two sides of equal length and two equal angles.
Explanation: The correct answer is two sides of equal length and two equal angles.. A common mistake here: Confuses isosceles and equilateral triangles. Double-check each step in describe the properties of an isosceles triangle to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses isosceles and equilateral triangles
L1 eedi diagram eedi_282
A B C D is a rhombus. Which lines must be perpendicular? (Diagram: Tilted rhombus with top left vertex labelled A, top right vertex labelled B, bottom left vertex labelled D and bottom right vertex labelled C.)
  • A. A B and B C
  • B. A C and D B
  • C. A B and D C
  • D. None of them
Explanation: The correct answer is A C and D B. A common mistake here: Believes adjacent sides of a rhombus are always perpendicular. Double-check each step in describe the properties of a rhombus to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes adjacent sides of a rhombus are always perpendicular
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_342
Which of the following is an obtuse angle?
  • A. 85^°
  • B. 270^°
  • C. 110^°
  • D. 180^°
Explanation: The correct answer is 110^°. A common mistake here: Does not understand that an obtuse angle is between 90 and 180 degrees. Double-check each step in identify obtuse angles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that an obtuse angle is between 90 and 180 degrees
L1 eedi diagram eedi_44
A B C D is a rectangle. What type of triangle is triangle BCD ? (Diagram: Rectangle ABCD with the diagonal shown using a red dotted line)
  • A. Scalene
  • B. Isosceles
  • C. Right-angled
  • D. Equilateral

⚠ Common trap: Confuses scalene and right angle triangles
L1 eedi diagram eedi_53
In which region would a rectangle belong? (Diagram: Venn diagram with two circles labelled with 'exactly two lines of symmetry' and 'rotational symmetry of order 2'. A is in the only exactly two lines of symmetry region, B is in the intersection, C is in the only rotational symmetry of order 2 region and D is outside the two circles.)
  • A. A
  • B. B
  • C. C
  • D. D
Explanation: The correct answer is B. A common mistake here: Thinks rectangles have rotational symmetry or order 4. Double-check each step in identify the order of rotational symmetry of a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks rectangles have rotational symmetry or order 4
L1 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_650
Which description describes an isosceles triangle?
  • A. A triangle with one right angle.
  • B. A triangle with two sides of equal length and two equal angles.
  • C. A triangle with three sides of equal length and three equal angles.
  • D. A triangle with no sides of equal length and no equal angles.
Explanation: The correct answer is A triangle with two sides of equal length and two equal angles.. A common mistake here: Confuses isosceles and right angle triangles. Double-check each step in describe the properties of an isosceles triangle to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses isosceles and right angle triangles

In which region would an isosceles triangle belong? (Diagram: Venn diagram with two circles labelled with 'exactly one line of symmetry' and 'rotational symmetry of order 3'.   • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct

L2 act_master diagram act_math_t01_q08
In the following figure, the two triangles are similar. The lengths of their sides are shown. What is the value of y in terms of x ?
  • A. x + 1
  • B. x + 2
  • C. x + 3
  • D. x + 4
  • E. x + 5
Explanation: Step 1: Identify the two similar triangles: small has sides {2, 3, x}, large has sides {x, 6, y}. Step 2: Match corresponding sides by position. Side 3 (small) corresponds to 6 (large), giving scale ratio = 6÷3 = 2. Step 3: Apply ratio to the next pair: 2 (small) corresponds to x (large) → x = 2×2 = 4. Step 4: Apply ratio to third pair: x (small) corresponds to y (large) → y = 2×x = 2(4) = 8. Step…
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1060
The dashes on the sides tell us that this is a __________ polygon What word completes the sentence? (Diagram: A shape with five equal sides. Each side has a small dash through the middle.)
  • A. Parallel
  • B. Pentagon
  • C. Irregular
  • D. Regular
Explanation: The correct answer is Regular. A common mistake here: Does not know the meaning of the word parallel. Double-check each step in understand the terms equilateral and regular, and recognise the notation for regular polygons to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the meaning of the word parallel
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_111
Which quadrilateral below is a regular polygon?
  • A. ![Trapezium]()
  • B. ![Rhombus]()
  • C. ![Rectangle]()
  • D. ![Square]()
Explanation: The correct answer is ![Square](). A common mistake here: Does not know the meaning of the word regular . Double-check each step in understand the terms equilateral and regular, and recognise the notation for regular polygons to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know the meaning of the word regular
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1143
If you continue to join up the intersections of the curves with straight lines, which of the following regular polygons could you have constructed? (Diagram: A circle has been intersected by curves at 5 equally spaced intervals about its circumference)
  • A. An octagon
  • B. A hexagon
  • C. A pentagon
  • D. A circle
Explanation: The correct answer is A pentagon. A common mistake here: Confuses octagon and pentagon. Double-check each step in given the number of sides of a polygon identify the name to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses octagon and pentagon
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1276
Which of the combinations of properties are not possible for a polygon?
  • A. Rotation symmetry order 3 and 0 line of symmetry
  • B. Rotation symmetry order 1 and 2 lines of symmetry
  • C. Rotation symmetry order 4 and 4 lines of symmetry
  • D. Rotation symmetry order 2 and 0 lines of symmetry
Explanation: The correct answer is Rotation symmetry order 1 and 2 lines of symmetry. A common mistake here: Believes if shapes have rotational symmetry they must have line symmetry. Double-check each step in identify or mark lines of symmetry on a shape to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes if shapes have rotational symmetry they must have line symmetry
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1280
A ten sided polygon is also known as...
  • A. An octagon
  • B. A hexagon
  • C. A decagon
  • D. A dodecagon
Explanation: The correct answer is A decagon. A common mistake here: Does not understand the naming structure of polygons. Double-check each step in given the number of sides of a polygon identify the name to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand the naming structure of polygons
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_133
Tom and Katie are discussing how to calculate the size of each interior angle in a regular 20 sided shape Tom says you can do 18 × 180/20 Katie says you can do 180-360/20 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not recognise that interior and exterior angles are supplementary. Double-check each step in work out the size of each interior angle in a regular polygon to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise that interior and exterior angles are supplementary
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_1334
Which of the following shapes is a hexagon?
  • A. ![An irregular, five sided shape]()
  • B. ![A regular, five sided shape]()
  • C. ![A regular, eight sided shape]()
  • D. None of these
Explanation: The correct answer is None of these. A common mistake here: Confuses pentagon and hexagon. Double-check each step in given the name of a polygon, draw or identify a corresponding diagram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Confuses pentagon and hexagon
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1559
Tom and Katie are discussing congruent shapes Tom says these two shapes are congruent (Diagram: Two identical 6-sided "L shape" polygons. One of the polygons has been rotated through 90 degrees.) Katie says these two shapes are congruent (Diagram: Two 6-sided "L shape" polygons. One of the polygons has a wider upright section than the other.) Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Thinks congruent shapes are the same shape but can be different sizes. Double-check each step in identify congruence in shapes other than triangles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks congruent shapes are the same shape but can be different sizes
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1651
Tom and Katie are arguing about parallelograms. Tom says this shape is a parallelogram (Diagram: A four sided shape. All sides are equal, opposite angles are equal. There are no right angles.) Katie says this shape is a parallelogram (Diagram: A four sided shape. Opposite sides are equal, all angles are right angles.) Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not know a rectangle is a type of parallelogram . Double-check each step in sort and class quadrilaterals into hierarchical families to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not know a rectangle is a type of parallelogram
L2 eedi diagram eedi_1867
Tom and Katie are discussing congruence and similarity. Who is correct? Tom says these two squares are congruent (Diagram: An image of two squares. A little one and a big one. They are enlargements of each other.) Katie says these two squares are similar (Diagram: An image of two squares. A little one and a big one. They are enlargements of each other.)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Katie. A common mistake here: Thinks congruent shapes are the same shape but can be different sizes. Double-check each step in distinguish between congruency and similarity to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks congruent shapes are the same shape but can be different sizes
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_324
Which of the following quadrilaterals could not be classified as a kite?
  • A. Square
  • B. Rhombus
  • C. Trapezium
  • D. None of them are kites
Explanation: The correct answer is Trapezium. A common mistake here: Does not recognise the properties of a kite. Double-check each step in identify a kite from a description of the properties to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not recognise the properties of a kite
L2 eedi diagram eedi_518
The octagon below is regular. Find angle x . (Diagram: A regular octagon (8 sided polygon). Each side is marked with a single dash to show that all sides are equal in length. One of the interior angles of the octagon is labelled with the letter "x".)
  • A. 22.5^°
  • B. 120^°
  • C. 135^°
  • D. 45^°
Explanation: The correct answer is 135^°. A common mistake here: Thinks the interior angles of any polygon add up to 180 degrees. Double-check each step in work out the size of each interior angle in a regular polygon to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks the interior angles of any polygon add up to 180 degrees
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_559
An equilateral shape must have...
  • A. All sides equal
  • B. All sides and all angles equal
  • C. A right angle
  • D. All angles equal
Explanation: The correct answer is All sides equal. A common mistake here: Does know know equilateral means the same as regular . Double-check each step in understand the terms equilateral and regular, and recognise the notation for regular polygons to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does know know equilateral means the same as regular
L2 eedi diagram eedi_589
What is the size of angle s ? (Diagram: 4 angles on an irregular quadrilateral these are labelled s, 2s, 2s, and 3s)
  • A. 45^°
  • B. 51.4^°
  • C. 60^°
  • D. Not enough information
Explanation: The correct answer is 45^°. A common mistake here: Does not think that a variable with no coefficient has a coefficient of 1. Double-check each step in use facts about interior angles in polygons with algebra to write expressions to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not think that a variable with no coefficient has a coefficient of 1
L2 eedi word_problem eedi_659
Tom and Katie are discussing how to calculate the size of each interior angle in a regular 18 sided shape Tom says you can do 20 × 180/18 Katie says you can do 360-180/18 Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Neither is correct. A common mistake here: Believes an interior angle in a regular polygon can be found using 180(n+2)/n. Double-check each step in work out the size of each interior angle in a regular polygon to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Believes an interior angle in a regular polygon can be found using 180(n+2)/n
L2 eedi symbolic_expression eedi_665
Tom and Katie are discussing congruent shapes. Tom says you can reflect a shape and it will still be congruent to the original. Katie says you can rotate a shape and it will still be congruent to the original. Who is correct?
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Both Tom and Katie. A common mistake here: Does not understand that shapes are congruent if they have the same size and shape. Double-check each step in understand the meaning of congruency to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that shapes are congruent if they have the same size and shape
L2 eedi diagram eedi_703
What is the size of angle p in this quadrilateral? (Diagram: Parallelogram showing 65 degrees in bottom left angles, and missing angle p, in top left)
  • A. 65^°
  • B. 125^°
  • C. 115^°
  • D. Not enough information to work it out
Explanation: The correct answer is 115^°. A common mistake here: Thinks co-interior angles are equal. Double-check each step in find missing angles in a parallelogram to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks co-interior angles are equal
L2 eedi diagram eedi_843
Tom and Katie are discussing congruent shapes. Who is correct? Tom says these two shapes are congruent (Diagram: An image of two triangles. The triangles are the same shape and size but have different orientations.) Katie says these two shapes are congruent (Diagram: An image of two triangles. A big one and a small one.)
  • A. Only Tom
  • B. Only Katie
  • C. Both Tom and Katie
  • D. Neither is correct
Explanation: The correct answer is Only Tom. A common mistake here: Thinks congruent shapes are the same shape but can be different sizes. Double-check each step in identify congruence in shapes other than triangles to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Thinks congruent shapes are the same shape but can be different sizes
L3 eedi diagram eedi_1363
(Diagram: Two cuboids. Cuboid P has width 5cm, and cuboid Q has width 15cm.) Shape P and Shape Q are similar. What is the volume scale factor to get from Shape P to Shape Q ?
  • A. × 3
  • B. × 6
  • C. × 9
  • D. × 27
Explanation: The correct answer is × 27. A common mistake here: Does not understand that the volume of an enlarged shape is the volume of the original multiplied by the cube of the scale factor of the lengths. Double-check each step in find the volume scale factor of similar shapes given the length scale factor to avoid this error.
⚠ Common trap: Does not understand that the volume of an enlarged shape is the volume of the original...

Trigonometry Basics

core · 11 questions · trigonometry_basics
L1 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t08_q04
Which of the following is equal to sin x sec x ?
  • A. tan x
  • B. cot x
  • C. cos x tan x
  • D. cos x csc x
  • E. cot x csc x
Explanation: sin(x) · sec(x) = sin(x) · (1/cos(x)) = sin(x)/cos(x) = tan(x).
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t07_q07
A 25-foot ladder stands against a vertical wall at an angle of n degrees with the ground. If , how far is the base of the ladder from the wall?
  • A. 12
  • B. 13
  • C. 14
  • D. 15
  • E. 16
Explanation: 25-foot ladder against a vertical wall. The ladder, wall, and ground form a right triangle. If sin(n) = 4/5 (i.e., the vertical height reached is (4/5)(25)=20 ft): horizontal base = sqrt(25²-20²) = sqrt(625-400) = sqrt(225) = 15 ft. Equivalently: cos(n) = adjacent/hypotenuse = 15/25 = 3/5, so base = 25×(3/5) = 15.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t10_q08
In the following figure, sin θ =
  • A. E.
Explanation: Step 1: Identify the right triangle. The two legs are 2 and 5 (from the figure). Step 2: Compute hypotenuse: c = √(2²+5²) = √(4+25) = √29. Step 3: sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse = 2/√29. Step 4: Rationalize the denominator: 2/√29 = 2√29/29. Step 5: Answer: E. 2√29/29.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t11_q06
A scientist performs an experiment in which she measures four values two times each, with the following results:
  • A. 0.5
  • B. 1
  • C. π
  • D. 2 π
  • E. 4 π
Explanation: Step 1: Identify two consecutive peaks (or zeros, or troughs) on the graph. Step 2: The period = horizontal distance between consecutive identical points. Step 3: From the graph, consecutive peaks are 4π apart. Step 4: Period = 4π. Answer: K. 4π.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t11_q07
Which of the following is the amplitude of the function shown in the following graph?
  • A. 0.5
  • B. 1
  • C. Ï_x0080_
  • D. 2 Ï_x0080_
  • E. 4 Ï_x0080_
Explanation: Step 1: Amplitude = maximum y-value of the sinusoidal function (or half the peak-to-trough height). Step 2: The graph shows peaks at y = 0.5 and troughs at y = −0.5. Step 3: Amplitude = 0.5. Answer: A. 0.5.
L2 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t12_q06
Which of the following is equivalent to ?
  • A. 1
  • B. sin n
  • C. cos n
  • D. cot n
  • E. csc n
Explanation: Step 1: Convert to sin/cos: tan n=sin/cos, csc n=1/sin, sin n=sin, sec n=1/cos. Step 2: Numerator: (sin/cos)(1/sin)=1/cos. Step 3: Denominator: sin×(1/cos)=sin/cos. Step 4: Divide: (1/cos)/(sin/cos)=(1/cos)×(cos/sin)=1/sin=csc n → E.
L2 act_master diagram act_math_t15_q07
A rock band, The Young Sohcahtoans, is trying to design a t-shirt logo. The measurements they have chosen are represented on the figure below. The angle to the right of the logo "TYS" has a degree measure of 35Â°, and the side of the figure has a measure of 10 in. Which of the following expressions gives the measure, in inches, of the diagonal top side of the figure?
  • A. 10 tan 35Â°
  • B. 10 cos 35Â°
  • C. 10 sin 35Â°
  • D. E.
Explanation: Step 1: The figure shows a right triangle with a vertical side = 10 in and angle = 35° at the base. Step 2: The vertical side (10) is OPPOSITE the 35° angle. Step 3: The diagonal top side is the HYPOTENUSE. Step 4: sin(35°) = opposite/hypotenuse = 10/hyp → hyp = 10/sin(35°) → D.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t01_q13
In the following triangle, what is the value of sec n ?
  • A. √5
  • B. 2/√5
  • C. 4/√5
  • D. √5/2
  • E. 2√5
Explanation: Step 1: Identify angle n and label the triangle sides relative to n. Angle n is at the bottom-left. Adjacent side (base) = 4. Opposite side (vertical) = 2. Hypotenuse = 2√5. (Verify: 4² + 2² = 16+4=20 = (2√5)² ✓.) Step 2: sec(n) = 1/cos(n) = hypotenuse ÷ adjacent. Step 3: sec(n) = 2√5 ÷ 4 = √5/2. Step 4: Check: ≈ 1.118 ≥ 1 ✓ (valid for a real angle).
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t03_q12
If sin a = 7/8, what is the value of cos a?
  • A. K.
Explanation: Step 1: Use the Pythagorean identity: sin²a + cos²a = 1. Step 2: cos²a = 1 – sin²a = 1 – (7/8)² = 1 – 49/64 = 15/64. Step 3: cos a = √(15/64) = √15/8.
L3 act_master diagram act_math_t04_q11
The following figure shows the graph of . Which of the answer choices are the correct amplitude and period of this function?
  • A. amplitude = 5, period = π
  • B. amplitude = 5, period = 2 π
  • C. amplitude = 5, period = 4 π
  • D. amplitude = 10, period = 2 π
  • E. amplitude = 10, period = 4 π
Explanation: Step 1: The function is y = A·sin(Bx + C) + D. For y = 5·sin(x/2) (if that is the given form): A=5 so amplitude=5. B=1/2 so period = 2π/B = 2π/(1/2) = 4π. Step 2: Amplitude = |A| = 5; Period = 2π/|B| = 4π.
L3 act_master symbolic_expression act_math_t15_q15
Avi is trying to draw a map of his most recent bike ride. He chose to place Market Street on the x -axis and Broad Street on the y -axis. He rode 60 m at an angle of 60Â° relative to Market Street, then rode 100 m at an angle of 45Â° relative to Market Street, and finally rode 35 m directly north on Broad Street. How many meters north of Market Street did Avi ride?
  • A. 35
  • B. 115
  • C. 195
  • D. 50 + 30
  • E. 35 + 50 + 30
Explanation: Step 1: 60m at 60° from Market (x-axis): north component=60sin(60°)=60(√3/2)=30√3. Step 2: 100m at 45°: north component=100sin(45°)=100(√2/2)=50√2. Step 3: 35m directly north. Step 4: Total north=30√3+50√2+35=35+50√2+30√3 → E.

word_problems

· 9 questions · word_problems
L1 inline_ts wp-1-2
A taxi charges a $6 pickup fee plus $2 per mile. What is the cost of a 9-mile ride?
Explanation: Use total = pickup fee + rate × miles. The cost is 6 + 2(9) = 6 + 18 = $24.
L1 inline_ts wp-1-3
A class has 18 girls and 14 boys. How many students are in the class?
Explanation: The total number of students is the sum of the two groups: 18 + 14 = 32.
L1 inline_ts wp-1-4
Lena read 45 pages on Monday and 30 pages on Tuesday. If the book has 120 pages, how many pages remain?
Explanation: Lena read 45 + 30 = 75 pages. Pages remaining: 120 − 75 = 45.
L2 inline_ts wp-2-2
Tickets cost $12 for adults and $7 for students. A group buys 3 adult tickets and some student tickets for $64 total. How many student tickets are bought?
Explanation: Three adult tickets cost 3 × 12 = 36. The student tickets cost 64 − 36 = 28. At $7 each, 28 ÷ 7 = 4 student tickets.
L2 inline_ts wp-2-3
A recipe uses 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar. If a baker uses 12 cups of flour, how many cups of sugar are needed?
Explanation: The flour amount is multiplied by 4 because 3 × 4 = 12. Multiply sugar by the same factor: 2 × 4 = 8 cups.
L2 inline_ts wp-2-4
A phone plan costs $25 per month plus $0.10 per text after the first 100 texts. In one month, Sam sends 240 texts. What is the bill?
Explanation: Only texts after the first 100 are charged: 240 − 100 = 140. Text charge is 140 × $0.10 = $14. Total bill is $25 + $14 = $39.
L3 inline_ts wp-3-2
A car and a bus leave the same city at the same time in opposite directions. The car travels 58 mph and the bus travels 47 mph. After how many hours will they be 315 miles apart?
Explanation: Opposite directions means their distances add. Combined speed is 58 + 47 = 105 mph. Time = distance ÷ speed = 315 ÷ 105 = 3 hours.
L3 inline_ts wp-3-3
A school club sells candles for $8 and mugs for $11. They sell 37 items and collect $344. How many mugs did they sell?
Explanation: Let m be mugs, so candles = 37 − m. Revenue: 11m + 8(37 − m) = 344. Then 11m + 296 − 8m = 344, so 3m = 48 and m = 16.
L3 inline_ts wp-3-4
A rectangular garden is 6 feet longer than it is wide. If 60 feet of fencing surrounds the garden, what is the area?
Explanation: Let width = w, length = w + 6. Perimeter: 2w + 2(w+6) = 60, so 4w + 12 = 60 and w = 12. Length = 18. Area = 12 × 18 = 216 ft².
